Surah 62 (Al-Jumu`ah — "Friday") is eleven verses, Medinan. The surah's structure is three movements: (1) the gentile-messenger and the gift of scripture, (2) a polemic against those who received scripture but failed to bear it, (3) the Friday congregational prayer command.

**Movement I: the gentile messenger (62:1–4).**

[62:1]: "Glorifying GOD is everything in the heavens and everything on earth; the King, the Most Sacred, the Almighty, the Most Wise." — *yusabbiḥu* — present-tense glorification, ongoing. Four divine names in one verse: *al-Malik* (King), *al-Quddūs* (Most Sacred), *al-`Azīz* (Almighty), *al-Ḥakīm* (Most Wise).

[62:2]: "He is the One who sent to the gentiles a messenger from among them, to recite to them His revelations, purify them, and teach them the scripture and wisdom. Before this, they had gone far astray." — *al-ummiyyīn*, "the gentiles" — those without prior scripture. Khalifa preserves the term as a *historical-religious* category: peoples who had not received an earlier book. *Recite, purify, teach* — the threefold messenger's task.

[62:3]: "And to many generations subsequent to them. He is the Almighty, Most Wise." — the message extends to *generations subsequent*: the audience is not only the immediate Arab listeners but their descendants and beyond.

[62:4]: "Such is GOD's grace that He bestows upon whomever He wills. GOD is Possessor of Infinite Grace." — the choice of audience is named as *grace* (*faḍl*).

**Movement II: the Torah-burden polemic (62:5–8).**

[62:5]: "The example of those who were given the Torah, then failed to uphold it, is like the donkey carrying great works of literature. Miserable indeed is the example of people who rejected GOD's revelations. GOD does not guide the wicked people." — *ka-mathali l-ḥimāri yaḥmilu asfārā*. The donkey-and-books image is one of the Quran's most pointed: *possession without comprehension*. The critique is *not* of being given Torah but of *failing to carry it* — *ḥaml* in the sense of *bearing the meaning*, not just transporting the scroll.

[62:6]: "Say, 'O you who are Jewish, if you claim that you are GOD's chosen, to the exclusion of all other people, then you should long for death if you are truthful!'" — a *test claim*: if you really are uniquely beloved and saved, prove it by your eagerness to leave this world.

[62:7]: "They will never long for it, because of what they have committed. GOD is fully aware of the wicked." — the test answers itself.

[62:8]: "Say, 'The death that you are trying to evade will catch up with you sooner or later. Then you will be returned to the Knower of all secrets and declarations, then He will inform you of everything you had done.'" — death's inevitability re-framed.

**Movement III: the Friday command (62:9–11).**

[62:9]: "O you who believe, when the Congregational Prayer (Ṣalāt al-Jumu`ah) is announced on Friday, you shall hasten to the commemoration of GOD, and drop all business. This is better for you, if you only knew." — the explicit institution of the Friday congregational prayer in Quranic text. *Fa-s`aw ilā dhikri llāh* — "hasten to the commemoration." *Wa-dharū l-bay`* — "drop all business" — including buying, selling, transactions.

[62:10]: "Once the prayer is completed, you may spread through the land to seek GOD's bounties, and continue to remember GOD frequently, that you may succeed." — the prayer is not the end of *worldly* activity; once it is done, work resumes — with *frequent remembrance* attached.

[62:11]: "When some of them come across a business deal, or some entertainment, they rush to it and leave you standing! Say, 'What GOD possesses is far better than the entertainment or the business. GOD is the best Provider.'" — closing rebuke of those who *left the Prophet standing* in the pulpit when a trade caravan arrived. *Inḥaḍḍū ilayhā wa-tarakūka qā'iman* — "they rushed to it and left you standing." The implied valuation: *what God possesses* (*mā `inda llāh*) is better than commerce or entertainment.

**Synthesis.**

The surah opens with God's sovereignty being declared by all creation, identifies the messenger as one sent specifically to the *ummī* community to begin the work of scriptural civilization, contrasts the Arab reception with the Israelite failure to carry the Torah's meaning, and institutes the Friday prayer as a *suspension* of commerce in favor of remembrance — closing with the precise rebuke of those who break that suspension. Friday is, in this surah, not a sabbath of rest but a *commercial pause*: business stops only as long as the call sounds and the prayer is performed.

62:1.text: Glorifying GOD is everything in the heavens and everything on earth; the King, the Most Sacred, the Almighty, the Most Wise.
62:1.arabic: يسبح لله ما في السموت وما في الأرض الملك القدوس العزيز الحكيم

62:2.text: He is the One who sent to the gentiles a messenger from among them, to recite to them His revelations, purify them, and teach them the scripture and wisdom. Before this, they had gone far astray.
62:2.arabic: هو الذي بعث في الأميـن رسولا منهم يتلوا عليهم ءايته ويزكيهم ويعلمهم الكتب والحكمة وإن كانوا من قبل لفي ضلل مبين

62:3.text: And to many generations subsequent to them. He is the Almighty, Most Wise.
62:3.arabic: وءاخرين منهم لما يلحقوا بهم وهو العزيز الحكيم

62:4.text: Such is GOD's grace that He bestows upon whomever He wills. GOD is Possessor of Infinite Grace.
62:4.arabic: ذلك فضل الله يؤتيه من يشاء والله ذو الفضل العظيم

62:5.text: The example of those who were given the Torah, then failed to uphold it, is like the donkey carrying great works of literature. Miserable indeed is the example of people who rejected GOD's revelations. GOD does not guide the wicked people.
62:5.arabic: مثل الذين حملوا التورىة ثم لم يحملوها كمثل الحمار يحمل أسفارا بئس مثل القوم الذين كذبوا بـايت الله والله لا يهدي القوم الظـلمين

62:6.text: Say, "O you who are Jewish, if you claim that you are GOD's chosen, to the exclusion of all other people, then you should long for death if you are truthful!"
62:6.arabic: قل يـأيها الذين هادوا إن زعمتم أنكم أولياء لله من دون الناس فتمنوا الموت إن كنتم صدقين

62:7.text: They will never long for it, because of what they have committed. GOD is fully aware of the wicked.
62:7.arabic: ولا يتمنونه أبدا بما قدمت أيديهم والله عليم بالظـلمين

62:8.text: Say, "The death that you are trying to evade will catch up with you sooner or later. Then you will be returned to the Knower of all secrets and declarations, then He will inform you of everything you had done."
62:8.arabic: قل إن الموت الذي تفرون منه فإنه ملقيكم ثم تردون إلى علم الغيب والشهدة فينبئكم بما كنتم تعملون

62:9.text: O you who believe, when the Congregational Prayer (Salat Al- Jumu`ah) is announced on Friday, you shall hasten to the commemoration of GOD, and drop all business. This is better for you, if you only knew.
62:9.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا إذا نودي للصلوة من يوم الجمعة فاسعوا إلى ذكر الله وذروا البيع ذلكم خير لكم إن كنتم تعلمون

62:10.text: Once the prayer is completed, you may spread through the land to seek GOD's bounties, and continue to remember GOD frequently, that you may succeed.
62:10.arabic: فإذا قضيت الصلوة فانتشروا في الأرض وابتغوا من فضل الله واذكروا الله كثيرا لعلكم تفلحون

62:11.text: When some of them come across a business deal, or some entertainment, they rush to it and leave you standing! Say, "What GOD possesses is far better than the entertainment or the business. GOD is the best Provider."
62:11.arabic: وإذا رأوا تجرة أو لهوا انفضوا إليها وتركوك قائما قل ما عند الله خير من اللهو ومن التجرة والله خير الرـزقين

question: Tell me the story of Sulaiman as the Quran narrates it.
answer:
The Quran's Solomon (Sulaymān) is the son and heir of David, gifted with extraordinary endowments — language of birds, controlled wind, mobilized jinn — and the central narrative is his encounter with the Queen of Sheba. The story is distributed mainly across sūras 21, 27, 34, and 38, with a brief defense in 2:102.

**1. Heir of David, granted understanding.**

[38:30]: "To David we granted Solomon; a good and obedient servant." — *ni`ma l-`abdu innahū awwāb*. The same description applied to David is applied to him: *awwāb*, "ever-returning to God."

[27:15]: "We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, 'Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants.'" — knowledge as inheritance, *gratitude* as the first response.

[27:16]: "Solomon was David's heir. He said, 'O people, we have been endowed with understanding the language of the birds, and all kinds of things have been bestowed upon us. This is indeed a real blessing.'" — *manṭiqu ṭ-ṭayr*, "the speech of the birds." Solomon publicly identifies this as a *blessing*, not a personal achievement.

**2. The mobilized army.**

[27:17]: "Mobilized in the service of Solomon were his obedient soldiers of jinns and humans, as well as the birds; all at his disposal." — a three-domain command structure: jinns, humans, birds.

[21:81]: "For Solomon, we committed the wind gusting and blowing at his disposal. He could direct it as he wished, to whatever land he chose, and we blessed such land for him. We are fully aware of all things." — controlled wind.

[34:12]: "To Solomon we committed the wind at his disposal, traveling one month coming and one month going. And we caused a spring of oil to gush out for him. Also, the jinns worked for him." — the wind's range (one month of normal travel per direction). Jinns work for him. A spring of molten copper / oil gushes for him.

[34:13]: "They made for him anything he wanted — niches, statues, deep pools, and heavy cooking pots. 'O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative.'" — the jinns produce monumental architecture; the verse pivots to a *moral command to the household*.

**3. The judgment with David.**

[21:78]: "And David and Solomon, when they once ruled with regard to someone's crop that was destroyed by another's sheep, we witnessed their judgment." — joint judgment, witnessed by God.

[21:79]: "We granted Solomon the correct understanding..." — a hint that Solomon's ruling was preferred. Classical commentary fills in: David ruled the sheep-owner should hand over the sheep; Solomon proposed the sheep work the field temporarily until it was restored — both rulings *correct in different senses*; Solomon's solution was the more equitable.

**4. The ant.**

[27:18]: "When they approached the valley of the ants, one ant said, 'O you ants, go into your homes, lest you get crushed by Solomon and his soldiers, without perceiving.'" — a famous Quranic image: an ant gives evacuation orders; Solomon hears her speech ([27:19]: "He smiled and laughed at her statement..."). The story's point: Solomon's gift extends not only to commanding nature but to *understanding the speech of its smallest agents*.

**5. The hoopoe and the Queen of Sheba.**

[27:20]–[27:24]: Solomon reviews his bird-corps; the hoopoe is missing. The hoopoe returns from Sheba with news: a queen reigns there, sun-worshipping, with a great throne.

[27:27]: "We will see if you told the truth, or if you are a liar." — Solomon initially distrusts; he sends a letter via the hoopoe.

[27:30]: "It is from Solomon, and it is, 'In the name of GOD, Most Gracious, Most Merciful.'" — the Quran preserves Solomon's letter's opening *bi-smi llāh*.

**6. The throne brought across.**

[27:36]: When her envoys arrive bearing gifts, Solomon rejects the gifts: "What GOD has given me is far better than what He has given you." Then he asks his court: "Which of you can bring her throne before she comes here?" [27:39]: a jinn says, "I can bring it before you stand up." [27:40]: "One who possessed knowledge from the book said, 'I can bring it before you blink your eyes.'" The throne appears.

[27:42]: "When she arrived, she was asked, 'Does your mansion look like this?' She said, 'It seems that this is it.'" — Solomon disguises her throne; she recognizes it but does not fully commit.

**7. The crystal floor.**

[27:44]: "She was told, 'Go inside the palace.' When she saw its interior, she thought it was a pool of water, and she (pulled up her dress,) exposing her legs. He said, 'This interior is now paved with crystal.' She said, 'My Lord, I have wronged my soul. I now submit with Solomon to GOD, Lord of the universe.'" — the conversion. She submits *with* Solomon — to God.

**8. The test of wealth.**

[38:34]: "We thus put Solomon to the test; we blessed him with vast material wealth, but he steadfastly submitted." — the wealth itself is the test; his response was submission.

**9. The death detail.**

[34:14]: "When the appointed time for his death came, nothing indicated to them that he had died until an earthworm ate (his staff). When he fell down, the jinns realized that if they really knew the unseen, they would have stopped working for Solomon." — Solomon dies leaning on his staff. The jinns continue working until the staff is eaten through and he falls. The moral the verse extracts: *the jinns did not know the unseen* — a Quranic correction of the magical-jinns folk theology.

**10. The exoneration of Solomon against the sorcery slander.**

[2:102]: "They pursued what the devils taught concerning Solomon's kingdom. Solomon, however, was not a disbeliever, but the devils were disbelievers. They taught the people sorcery, and that which was sent down through the two angels of Babel, Hārūt and Mārūt." — the Quran defends Solomon against the *post-mortem slander* that he was a sorcerer-king.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's Solomon is: David's heir granted knowledge ([27:15]–[27:16]); commander of jinns, humans, birds, and wind ([21:81], [27:17], [34:12]); maker of monumental architecture ([34:13]); a just judge ([21:78]–[21:79]); the king who *hears the ant* ([27:18]); the recipient of the Queen of Sheba's conversion ([27:44]); the man tested with vast wealth who *remained submitting* ([38:34]); and the prophet defended against the post-mortem charge of sorcery ([2:102]). His narrative is the Quran's longest single prophetic arc after Moses, and its central lesson is *gratitude under unprecedented endowment*.

2:102.text: They pursued what the devils taught concerning Solomon's kingdom. Solomon, however, was not a disbeliever, but the devils were disbelievers. They taught the people sorcery, and that which was sent down through the two angels of Babel, Haroot and Maroot. These two did not divulge such knowledge without pointing out: "This is a test. You shall not abuse such knowledge." But the people used it in such evil schemes as the breaking up of marriages. They can never harm anyone against the will of GOD. They thus learn what hurts them, not what benefits them, and they know full well that whoever practices witchcraft will have no share in the Hereafter. Miserable indeed is what they sell their souls for, if they only knew.
2:102.arabic: واتبعوا ما تتلوا الشيطين على ملك سليمن وما كفر سليمن ولكن الشيطين كفروا يعلمون الناس السحر وما أنزل على الملكين ببابل هروت ومروت وما يعلمان من أحد حتى يقولا إنما نحن فتنة فلا تكفر فيتعلمون منهما ما يفرقون به بين المرء وزوجه وما هم بضارين به من أحد إلا بإذن الله ويتعلمون ما يضرهم ولا ينفعهم ولقد علموا لمن اشترىه ما له في الأخرة من خلق ولبئس ما شروا به أنفسهم لو كانوا يعلمون

21:78.text: And David and Solomon, when they once ruled with regard to someone's crop that was destroyed by another's sheep, we witnessed their judgment.
21:78.arabic: وداود وسليمن إذ يحكمان في الحرث إذ نفشت فيه غنم القوم وكنا لحكمهم شهدين

21:79.text: We granted Solomon the correct understanding, though we endowed both of them with wisdom and knowledge. We committed the mountains to serve David in glorifying (God), as well as the birds. This is what we did.
21:79.arabic: ففهمنها سليمن وكلا ءاتينا حكما وعلما وسخرنا مع داود الجبال يسبحن والطير وكنا فعلين

21:81.text: For Solomon, we committed the wind gusting and blowing at his disposal. He could direct it as he wished, to whatever land he chose, and we blessed such land for him. We are fully aware of all things.
21:81.arabic: ولسليمن الريح عاصفة تجري بأمره إلى الأرض التي بركنا فيها وكنا بكل شيء علمين

27:15.text: We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, "Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants."
27:15.arabic: ولقد ءاتينا داود وسليمن علما وقالا الحمد لله الذي فضلنا على كثير من عباده المؤمنين

27:16.text: Solomon was David's heir. He said, "O people, we have been endowed with understanding the language of the birds, and all kinds of things have been bestowed upon us. This is indeed a real blessing."
27:16.arabic: وورث سليمن داود وقال يـأيها الناس علمنا منطق الطير وأوتينا من كل شيء إن هذا لهو الفضل المبين

27:17.text: Mobilized in the service of Solomon were his obedient soldiers of jinns and humans, as well as the birds; all at his disposal.
27:17.arabic: وحشر لسليمن جنوده من الجن والإنس والطير فهم يوزعون

27:18.text: When they approached the valley of the ants, one ant said, "O you ants, go into your homes, lest you get crushed by Solomon and his soldiers, without perceiving."
27:18.arabic: حتى إذا أتوا على واد النمل قالت نملة يـأيها النمل ادخلوا مسكنكم لا يحطمنكم سليمن وجنوده وهم لا يشعرون

27:27.text: (Solomon) said, "We will see if you told the truth, or if you are a liar.
27:27.arabic: ۞قال سننظر أصدقت أم كنت من الكذبين

27:30.text: "It is from Solomon, and it is, `In the name of GOD, Most Gracious, Most Merciful.'
27:30.arabic: إنه من سليمن وإنه بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم

27:36.text: When the hoopoe returned to Solomon (he told him the news), and he responded (to Sheba's people): "Are you giving me money? What GOD has given me is far better than what He has given you. You are the ones to rejoice in such gifts."
27:36.arabic: فلما جاء سليمن قال أتمدونن بمال فما ءاتىن الله خير مما ءاتىكم بل أنتم بهديتكم تفرحون

27:42.text: When she arrived, she was asked, "Does your mansion look like this?" She said, "It seems that this is it." (Solomon said,) "We knew beforehand what she was going to do, and we were already submitters."
27:42.arabic: فلما جاءت قيل أهكذا عرشك قالت كأنه هو وأوتينا العلم من قبلها وكنا مسلمين

27:44.text: She was told, "Go inside the palace." When she saw its interior, she thought it was a pool of water, and she (pulled up her dress,) exposing her legs. He said, "This interior is now paved with crystal." She said, "My Lord, I have wronged my soul. I now submit with Solomon to GOD, Lord of the universe."
27:44.arabic: قيل لها ادخلي الصرح فلما رأته حسبته لجة وكشفت عن ساقيها قال إنه صرح ممرد من قوارير قالت رب إني ظلمت نفسي وأسلمت مع سليمن لله رب العلمين

34:12.text: To Solomon we committed the wind at his disposal, traveling one month coming and one month going. And we caused a spring of oil to gush out for him. Also, the jinns worked for him, by his Lord's leave. Any one of them who disregarded our commands, we subjected him to a severe retribution.
34:12.arabic: ولسليمن الريح غدوها شهر ورواحها شهر وأسلنا له عين القطر ومن الجن من يعمل بين يديه بإذن ربه ومن يزغ منهم عن أمرنا نذقه من عذاب السعير

34:13.text: They made for him anything he wanted - niches, statues, deep pools, and heavy cooking pots. O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative. Jinns' Knowledge is Limited
34:13.arabic: يعملون له ما يشاء من محريب وتمثيل وجفان كالجواب وقدور راسيت اعملوا ءال داود شكرا وقليل من عبادي الشكور

38:30.text: To David we granted Solomon; a good and obedient servant.
38:30.arabic: ووهبنا لداود سليمن نعم العبد إنه أواب

38:34.text: We thus put Solomon to the test; we blessed him with vast material wealth, but he steadfastly submitted.
38:34.arabic: ولقد فتنا سليمن وألقينا على كرسيه جسدا ثم أناب

question: Explain Surah 77 (Dispatched) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 77 (Al-Mursalāt — "Dispatched") is fifty verses, Meccan. The surah is built on two architectural features: an opening five-oath sequence on angelic dispatch, and the repeated refrain *wayl-un yawma'idhin li-l-mukadhdhibīn* ("Woe on that day to the rejectors") — recurring ten times.

**Oath-sequence: the dispatched ones (77:1–7).**

[77:1]: "(Angels) dispatched in succession." — *wa-l-mursalāti `urfā*. *`Urf* = "in a steady stream" / "like a mane." First oath: angels (or winds; exegesis is split) sent in succession.

[77:2]: "To drive the wind." — *fa-l-`āṣifāti `aṣfā*.

[77:3]: "Stir up clouds." — *wa-n-nāshirāti nashrā*.

[77:4]: "Distribute the provisions." — *fa-l-fāriqāti farqā*.

[77:5]: "Deliver messages." — *fa-l-mulqiyāti dhikrā*. Five present participles in a row, each followed by its cognate verbal noun — a tight rhythmic structure unique to this opening. The five-fold task: succession, driving, stirring, distributing, delivering.

[77:6]: "Good news, as well as warnings." — *`udhran aw nudhrā*. The content of what is delivered.

[77:7]: "What is promised will come to pass." — *innamā tū`adūna la-wāqi`*. The conclusion of the five-oath frame: *the promise will fall*.

**Cosmic dissolution (77:8–14).**

[77:8]: "Thus, when the stars are put out." — *fa-idhā n-nujūmu ṭumisat*.

[77:9]: "The sky is opened up." — *furijat*.

[77:10]: "The mountains are blown up." — *nusifat*.

[77:11]: "The messengers are summoned." — *uqqitat*. Khalifa: messengers summoned to bear witness.

[77:12]–[77:13]: "That is the appointed day. The Day of Decision." — *li-ayyi yawmin ujjilat. li-yawmi l-faṣl*. *Yawm al-faṣl* = Day of Decision/Separation.

**Refrain 1 ([77:15]).**

"Woe on that day to the rejectors." — *wayl-un yawma'idhin li-l-mukadhdhibīn*. This refrain will return ten times; each occurrence concludes a small unit of argument.

**Historical precedent (77:16–18).**

[77:16]: "Did we not annihilate the earlier generations?" — historical induction.

[77:17]: "Then we made others follow them?"

[77:18]: "This is what we do to the criminals." — *kadhālika naf`alu bi-l-mujrimīn*.

**Refrain 2 ([77:19]).** "Woe on that day to the rejectors."

**Creation reminder (77:20–24).**

[77:20]: "Did we not create you from a lowly liquid?"

[77:21]: "Then we placed it in a well-protected repository." — *makīn*, secure.

[77:22]: "For a specific period." — gestation as scheduled.

[77:23]: "We measured it precisely. We are the best designers." — *fa-na`ma l-qādirūn*.

**Refrain 3 ([77:24]).**

**Earth reminder (77:25–27).**

[77:25]: "Did we not make the earth an abode?" — *kifātā*, an enclosure / container.

[77:26]: "For the living and the dead?" — the earth holds both.

[77:27]: "We placed on it high mountains, and provided you with fresh water to drink." — *furātā*, fresh.

**Refrain 4 ([77:28]).**

**Hell-scene (77:29–37).**

[77:29]–[77:30]: "Go to what you used to disbelieve in. Go to a shade of three different densities." — *ẓillin dhī thalāthi shu`ab*.

[77:31]: "Yet, it provides neither coolness, nor protection from the heat." — the shade *that fails to shade*.

[77:32]–[77:33]: sparks as big as castles, "yellow as the color of camels."

**Refrain 5–7.**

[77:34], [77:37], [77:40] — repeated.

**The summoning (77:38–40).**

[77:38]: "This is the Day of Decision. We have summoned you and the previous generations." — *jama`nākum wa-l-awwalīn*.

[77:39]: "If you have any schemes, go ahead and scheme." — ironic dare.

**Paradise-scene (77:41–44).**

[77:41]: "The righteous will enjoy shade and springs." — proper shade vs. the failed shade of [77:31].

[77:42]: "And fruits that they desire."

[77:43]: "Eat and drink happily in return for your works."

[77:44]: "We thus reward the virtuous." — *inna kadhālika najzī l-muḥsinīn*.

**Refrain 8 ([77:45]).**

**Address to the rejectors (77:46–47).**

[77:46]: "Eat and enjoy temporarily; you are guilty." — *innakum mujrimūn*.

**Refrain 9 ([77:47]).**

**The bowing test (77:48–49).**

[77:48]: "When they are told, 'Bow down,' they do not bow down." — *idhā qīla lahum-u rka`ū lā yarka`ūn*. The diagnostic of disbelief: *refusal of the physical posture of submission*.

**Refrain 10 ([77:49]).**

**The closing rhetorical question.**

[77:50]: "Which Hadith, other than this, do they uphold?" — *fa-bi-ayyi ḥadīthin ba`dahū yu'minūn*. In Khalifa's exegesis this verse, like [69:43]–[69:47], is a central anti-Hadith proof-text: the Quran asks rhetorically *which other ḥadīth* the audience would accept after this — implying that, once the Quran is delivered, no other ḥadīth (saying / report) competes for acceptance.

**Synthesis.**

The surah's architecture — the opening oath, the dissolution scene, the alternation of *refrain + reminder* (creation, earth, hell, paradise, bowing), the closing rhetorical "which other ḥadīth" — works as a chained warning. The refrain *wayl-un yawma'idhin li-l-mukadhdhibīn* is the load-bearing repetition; each verse-cluster offers a fresh ground for the woe. The closing verse, in Khalifa's frame, presses the question: *what other saying will the rejector accept, having heard this?*

77:6.text: Good news, as well as warnings.

77:7.text: What is promised will come to pass.

77:8.text: Thus, when the stars are put out.

77:15.text: Woe on that day to the rejectors.

77:16.text: Did we not annihilate the earlier generations?

77:18.text: This is what we do to the criminals.

77:20.text: Did we not create you from a lowly liquid?

77:25.text: Did we not make the earth an abode?

77:29.text: Go to what you used to disbelieve in.
77:29.arabic: انطلقوا إلى ما كنتم به تكذبون

77:38.text: This is the Day of Decision. We have summoned you and the previous generations.

77:41.text: The righteous will enjoy shade and springs.

77:48.text: When they are told, "Bow down," they do not bow down.
77:48.arabic: وإذا قيل لهم اركعوا لا يركعون

77:50.text: Which Hadith, other than this, do they uphold?

question: Explain Surah 92 (The Night) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 92 (Al-Layl — "The Night") is twenty-one verses, Meccan. The surah opens with three oaths, then bifurcates humanity into two ethical-economic types and traces each one's outcome.

**The three oaths (92:1–3).**

[92:1]: "By the night as it covers." — *wa-l-layli idhā yaghshā*. *Yaghshā* = enwraps. The night *that covers* — same root as the *Ghāshiya* of surah 88.

[92:2]: "The day as it reveals." — *wa-n-nahāri idhā tajallā*. *Tajallā* — manifestation, becoming visible.

[92:3]: "And Him who created the male and the female." — *wa-mā khalaqa dh-dhakara wa-l-unthā*. The third oath shifts from cosmic alternation (night/day) to the human pair. *Mā* here = "Him who" — the creator of the gendered pair.

**The thesis: works are various (92:4).**

[92:4]: "Your works are of various kinds." — *inna sa`yakum la-shattā*. The opening claim of the moral teaching: *your strivings are diverse*.

**Type A: the giver (92:5–7).**

[92:5]: "As for him who gives to charity and maintains righteousness." — *fa-ammā man a`ṭā wa-ttaqā*. The two predicates: *giving* + *taqwā* (reverent restraint).

[92:6]: "And upholds the scripture." — *wa-ṣaddaqa bi-l-ḥusnā*. *Al-Ḥusnā* (Khalifa: "the scripture" / classical: "the best, the most beautiful") — Khalifa reads this as the scripture itself.

[92:7]: "We will direct him towards happiness." — *fa-sa-nuyassiruhū li-l-yusrā*. *Yassara* = made easy; the *easy path* is graded for him.

**Type B: the stingy (92:8–10).**

[92:8]: "But he who is stingy, though he is rich." — *wa-ammā man bakhila wa-staghnā*. *Bukhl* (stinginess) and *istighnā'* (self-sufficiency-claim) are the two predicates of Type B. Note: *istaghnā* is the same verb used in [96:7] to describe the moment of human transgression — *when he thinks he is rich enough*.

[92:9]: "And disbelieves in the scripture." — *wa-kadhdhaba bi-l-ḥusnā*. The mirror of [92:6].

[92:10]: "We will direct him towards misery." — *fa-sa-nuyassiruhū li-l-`usrā*. *Al-`Usrā* = the difficult path. The same verb *nuyassiruhū* — *we will smooth his way* — but to the opposite destination. The Arabic is precise: God makes *easy* both the path to happiness *and* the path to misery, depending on the agent's choice.

**The futility of wealth at the fall (92:11).**

[92:11]: "His money cannot help him when he falls." — *wa-mā yughnī `anhu māluhū idhā taraddā*. *Taraddā* = "to fall, to be flung down" — a death-fall.

**The divine claim (92:12–13).**

[92:12]: "We provide the guidance." — *inna `alaynā la-l-hudā*. *On us is the guidance* — the responsibility of guiding is *on God*.

[92:13]: "We control the Hereafter, as well as this life." — *wa-inna lanā la-l-ākhirata wa-l-ūlā*. Both lives, both stakes.

**The warning (92:14–16).**

[92:14]: "I have warned you about the blazing Hellfire." — *fa-andhartukum nāran talaẓẓā*. *Talaẓẓā* = "blazing fiercely."

[92:15]: "None burns therein except the wicked." — *lā yaṣlāhā illā l-ashqā*.

[92:16]: "Who disbelieves and turns away." — *alladhī kadhdhaba wa-tawallā*.

**The righteous (92:17–21).**

[92:17]: "Avoiding it will be the righteous." — *wa-sa-yujannabuhā l-atqā*. *Atqā* = the most reverent.

[92:18]: "Who gives from his money to charity." — *alladhī yu'tī mālahū yatazakkā*. *Yatazakkā* = "to purify himself" — the same *tazakkā* of [87:14].

[92:19]–[92:20]: "Seeking nothing in return. Seeking only his Lord, the Most High." — *illā btighā'a wajhi rabbihi l-a`lā*. *Wajh* = "face" — seeking only *the face of his Lord*. The verses define pure-motive charity: not for return, not for reputation, only *li-wajhihī*.

[92:21]: "He will certainly attain salvation." — *wa-la-sawfa yarḍā*. *Yarḍā* = "he will be content / satisfied" — the final verse promises *contentment*, not just rescue.

**Synthesis.**

The surah's central tool is *fork in the road*: same divine verb (*nuyassiru*, "we make easy"), two opposite destinations, dependent on whether the agent gives + reveres + upholds the scripture or hoards + self-sufficient + denies it. Money cannot save at the fall ([92:11]); guidance is God's responsibility ([92:12]); the deciding posture is *pure-motive charity* ([92:18]–[92:20]). The closing word *yarḍā* — *he will be content* — points to the *internal* outcome, not just external reward.

92:1.text: By the night as it covers.

92:3.text: And Him who created the male and the female.

92:4.text: Your works are of various kinds.

92:5.text: As for him who gives to charity and maintains righteousness.

92:7.text: We will direct him towards happiness.

92:8.text: But he who is stingy, though he is rich.

92:10.text: We will direct him towards misery.

92:11.text: His money cannot help him when he falls.
92:11.arabic: وما يغني عنه ماله إذا تردى

92:13.text: We control the Hereafter, as well as this life.

92:14.text: I have warned you about the blazing Hellfire.

92:15.text: None burns therein except the wicked.

92:17.text: Avoiding it will be the righteous.

92:18.text: Who gives from his money to charity.

92:19.arabic: وما لأحد عنده من نعمة تجزى

92:20.text: Seeking only his Lord, the Most High.

question: Who were the people of Dawud and what was their fate?
answer:
Like Ishaq and Aaron, David (Dawud) does not have a "destruction of his people" narrative — he is not a prophet sent to a community later annihilated. He is a *prophet-king ruling the Children of Israel*. The question is best answered as: *who were the people David ruled, and what is their long-term fate in the Quran's account?*

**1. The Children of Israel under David.**

[2:251]: "They defeated them by GOD's leave, and David killed Goliath. GOD gave him kingship and wisdom, and taught him as He willed. If it were not for GOD's support of some people against others, there would be chaos on earth." — David's people are the Israelite militia confronting Jālūt (Goliath). The frame is the Saul-David transition in the post-Mosaic period: David is in Saul's army, slays Goliath, and is granted kingship.

[27:15]: "We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, 'Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants.'" — David's people are recipients of a prophet-king blessing *uncommon among His believing servants*. (The phrase implicitly distinguishes them from gentile communities.)

**2. The constitutional charge.**

[38:26]: "O David, we have made you a ruler on earth. Therefore, you shall judge among the people equitably, and do not follow your personal opinion, lest it diverts you from the way of GOD. Surely, those who stray off the path of GOD incur severe retribution for forgetting the Day of Reckoning." — David's people are placed under *equitable judgment*; the king is bound not to *hawā* (personal opinion) but to God's path.

**3. The civic project.**

[34:10]: "We endowed David with blessings from us: 'O mountains, submit with him, and you too, O birds.' We softened the iron for him." — David's people are part of a *civic-religious project* including metallurgy.

[34:13]: "They made for him anything he wanted — niches, statues, deep pools, and heavy cooking pots. O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative." — *āl Dāwūd*, "the family/house of David," is addressed directly: *work to show your appreciation*. The economic ethic is built into the dynasty.

**4. The dispute test.**

[38:21]–[38:22]: The two litigants approach David: "Why are you alarmed? We are two disputants...". They present the case of one having ninety-nine sheep and the other having one. David rules, then realizes it was a test ([38:24] — "he implored his Lord for forgiveness"). His people see the king *publicly judging, publicly repenting*.

**5. The judgment with Solomon.**

[21:78]: "And David and Solomon, when they once ruled with regard to someone's crop that was destroyed by another's sheep, we witnessed their judgment." — joint judgment, intergenerational.

**6. The Psalms.**

[17:55]: "Your Lord is the best knower of everyone in the heavens and the earth... we gave David the Psalms." — David's people are the *recipients of the Zabūr* — a third revealed book named in the Quran alongside Torah and Gospel.

**7. The verdict on the line.**

[5:78]: "Condemned are those who disbelieved among the Children of Israel, by the tongue of David and Jesus, the son of Mary. This is because they disobeyed and transgressed." — *the tongue of David* is one of the two prophetic voices that *condemn* the disbelievers within Israel. David's people did not all remain faithful; the curse is selective and verdicted *by David himself* (in the Psalms, in Quranic frame) and by Jesus later.

**Summary.**

David's "people" are the Children of Israel during the establishment of their kingdom under his rule. The Quran's account does not depict their fate as destruction but as: granted a prophet-king, instructed in equitable judgment, charged to *work in gratitude*, blessed with the Psalms, joint-ruled with Solomon, and finally *split into faithful and disbelieving* — with the disbelievers verdicted by David's own prophetic tongue ([5:78]). The "fate" is mixed, accountable, and individuated rather than nation-wide annihilation.

2:251.text: They defeated them by GOD's leave, and David killed Goliath. GOD gave him kingship and wisdom, and taught him as He willed. If it were not for GOD's support of some people against others, there would be chaos on earth. But GOD showers His grace upon the people.
2:251.arabic: فهزموهم بإذن الله وقتل داود جالوت وءاتىه الله الملك والحكمة وعلمه مما يشاء ولولا دفع الله الناس بعضهم ببعض لفسدت الأرض ولكن الله ذو فضل على العلمين

5:78.text: Condemned are those who disbelieved among the Children of Israel, by the tongue of David and Jesus, the son of Mary. This is because they disobeyed and transgressed.
5:78.arabic: لعن الذين كفروا من بني إسرـءيل على لسان داود وعيسى ابن مريم ذلك بما عصوا وكانوا يعتدون

17:55.text: Your Lord is the best knower of everyone in the heavens and the earth. In accordance with this knowledge, we preferred some prophets over others. For example, we gave David the Psalms.
17:55.arabic: وربك أعلم بمن في السموت والأرض ولقد فضلنا بعض النبيـن على بعض وءاتينا داود زبورا

21:78.text: And David and Solomon, when they once ruled with regard to someone's crop that was destroyed by another's sheep, we witnessed their judgment.
21:78.arabic: وداود وسليمن إذ يحكمان في الحرث إذ نفشت فيه غنم القوم وكنا لحكمهم شهدين

27:15.text: We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, "Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants."
27:15.arabic: ولقد ءاتينا داود وسليمن علما وقالا الحمد لله الذي فضلنا على كثير من عباده المؤمنين

34:10.text: We endowed David with blessings from us: "O mountains, submit with him, and you too, O birds." We softened the iron for him.
34:10.arabic: ۞ولقد ءاتينا داود منا فضلا يجبال أوبي معه والطير وألنا له الحديد

34:13.text: They made for him anything he wanted - niches, statues, deep pools, and heavy cooking pots. O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative. Jinns' Knowledge is Limited
34:13.arabic: يعملون له ما يشاء من محريب وتمثيل وجفان كالجواب وقدور راسيت اعملوا ءال داود شكرا وقليل من عبادي الشكور

38:21.text: Have you received news of the feuding men who sneaked into his sanctuary?
38:21.arabic: ۞وهل أتىك نبؤا الخصم إذ تسوروا المحراب

38:22.text: When they entered his room, he was startled. They said, "Have no fear. We are feuding with one another, and we are seeking your fair judgment. Do not wrong us, and guide us in the right path.
38:22.arabic: إذ دخلوا على داود ففزع منهم قالوا لا تخف خصمان بغى بعضنا على بعض فاحكم بيننا بالحق ولا تشطط واهدنا إلى سواء الصرط

38:26.text: O David, we have made you a ruler on earth. Therefore, you shall judge among the people equitably, and do not follow your personal opinion, lest it diverts you from the way of GOD. Surely, those who stray off the way of GOD incur severe retribution for forgetting the Day of Reckoning.
38:26.arabic: يداود إنا جعلنك خليفة في الأرض فاحكم بين الناس بالحق ولا تتبع الهوى فيضلك عن سبيل الله إن الذين يضلون عن سبيل الله لهم عذاب شديد بما نسوا يوم الحساب

38:30.text: To David we granted Solomon; a good and obedient servant.
38:30.arabic: ووهبنا لداود سليمن نعم العبد إنه أواب

question: How is Dawud's relationship with God portrayed in the Quran?
answer:
Dawud's relationship with God in the Quran is presented in four overlapping frames: inspired prophet-king, recipient of the Psalms, master craftsman whose work is *gratitude*, and *awwāb* — one who turns back to God quickly.

**1. Inspired in the prophetic chain.**

[4:163]: "...and we gave David the Psalms." — David is on the same revelatory channel as Noah, Abraham, Ismail, Isaac, Jacob, Aaron, Solomon, Jesus, etc.

[17:55]: "Your Lord is the best knower of everyone in the heavens and the earth. In accordance with this knowledge, we preferred some prophets over others. For example, we gave David the Psalms." — the Psalms (*Zabūr*) are named as the *evidence* of David's prefer­ment.

**2. Prophet-king — wisdom and kingship together.**

[2:251]: "GOD gave him kingship and wisdom, and taught him as He willed." — *al-mulk wa-l-ḥikmah* are paired: the kingship is *wisdom-bearing*.

[38:26]: "O David, we have made you a ruler on earth. Therefore, you shall judge among the people equitably, and do not follow your personal opinion, lest it diverts you from the way of GOD." — the office of king is *direct address from God*. The relationship is *covenanted ruler­ship* — a king under direct constitution.

**3. Worship that creation echoes.**

[21:79]: "We committed the mountains to serve David in glorifying (God), as well as the birds." — *yusabbiḥna* — mountains and birds glorify *with him*.

[34:10]: "We endowed David with blessings from us: 'O mountains, submit with him, and you too, O birds.' We softened the iron for him." — the same image, with the addition of *softened iron*. The relationship: David's worship *commands the resonance of nature*.

**4. Craft as gratitude.**

[34:11]: "Making complete coats of mail, and measure the rings perfectly. And do righteous works, all of you. I am Seer of everything you do." — David is taught *armor-making* — the precise measurement of links, the technical perfection. The relationship: his daily craft is part of his service to God.

[34:13]: "O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative." — the family is *directly addressed*. The relationship is dynastic-covenantal.

**5. The two litigants — repentance and self-discipline.**

[38:22] (the litigants entering David's chamber, alarming him): "We are two disputants, one of whom has wronged the other; rule between us equitably..."

[38:24]: "He said, 'He is being unfair to you by asking to combine your sheep with his.' Most people who combine their properties treat each other unfairly, except those who believe and work righteousness, and these are unfortunately few. Afterwards, David wondered if he made the right judgment. He thought that we were testing him. He then implored his Lord for forgiveness, fell down prostrate, and repented." — the test arrives *in disguise*; David's response is *immediate prostration and repentance*. The relationship is one of *quick return* — *awwāb*, ever-turning-back.

**6. The *awwāb* signature.**

[38:17]: "Be patient in the face of their utterances, and remember our servant David, the resourceful; he was obedient." — *ni`ma l-`abdu innahū awwāb*.

[38:30]: "To David we granted Solomon; a good and obedient servant." — the same description applied to his son. The dynasty inherits the *awwāb* posture.

**7. Joint witnessed judgment.**

[21:78]: "And David and Solomon, when they once ruled with regard to someone's crop that was destroyed by another's sheep, we witnessed their judgment." — *we witnessed* — God is *present at the rulings*. The relationship: God watches the judgments David and his son render.

**8. The complete portrait.**

[27:15]: "We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, 'Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants.'" — the relationship's *characteristic verbal expression* is *al-ḥamdu li-llāh* — gratitude.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's David is a *prophet-king under direct constitution* (the equitable-judgment command of [38:26]), *recipient of the Psalms* ([17:55]), *master craftsman whose work is gratitude* ([34:10]–[34:13]), *worshiper to whom mountains and birds respond* ([21:79]), and *quick-repenter / ever-turning-back* (*awwāb*) ([38:17], [38:24], [38:30]). The relationship is *covenanted, public, repentant, technical* — a king who works with his hands, judges with fear of his Lord, weeps quickly when convicted, and inherits in his son the same *awwāb* signature.

2:251.text: They defeated them by GOD's leave, and David killed Goliath. GOD gave him kingship and wisdom, and taught him as He willed. If it were not for GOD's support of some people against others, there would be chaos on earth. But GOD showers His grace upon the people.
2:251.arabic: فهزموهم بإذن الله وقتل داود جالوت وءاتىه الله الملك والحكمة وعلمه مما يشاء ولولا دفع الله الناس بعضهم ببعض لفسدت الأرض ولكن الله ذو فضل على العلمين

4:163.text: We have inspired you, as we inspired Noah and the prophets after him. And we inspired Abraham, Ismail, Isaac, Jacob, the Patriarchs, Jesus, Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon. And we gave David the Psalms.
4:163.arabic: ۞إنا أوحينا إليك كما أوحينا إلى نوح والنبيـن من بعده وأوحينا إلى إبرهيم وإسمعيل وإسحق ويعقوب والأسباط وعيسى وأيوب ويونس وهرون وسليمن وءاتينا داود زبورا

17:55.text: Your Lord is the best knower of everyone in the heavens and the earth. In accordance with this knowledge, we preferred some prophets over others. For example, we gave David the Psalms.
17:55.arabic: وربك أعلم بمن في السموت والأرض ولقد فضلنا بعض النبيـن على بعض وءاتينا داود زبورا

21:78.text: And David and Solomon, when they once ruled with regard to someone's crop that was destroyed by another's sheep, we witnessed their judgment.
21:78.arabic: وداود وسليمن إذ يحكمان في الحرث إذ نفشت فيه غنم القوم وكنا لحكمهم شهدين

21:79.text: We granted Solomon the correct understanding, though we endowed both of them with wisdom and knowledge. We committed the mountains to serve David in glorifying (God), as well as the birds. This is what we did.
21:79.arabic: ففهمنها سليمن وكلا ءاتينا حكما وعلما وسخرنا مع داود الجبال يسبحن والطير وكنا فعلين

27:15.text: We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, "Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants."
27:15.arabic: ولقد ءاتينا داود وسليمن علما وقالا الحمد لله الذي فضلنا على كثير من عباده المؤمنين

34:10.text: We endowed David with blessings from us: "O mountains, submit with him, and you too, O birds." We softened the iron for him.
34:10.arabic: ۞ولقد ءاتينا داود منا فضلا يجبال أوبي معه والطير وألنا له الحديد

34:11.text: "You may make shields that fit perfectly, and work righteousness. Whatever you do, I am Seer thereof."
34:11.arabic: أن اعمل سبغت وقدر في السرد واعملوا صلحا إني بما تعملون بصير

34:13.text: They made for him anything he wanted - niches, statues, deep pools, and heavy cooking pots. O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative. Jinns' Knowledge is Limited
34:13.arabic: يعملون له ما يشاء من محريب وتمثيل وجفان كالجواب وقدور راسيت اعملوا ءال داود شكرا وقليل من عبادي الشكور

38:17.text: Be patient in the face of their utterances, and remember our servant David, the resourceful; he was obedient.
38:17.arabic: اصبر على ما يقولون واذكر عبدنا داود ذا الأيد إنه أواب

38:22.text: When they entered his room, he was startled. They said, "Have no fear. We are feuding with one another, and we are seeking your fair judgment. Do not wrong us, and guide us in the right path.
38:22.arabic: إذ دخلوا على داود ففزع منهم قالوا لا تخف خصمان بغى بعضنا على بعض فاحكم بيننا بالحق ولا تشطط واهدنا إلى سواء الصرط

38:24.text: He said, "He is being unfair to you by asking to combine your sheep with his. Most people who combine their properties treat each other unfairly, except those who believe and work righteousness, and these are so few." Afterwards, David wondered if he made the right judgment. He thought that we were testing him. He then implored his Lord for forgiveness, bowed down, and repented.
38:24.arabic: قال لقد ظلمك بسؤال نعجتك إلى نعاجه وإن كثيرا من الخلطاء ليبغي بعضهم على بعض إلا الذين ءامنوا وعملوا الصـلحت وقليل ما هم وظن داود أنما فتنـه فاستغفر ربه وخر راكعا وأناب۩

38:26.text: O David, we have made you a ruler on earth. Therefore, you shall judge among the people equitably, and do not follow your personal opinion, lest it diverts you from the way of GOD. Surely, those who stray off the way of GOD incur severe retribution for forgetting the Day of Reckoning.
38:26.arabic: يداود إنا جعلنك خليفة في الأرض فاحكم بين الناس بالحق ولا تتبع الهوى فيضلك عن سبيل الله إن الذين يضلون عن سبيل الله لهم عذاب شديد بما نسوا يوم الحساب

38:30.text: To David we granted Solomon; a good and obedient servant.
38:30.arabic: ووهبنا لداود سليمن نعم العبد إنه أواب

question: Explain Surah 1 (The Key) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 1 (Al-Fātiḥah — "The Key" in Khalifa's rendering, "The Opening" in classical translation) is seven verses, Meccan. It is the only surah recited word-for-word in every cycle of every formal prayer (*ṣalāh*) — and Khalifa's translation of the title as "The Key" registers exactly this function: it *opens* every contact prayer.

**[1:1]: "In the name of GOD, Most Gracious, Most Merciful." — *bi-smi llāhi r-Raḥmāni r-Raḥīm.*

The *basmalah*. Two divine attributes from the same root R-Ḥ-M: *Raḥmān* — usually rendered "Most Gracious" — denoting the *universal* mercy extended to all creation; *Raḥīm* — "Most Merciful" — the *particular* mercy extended to the believers. Khalifa's exegesis treats the basmalah as *verse 1*, contrary to the *kūfan* counting tradition where the basmalah is unnumbered. This counting matters for his Code-19 framework: with the basmalah counted, surah 1 has 7 verses with 29 words and 139 letters in a particular arrangement Khalifa argues is mathematically structured.

**[1:2]: "Praise be to GOD, Lord of the universe." — *al-ḥamdu li-llāhi rabbi l-`ālamīn.*

*Al-ḥamd* — praise that combines *acknowledgment* and *gratitude* — is *li-llāh*, "for God." *Rabb al-`ālamīn* — *Lord of the worlds / the universe*. The plural *`ālamīn* implies *multiple universes / multiple created orders* — a phrasing Khalifa repeatedly cross-references with [69:17] (*the throne above eight*) and other plural-creation passages.

**[1:3]: "Most Gracious, Most Merciful." — repeated.**

The same pair from the *basmalah* is repeated in [1:3]. The repetition is theologically significant: the surah opens *and centers* on mercy. After acknowledging *Lord of the universe* — the sovereign attribute — the surah immediately re-asserts the mercy-attribute, lest the *rabb* be misread as bare power.

**[1:4]: "Master of the Day of Judgment." — *māliki yawmi d-dīn.*

*Mālik* (Master) — distinct from *malik* (King). The diacritical mark matters: *Mālik* in the canonical reading-tradition (with the elongated ā) denotes ownership of the entire process, not just sovereignty over a kingdom. *Yawm ad-dīn* = the Day of Religion / Judgment / Reckoning. *Dīn* in this verse carries the sense of *accounting* — Hebrew cognate is *din*, "judgment." The progression of [1:2]–[1:4] is: *Lord of the universe (creation) → Most Gracious, Most Merciful (mercy) → Master of the Day of Judgment (eschatology)*.

**[1:5]: "You alone we worship; You alone we ask for help." — *iyyāka na`budu wa-iyyāka nasta`īn.*

The *first-person plural* turns the surah from declaration to invocation. *Iyyāka* — emphatic *You alone* — fronts the object before the verb, the Arabic syntactical marker of *exclusivity*. The two verbs: *na`bud* (worship) and *nasta`īn* (ask help) — the two operations of the religious life. Worship without seeking help is incomplete; seeking help apart from worship is also incomplete.

**[1:6]: "Guide us in the right path." — *ihdinā ṣ-ṣirāṭa l-mustaqīm.*

The petition. *Hidāyah* (guidance) — *ṣirāṭ mustaqīm* — *the straight path*. *Ṣirāṭ* is borrowed Greek (*strata*) into Arabic — a Roman road, paved, level, clear. *Mustaqīm* = straight, upright, level.

**[1:7]: "The path of those whom You blessed; not of those who have deserved wrath, nor of the strayers." — *ṣirāṭa lladhīna an`amta `alayhim ghayri l-maghḍūbi `alayhim wa-lā ḍ-ḍāllīn.*

The path is *positively* characterized as *those whom You blessed* — the company of the prophets and the righteous (cross-reference [4:69] — "the prophets, the saints, the martyrs, the righteous"). And *negatively* characterized by two contrasts: *those whom wrath has been earned upon* and *those who go astray*. Classical exegesis identifies the first as Jews-who-rejected-prophecy and the second as Christians-who-overstepped-monotheism; Khalifa's exegesis preserves this exegetical history but emphasizes the *categorical, not ethnic* reading: the two errors are *willful rejection* and *unmoored straying* — anyone in either category, in any community, falls under the *negative* clauses.

**Why "The Key"?**

Khalifa's rendering names the surah by its *liturgical role*: this surah is recited at the start of every *rak`ah* (prayer cycle). It *opens* the prayer; it is the *key* that the prayer turns on. The surah's content matches its function — it is a compact theological summary (Lordship, mercy, judgment) plus a personal-plural petition (worship, help, guidance). One could argue the entire Quran is the answer to the petition uttered in [1:6]–[1:7].

**Synthesis.**

Seven short verses; one liturgical anchor; the architecture is *declaration (1–4) → petition (5–7)*. Within the petition, the *first move* is worship and help-seeking together ([1:5]), the *second* is the request for guidance ([1:6]), the *third* is the description of what guidance *is* — the path of the blessed, away from wrath and from straying ([1:7]).

1:1.text: In the name of GOD, Most Gracious, Most Merciful.

1:2.text: Praise be to GOD, Lord of the universe.

1:4.text: Master of the Day of Judgment.

1:5.text: You alone we worship; You alone we ask for help.

1:6.text: Guide us in the right path;

1:7.text: the path of those whom You blessed; not of those who have deserved wrath, nor of the strayers.
1:7.arabic: صرط الذين أنعمت عليهم غير المغضوب عليهم ولا الضالين

question: Explain Surah 80 (He Frowned) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 80 (`Abasa — "He Frowned") is forty-two verses, Meccan. The surah opens with one of the Quran's most striking interventions against its own messenger — a divine rebuke of Muhammad for showing impatience toward a blind questioner while attending to a wealthy pagan — and proceeds through honor of scripture, creation theology, food gratitude, and the Day of Decision.

**Movement I: the divine rebuke of the Prophet (80:1–10).**

[80:1]: "He (Muhammad) frowned and turned away." — *`abasa wa-tawallā*. The verbs are third-person — the Quran refers to its own messenger in the third person to enact a *distancing rebuke*.

[80:2]: "When the blind man came to him." — *an jā'ahu l-a`mā*. Tradition identifies the blind questioner as `Abdullāh ibn Umm Maktūm, who came interrupting Muhammad's outreach to Quraysh elites.

[80:3]: "How do you know? He may purify himself." — the rhetorical question: *what made you turn away — perhaps he was the one who would have benefited*.

[80:4]: "Or he may take heed, and benefit from the message." — the blind man's potential is fully unknown to the Prophet at the moment of the frown.

[80:5]–[80:7]: "As for the rich man. You gave him your attention. Even though you could not guarantee his salvation." — *anta lahū taṣaddā / wa-mā `alayka allā yazzakkā*. *Taṣaddā* = "to attend to, to make oneself accessible to." The accusation is precise: the Prophet *attended* to the wealthy, despite *having no guarantee* of the wealthy man's purification.

[80:8]–[80:10]: "The one who came to you eagerly. And is really reverent. You ignored him." — *anta `anhu talahhā*. *Talahhā* = "to be distracted from." The Prophet was *distracted* from the seeker.

The Quran *rebukes its own messenger*, on the public record. This is one of the foundational reasons later Muslim tradition resists the doctrine of prophetic impeccability (`iṣmah) in the form it sometimes took — the Quran itself records prophetic correction.

**Movement II: the scripture's honor (80:11–16).**

[80:11]: "Indeed, this is a reminder." — *kallā innahā tadhkirah*. *Kallā* — strong negation — sweeps away the preceding ranking of audiences.

[80:12]: "Whoever wills shall take heed."

[80:13]–[80:14]: "In honorable scriptures. Exalted and pure." — *fī ṣuḥufin mukarramah, marfū`atin muṭahharah*. The Quran is in *honored scrolls* — *raised up, purified*.

[80:15]–[80:16]: "(Written) by the hands of messengers. Who are honorable and righteous." — *bi-aydī safarah, kirāmin bararah*. *Safarah* = "scribes / messengers" (related to *sifr* = book). Tradition reads this as the angelic scribes; Khalifa preserves the angelic reading.

**Movement III: human ingratitude (80:17–22).**

[80:17]: "Woe to the human being; he is so unappreciative!" — *qutila l-insānu mā akfarah*. *Qutila l-insān* — a Quranic curse-formula, "may the human be killed" — used here rhetorically for *how unappreciative he is*. The Arabic *kafara* (the root behind *kāfir*) is, at its base, *to cover up, to be ungrateful*; "disbelief" and "ingratitude" are *the same word*.

[80:18]: "What did He create him from?" — direct question.

[80:19]: "From a tiny drop, He creates him and designs him." — *min nuṭfatin khalaqahū fa-qaddarahū*. Creation from a drop; precise design.

[80:20]: "Then He points out the path for him." — *thumma s-sabīla yassarahū*. *Yassara* — same root as the *yusrā* of [92:7]: the path was *eased* for him.

[80:21]: "Then He puts him to death, and into the grave." — *thumma amātahū fa-aqbarahū*.

[80:22]: "When He wills, He resurrects him." — death → grave → resurrection, in three quick verbs.

**Movement IV: food gratitude (80:24–32).**

[80:24]: "Let the human consider his food!" — *fa-l-yanẓuri l-insānu ilā ṭa`āmihī*. The verse opens an extended reflection on the food-supply chain.

[80:25]–[80:32]: water poured, soil split, grains grown — grapes, pasture, olives, palms, orchards, fruits, vegetables — *life-support for you and your animals*. The Quran walks through the agricultural chain item by item.

**Movement V: the day of fleeing (80:33–42).**

[80:33]: "Then, when the blow comes to pass." — *fa-idhā jā'ati ṣ-ṣākhkhah*. *Aṣ-Ṣākhkhah* = "the deafening blast" — a divine name for the Day.

[80:34]: "That is the day when one flees from his brother." — *yawma yafirru l-mar'u min akhīh*. The horror of the Day is rendered as the *unraveling of family*.

[80:35]–[80:36]: "From his mother and father. From his spouse and children." — the four most binding social relations, in order.

[80:37]: "Each one of them, on that day, worries about his own destiny." — *li-kulli mri'in minhum yawma'idhin sha'nun yughnīh*. *Sha'nun yughnīh* = "a concern that consumes him."

[80:38]–[80:42]: the two-face panel:

[80:38]–[80:39]: bright faces, *laughing and joyful*.

[80:40]–[80:42]: faces covered in *dust*, *overwhelmed by remorse* — *wa-wujūhun yawma'idhin `alayhā ghabarah / tarhaquhā qatarah / ulā'ika humu l-kafaratu l-fajarah*. The closing word *fajarah* — "the lewd, the dissolute" — pairs with *kafarah* (disbelievers) as the surah's last word.

**Synthesis.**

The surah's most distinctive feature is its opening: the Quran *rebukes its own messenger* for prioritizing the wealthy over the eager-and-blind. This rebuke is preserved verbatim in scripture, by the messenger himself — a structural humility-claim about the Quran's source. The surah then re-anchors: the scripture is in honored hands ([80:13]–[80:16]); the human is ungrateful despite being *eased* into life ([80:17]–[80:22]); food itself is a sign ([80:24]–[80:32]); and the Day will see family dissolve into individual accounting ([80:33]–[80:42]). The ethical thrust matches [90:13]–[90:17]: *attend to the one in need before the one in power*.

80:2.text: When the blind man came to him.

80:3.text: How do you know? He may purify himself.

80:4.text: Or he may take heed, and benefit from the message.

80:7.text: Even though you could not guarantee his salvation.

80:8.text: The one who came to you eagerly.

80:17.text: Woe to the human being; he is so unappreciative!

80:19.text: From a tiny drop, He creates him and designs him.

80:20.text: Then He points out the path for him.

80:21.text: Then He puts him to death, and into the grave.

80:22.text: When He wills, He resurrects him.

80:24.text: Let the human consider his food!

80:33.text: Then, when the blow comes to pass.

80:34.text: That is the day when one flees from his brother.

80:37.text: Each one of them, on that day, worries about his own destiny.
80:37.arabic: لكل امري منهم يومئذ شأن يغنيه

80:38.text: Some faces on that day will be happy.

80:40.text: Other faces, on that day, will be covered with misery.

question: What is the central message of Surah 50 (Q.)?
answer:
Surah 50 (Qāf) is forty-five verses, Meccan. The surah's title is a single Arabic letter — *Qāf* — one of the Quran's *muqaṭṭa`āt* (mysterious letters). In Khalifa's Code-19 framework, *Qāf* is one of the most analyzed initials; he counts every instance of the letter *Qāf* in this sūra and surah 42 (also Qāf-initialed) and reports a precise mathematical relationship. The surah's load-bearing theological claim is that *resurrection is no harder than first creation*, and the audience is being watched and recorded *moment by moment*.

**The opening — the initial letter and the disbelievers' complaint.**

[50:1]: "Q., and the glorious Quran." — *Qāf, wa-l-qur'āni l-majīd*. The oath is on the Quran itself, qualified as *majīd* — glorious, noble.

[50:2]–[50:3]: "They found it strange that a warner from among them came to them! The disbelievers said, 'This is really strange. After we die and become dust; this is impossible.'" — the disbelievers' double objection: (a) a warner from among them, (b) resurrection after dust.

**The first counter-argument — divine record.**

[50:4]–[50:6]: "We are fully aware of anyone of them who gets consumed by the earth; we have an accurate record." — *`indanā kitābun ḥafīẓ*. God has *a preserved book*.

**The second counter-argument — the historical roster.**

[50:12]–[50:14]: "Disbelieving before them were the people of Noah, the dwellers of Russ, and Thamoud. And `Aad, Pharaoh, and the brethren of Lot. And the dwellers of the woods, and the people of Tubba`. All of them disbelieved the messengers and, consequently, My retribution befell them." — *the people of Tubba`* (the Himyaritic dynasty of Yemen) appear only here and in [44:37]; *aṣḥāb al-rass* are the people of the well — both reminders that the prophetic-rejection pattern is *general*, not Arab-specific.

**The third counter-argument — first creation as proof.**

[50:15]: "Were we too burdened by the first creation? Is this why they doubt resurrection?" — the standard Quranic *a fortiori*: if He created you once, *resurrection is easier*, not harder.

**The closeness claim.**

[50:16]: "We created the human, and we know what he whispers to himself. We are closer to him than his jugular vein." — *aqrabu ilayhi min ḥabli l-warīd*. One of the most theologically dense verses in the surah: divine intimacy is rendered as *anatomical proximity*. The verse is often cited in Sufi literature; Khalifa preserves the literal rendering.

**The recording angels.**

[50:17]–[50:18]: "Two recording (angels), at right and at left, are constantly recording. Not an utterance does he utter without an alert witness." — the *kirāman kātibīn*, the noble scribes, are placed structurally here as the daily mechanism of accountability.

**The death moment.**

[50:19]: "Finally, the inevitable coma of death comes; this is what you tried to evade." — *sakratu l-mawti bi-l-ḥaqq* — "the intoxication / coma of death, in truth."

**The resurrection scene.**

[50:21]: "Every soul comes with a herder and a witness." — each soul comes accompanied: a *driver* (*sā'iq*) and a *witness* (*shahīd*).

[50:22]: "You used to be oblivious to this. We now remove your veil; today, your vision is (as strong as) steel." — the *kashf* (lifting of the veil) — what was unseen is now visible. *Baṣaruka l-yawma ḥadīd* — "your sight today is iron / steel" — sharp, penetrating.

**Hell-dialogue.**

[50:30]: "That is the day when we ask Hell, 'Have you had enough?' It will say, 'Give me more.'" — Hell *speaks* in this surah, asking for more. The dialogue rendered is theologically striking: the place of punishment is *active*, not just receptive.

**The conclusion.**

[50:37]: "This should be a lesson for everyone who possesses a mind, or is able to hear and witness." — *li-man kāna lahū qalbun aw alqā s-sam`a wa-huwa shahīd*. A *qalb* (heart/mind) or a *listening ear and present witness*.

[50:45]: "We are fully aware of everything they utter, while you have no power over them. Therefore, remind with this Quran, those who reverence My warnings." — the closing instruction: *remind by Quran*, no compulsion.

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Quran is glorious ([50:1]); resurrection is easier than first creation ([50:15]); God is closer to you than your jugular ([50:16]); angels record every word at left and right ([50:17]–[50:18]); when death's coma arrives, your sight will be iron-sharp ([50:22]) — therefore *remind by Quran* ([50:45]).

50:2.text: They found it strange that a warner from among them came to them! The disbelievers said, "This is really strange.
50:2.arabic: بل عجبوا أن جاءهم منذر منهم فقال الكفرون هذا شيء عجيب

50:3.text: "After we die and become dust; this is impossible."
50:3.arabic: أءذا متنا وكنا ترابا ذلك رجع بعيد

50:6.text: Have they not looked at the sky above them, and how we constructed it and adorned it, without a flaw?
50:6.arabic: أفلم ينظروا إلى السماء فوقهم كيف بنينها وزينـها وما لها من فروج

50:12.text: Disbelieving before them were the people of Noah, the dwellers of Russ, and Thamoud.
50:12.arabic: كذبت قبلهم قوم نوح وأصحب الرس وثمود

50:13.text: And `Aad, Pharaoh, and the brethren of Lot.

50:14.text: And the dwellers of the woods, and the people of Tubba`. All of them disbelieved the messengers and, consequently, My retribution befell them.
50:14.arabic: وأصحب الأيكة وقوم تبع كل كذب الرسل فحق وعيد

50:15.text: Were we too burdened by the first creation? Is this why they doubt resurrection?
50:15.arabic: أفعيينا بالخلق الأول بل هم في لبس من خلق جديد

50:16.text: We created the human, and we know what he whispers to himself. We are closer to him than his jugular vein.
50:16.arabic: ولقد خلقنا الإنسن ونعلم ما توسوس به نفسه ونحن أقرب إليه من حبل الوريد

50:17.text: Two recording (angels), at right and at left, are constantly recording.
50:17.arabic: إذ يتلقى المتلقيان عن اليمين وعن الشمال قعيد

50:18.text: Not an utterance does he utter without an alert witness.
50:18.arabic: ما يلفظ من قول إلا لديه رقيب عتيد

50:19.text: Finally, the inevitable coma of death comes; this is what you tried to evade.
50:19.arabic: وجاءت سكرة الموت بالحق ذلك ما كنت منه تحيد

50:21.text: Every soul comes with a herder and a witness.
50:21.arabic: وجاءت كل نفس معها سائق وشهيد

50:22.text: You used to be oblivious to this. We now remove your veil; today, your vision is (as strong as) steel.
50:22.arabic: لقد كنت في غفلة من هذا فكشفنا عنك غطاءك فبصرك اليوم حديد

50:30.text: That is the day when we ask Hell, "Have you had enough?" It will say, "Give me more."
50:30.arabic: يوم نقول لجهنم هل امتلأت وتقول هل من مزيد

50:37.text: This should be a lesson for everyone who possesses a mind, or is able to hear and witness.
50:37.arabic: إن في ذلك لذكرى لمن كان له قلب أو ألقى السمع وهو شهيد

50:45.text: We are fully aware of everything they utter, while you have no power over them. Therefore, remind with this Quran, those who reverence My warnings.
50:45.arabic: نحن أعلم بما يقولون وما أنت عليهم بجبار فذكر بالقرءان من يخاف وعيد

question: What is the central message of Surah 76 (The Human)?
answer:
Surah 76 (Al-Insān — "The Human") is thirty-one verses. Khalifa and the majority tradition place it as Medinan, though its rhythm and imagery resemble the Meccan paradise-surahs. The surah's central message is that *the human's eligibility for the eternal banquet is decided by what he does with the freedom God gave him* — and the operative test is *charity given for God's face alone*.

**The opening — the human's pre-history.**

[76:1]: "Is it not a fact that there was a time when the human being was nothing to be mentioned?" — *hal atā `alā l-insāni ḥīnun mina d-dahri lam yakun shay'an madhkūrā*. The most arresting opening in the short surahs: *was there not a time when you were nothing worth mentioning*?

[76:2]: "We created the human from a liquid mixture, from two parents, in order to test him. We made him a hearer and a seer." — *min nuṭfatin amshāj* — a *mixed* liquid (the male and female contributions of Khalifa's reading, mirroring [86:6]–[86:7]). Creation is for *testing* (*nabtalīhi*). The two senses given are *hearing* and *sight* — the two channels of evidence-reception.

[76:3]: "We showed him the two paths, then, he is either appreciative, or unappreciative." — *innā hadaynāhu s-sabīla immā shākiran wa-immā kafūrā*. The Quran's clearest one-verse statement of *free will under guidance*: God *showed* the two paths; the human *chooses*.

**The reward path — described in luxurious paradise imagery.**

[76:5]: "As for the pious, they will drink from cups spiced with nectar." — *kāfūr*, "camphor" in classical reading (Khalifa renders generically as "nectar"). The verse begins one of the longest paradise descriptions in the Quran.

[76:7]–[76:9]: "They fulfill their pledges, and reverence a day that is extremely difficult. They donate their favorite food to the poor, the orphan, and the captive. 'We feed you for the sake of GOD; we expect no reward from you, nor thanks.'" — three categories of recipient (poor, orphan, captive) and a *speech act* — they explicitly declare that they feed *li-wajhi llāh* (for God's face) *expecting no reward, no thanks*.

[76:10]: "We fear, from our Lord, a day that is full of misery and trouble."

The verses are read as the *operational signature* of paradise-eligibility: feeding the hungry, declaring no expectation of return, fearing the Day.

[76:11]: "Consequently, GOD protects them from the evils of that day, and rewards them with joy and contentment." — the response: *protection from the day's evils + joy + contentment*.

**The paradise imagery (76:12–22).**

[76:12]: "He rewards them for their steadfastness with Paradise, and silk." — *jannatan wa-ḥarīrā*.

Reclining on luxurious furnishings, neither too cold nor too hot ([76:13]); fruits in easy reach ([76:14]); silver vessels and crystal cups served by youths ([76:15]–[76:19]); silver bracelets and pure drinks ([76:21]).

[76:22]: "This is the reward that awaits you, for your efforts have been appreciated." — *inna hādhā kāna lakum jazā'an wa-kāna sa`yukum mashkūrā*. The *effort* (*sa`y*) is *appreciated* (*mashkūr*) — *thanked* by God.

**The Quran as scheduled gift.**

[76:23]–[76:25]: "We have revealed to you this Quran; a special revelation from us. You shall steadfastly carry out your Lord's commandments, and do not obey any sinful disbeliever, or idol worshiper among them. And commemorate the name of your Lord day and night." — direct instruction to the Prophet about *patience + invocation*.

**The closing exhortation.**

[76:29]: "This is a reminder; whoever wills, let him choose the path to his Lord." — *fa-man shā'a-ttakhadha ilā rabbihī sabīlā*. The freedom from [76:3] is restated.

[76:30]: "Whatever you will is in accordance with GOD's will. GOD is Omniscient, Wise." — *wa-mā tashā'ūna illā an yashā'a llāh*. Khalifa's translation here is theologically tight: human willing operates *within* the divine willing, not against it.

**Central message.**

You came from nothing-worth-mention ([76:1]); were created from a mixed drop and given hearing and sight ([76:2]); shown two paths and made *free to choose* ([76:3]); if you choose appreciation, you do it by *feeding the hungry, the orphan, the captive* *for God's face alone* ([76:8]–[76:9]); your reward is silk, springs, pure drink, and *the thanking of your effort* ([76:22]). The whole surah turns on *what you do with the freedom He gave you, on behalf of the people you cannot benefit from*.

76:1.text: Is it not a fact that there was a time when the human being was nothing to be mentioned?
76:1.arabic: هل أتى على الإنسن حين من الدهر لم يكن شيـا مذكورا

76:2.text: We created the human from a liquid mixture, from two parents, in order to test him. Thus, we made him a hearer and a seer.
76:2.arabic: إنا خلقنا الإنسن من نطفة أمشاج نبتليه فجعلنه سميعا بصيرا

76:3.text: We showed him the two paths, then he is either appreciative, or unappreciative.
76:3.arabic: إنا هدينه السبيل إما شاكرا وإما كفورا

76:5.text: As for the virtuous, they will drink from cups spiced with nectar.
76:5.arabic: إن الأبرار يشربون من كأس كان مزاجها كافورا

76:7.text: They fulfill their pledges, and reverence a day that is extremely difficult.
76:7.arabic: يوفون بالنذر ويخافون يوما كان شره مستطيرا

76:8.text: They donate their favorite food to the poor, the orphan, and the captive.
76:8.arabic: ويطعمون الطعام على حبه مسكينا ويتيما وأسيرا

76:9.text: "We feed you for the sake of GOD; we expect no reward from you, nor thanks.
76:9.arabic: إنما نطعمكم لوجه الله لا نريد منكم جزاء ولا شكورا

76:10.text: trouble." "We fear from our Lord a day that is full of misery and
76:10.arabic: إنا نخاف من ربنا يوما عبوسا قمطريرا

76:11.text: Consequently, GOD protects them from the evils of that day, and rewards them with joy and contentment.
76:11.arabic: فوقىهم الله شر ذلك اليوم ولقىهم نضرة وسرورا

76:12.text: He rewards them for their steadfastness with Paradise, and silk.

76:18.text: From a spring therein known as "Salsabeel."

76:19.text: Serving them will be immortal servants. When you see them, they will look like scattered pearls.
76:19.arabic: ۞ويطوف عليهم ولدن مخلدون إذا رأيتهم حسبتهم لؤلؤا منثورا

76:22.text: This is the reward that awaits you, for your efforts have been appreciated.
76:22.arabic: إن هذا كان لكم جزاء وكان سعيكم مشكورا

76:23.text: We have revealed to you this Quran; a special revelation from us.
76:23.arabic: إنا نحن نزلنا عليك القرءان تنزيلا

76:25.text: And commemorate the name of your Lord day and night.

76:29.text: This is a reminder: whoever wills shall choose the path to his Lord.
76:29.arabic: إن هذه تذكرة فمن شاء اتخذ إلى ربه سبيلا

76:30.text: Whatever you will is in accordance with GOD's will. GOD is Omniscient, Wise.
76:30.arabic: وما تشاءون إلا أن يشاء الله إن الله كان عليما حكيما

question: Explain Surah 79 (The Snatchers) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 79 (An-Nāzi`āt — "The Snatchers" / "Those Who Pull Out") is forty-six verses, Meccan. The surah opens with one of the Quran's most enigmatic oath-sequences — five participial-feminine plurals whose referent is contested — and proceeds through a compact Moses narrative and a tight resurrection-and-judgment argument.

**The five oaths (79:1–5).**

[79:1]: "The (angels who) snatch (the souls of the disbelievers) forcibly." — *wa-n-nāzi`āti gharqā*. *Nāza`a* = to pull out, to snatch, to wrest. *Gharqā* (root g-r-q) = "drowningly" — i.e., *pulled out completely, immersed in the act*. Khalifa identifies the referent as the angels of death extracting disbelieving souls.

[79:2]: "And those who gently take (the souls of the believers) joyfully." — *wa-n-nāshiṭāti nashṭā*. The contrast pair: gentle drawing.

[79:3]: "And those floating everywhere." — *wa-s-sābiḥāti sabḥā*. *Sābiḥa* = swimming, floating. The mid-passage of the angelic ascent.

[79:4]: "Eagerly racing with one another." — *fa-s-sābiqāti sabqā*. Racing forward.

[79:5]: "To carry out various commands." — *fa-l-mudabbirāti amrā*. Khalifa renders *al-mudabbirāt* as the angels who *manage / arrange affairs*. Classical exegesis splits between angels and (in some readings) the stars/planets in their orbits.

**The Day's announcement (79:6–9).**

[79:6]: "The day the quake quakes." — *yawma tarjufu r-rājifah*.

[79:7]: "Followed by the second blow." — *tatba`uhā r-rādifah*. Two events: *al-rājifah* (the quake) and *al-rādifah* (the follower). Khalifa identifies these as two distinct horn-blasts.

[79:8]–[79:9]: "Certain minds will be terrified. Their eyes will be subdued." — fear and submission depicted on the body.

**The disbelievers' objection (79:10–11).**

[79:10]: "They say, 'We have been recreated from the grave!'" — *a-innā la-mardūdūna fī l-ḥāfirah*. *Hāfirah* — "the starting state, the original" — they ask: *are we really returned to the starting state*?

[79:11]: "How could this be, after we have turned into rotten bones?" — *idhā kunnā `iẓāman nakhirah*. The disbelievers' objection: skeletal remains *cannot* be reassembled.

**The Moses narrative (79:15–26).**

[79:15]: "Have you known about the history of Moses?"

[79:16]: "His Lord called him at the holy valley of Tuwa." — *Ṭuwā* — the sacred valley.

[79:17]: "Go to Pharaoh; he has transgressed." — *idhhab ilā fir`awna innahū ṭaghā*.

[79:18]: "Tell him, 'Would you not reform?'" — *fa-qul hal laka ilā an tazakkā*. The opening offer — *would you purify yourself*?

[79:19]: "Let me guide you to your Lord, that you may turn reverent." — Moses's pitch to Pharaoh is *guidance toward reverence*, framed as a *gift*, not a confrontation.

**Pharaoh's response (79:21–24).**

[79:23]: "He summoned." — *fa-ḥashara*.

[79:24]: "He proclaimed: 'I am your Lord, most high.'" — *ana rabbukumu l-a`lā*. The most extreme blasphemy the Quran records on any human tongue. Pharaoh claims the *divine epithet* — *al-A`lā* — that surah 87 names as God's.

**The verdict on Pharaoh (79:25–26).**

[79:25]: "Consequently, GOD committed him to the retribution in the Hereafter, as well as in the first life." — *fa-akhadhahu llāhu nakāla l-ākhirati wa-l-ūlā*. Double punishment: first life and Hereafter.

[79:26]: "This is a lesson for the reverent." — *inna fī dhālika la-`ibratan li-man yakhshā*. *`Ibrah* — "lesson, instructive example."

**The creation-as-resurrection argument (79:27–33).**

The argument: *is the creation of you harder than the creation of the heaven*? — God built the heaven, raised its ceiling, darkened its night, brought out its forenoon ([79:27]–[79:29]); leveled the earth, brought out its water and pasture, set the mountains firm ([79:30]–[79:32]); all *as provisions for you and your livestock* ([79:33]). The implication: *recreation is trivial by comparison*.

**The Day named (79:34–41).**

[79:34]: "Then, when the great blow comes." — *fa-idhā jā'ati ṭ-ṭāmmatu l-kubrā*. *Aṭ-Ṭāmmah* — "the overwhelming" — a divine name for the Day.

[79:35]–[79:36]: That is the day when each soul will remember everything it did, and Hell will be brought into view for whoever sees.

[79:37]: "As for him who transgressed."

[79:38]: "And he was preoccupied with this worldly life."

[79:39]: "Hell will be the abode."

[79:40]–[79:41]: "As for him who reverenced the majesty of his Lord, and enjoined the self from sinful lusts. Paradise will be the abode."

**The Hour's timing (79:42–46).**

[79:42]: "They ask you about the Hour, and when it will take place!" — *yas'alūnaka `ani s-sā`ati ayyāna mursāhā*.

[79:43]: "It is not you (Muhammad) who is destined to announce its time." — the Prophet is *not* given the timing.

[79:44]: "Your Lord decides its fate." — *ilā rabbika muntahāhā*.

[79:46]: "The day they see it, it will seem as if they lasted one evening or half a day." — *ka-annahum yawma yarawnahā lam yalbathū illā `ashiyyatan aw ḍuḥāhā*. The phenomenology of the resurrected: *the whole worldly life will collapse, in retrospect, to an evening or a forenoon*.

**Synthesis.**

The opening five oaths invoke the angels of death-and-management ([79:1]–[79:5]) — those who pull souls, the gentle takers, the floaters, the racers, the managers; the Quran's anthropology places these agents at the seam between life and Hereafter. The Moses–Pharaoh narrative ([79:15]–[79:26]) gives the *exemplum* of trans­gression versus reverence; the cosmological argument ([79:27]–[79:33]) makes resurrection *easier than creation*; the Hour's timing is *not the Prophet's to disclose* ([79:43]). The whole surah ends on the *temporal compression* the resurrected will experience: this life will feel like *an evening or a forenoon*.

79:1.text: The (angels who) snatch (the souls of the disbelievers) forcibly.

79:2.text: And those who gently take (the souls of the believers) joyfully.

79:10.text: They will say, "We have been recreated from the grave!

79:11.text: "How did this happen after we had turned into rotten bones?"

79:15.text: Have you known about the history of Moses?

79:16.text: His Lord called him at the holy valley of Tuwaa.
79:16.arabic: إذ نادىه ربه بالواد المقدس طوى

79:18.arabic: فقل هل لك إلى أن تزكى

79:19.text: "Let me guide you to your Lord, that you may turn reverent."

79:24.text: He said, "I am your Lord; most high."

79:25.text: Consequently, GOD committed him to the retribution in the Hereafter, as well as in the first life.

79:26.text: This is a lesson for the reverent.
79:26.arabic: إن في ذلك لعبرة لمن يخشى

79:34.text: Then, when the great blow comes.

79:37.text: As for the one who transgressed.

79:40.text: As for the one who reverenced the majesty of his Lord, and enjoined the self from sinful lusts.
