25:53.arabic: ۞وهو الذي مرج البحرين هذا عذب فرات وهذا ملح أجاج وجعل بينهما برزخا وحجرا محجورا

55:19.text: He separates the two seas where they meet.

55:20.text: A barrier is placed between them, to prevent them from transgressing.

question: How does the Quran describe the trumpet (sur) of the Day of Resurrection?
answer:
*Aṣ-Ṣūr* — "the horn" / "the trumpet" — is a Quranic eschatological term appearing in *ten places*, marking the *sound-event* that begins the Day of Resurrection. The Quran's treatment is consistent in some features and variable in others: there are *blasts* (sometimes one, sometimes two), they bring *cosmic dissolution* and *resurrection*, and they signal *the transition from this life to the final accounting*.

**1. The horn — basic citation.**

[6:73]: "He is the One who created the heavens and the earth, truthfully. Whenever He says, 'Be,' it is. His word is the absolute truth. All sovereignty belongs to Him the day the horn is blown." — *yawma yunfakhu fī ṣ-Ṣūr*. *The day the horn is blown*. The verse establishes the formula: *yunfakhu fī ṣ-Ṣūr* — *the horn is blown* — is the *standard idiom* for the eschatological event.

**2. The variable count — one blast or two?**

The Quran's treatment of *the count* of horn-blasts is *not uniform*:

[39:68]: "The horn will be blown, whereupon everyone in the heavens and the earth will be struck unconscious, except those who will be spared by GOD. Then it will be blown another time, whereupon they will all rise up, looking." — *thumma nufikha fīhi ukhrā fa-idhā hum qiyāmun yanẓurūn*. The verse is *the clearest* statement of *two blasts*: the *first* causes *all in heavens and earth* to *fall* (except those God spares); the *second* causes them to *rise up, looking*.

[69:13]: "When the horn is blown once." — *nafkhatun wāḥidah*. The verse refers to *a single blast* — classically read as the *one earth-and-mountains-crushing blast*.

Khalifa, in his exegesis, treats the two-blast model of [39:68] as the canonical: a *first blast that levels* and a *second blast that resurrects*. The single-blast references are read as referring to one *or the other* of the two events.

**3. The cosmic consequences.**

[20:102]: "That is the day when the horn is blown, and we summon the guilty on that day blue." — *wa-naḥshuru l-mujrimīna yawma'idhin zurqā*. *Blue* — the disbelievers' *complexion* on rising. (The image: *the dust-and-dread of the resurrected disbeliever*.)

[23:101]: "When the horn is blown, no relationships among them will exist on that day, nor will they care about one another." — *the family-tie dissolution at the horn-blast*.

[27:87]: "On the day the horn is blown, everyone in the heavens and the earth will be horrified, except those chosen by GOD. All will come before Him, humiliated." — *fa-faza`a*. *They will be terrified*. The verb *fazi`a* — *to be alarmed, startled into fear* — captures the *first reaction*.

[36:51]–[36:52]: "The horn will be blown, whereupon they will rise from the graves and go forth to their Lord. They will say, 'Woe to us. Who resurrected us from our death? This is what the Most Gracious has promised. The messengers were right.'" — the *rising* from graves + the *self-realization* of the previously-doubting.

**4. The horn and the cosmic dissolution.**

[78:18]: "The day the horn is blown, and you come in throngs." — *fa-ta'tūna afwājā*. *In throngs*.

[18:99]: "At that time, we will let them invade with one another, then the horn will be blown, and we will summon them all together." — the *summons* by horn.

[50:20]: "The horn is blown; this is the promised day." — *dhālika yawmu l-wa`īd*. *That is the day of the threat*.

**5. The horn's bearer.**

The *who blows the horn* question: the Quran consistently uses *passive voice* (*yunfakhu fī ṣ-Ṣūr* — *is blown into the horn*) rather than naming the blower. Classical exegesis identifies *Isrāfīl* — one of the four chief angels in Islamic angelology — as the *horn-bearer*; this name is *not* in the Quran but in the Hadith corpus. For Khalifa, who limits source to the Quran, the *actor* is simply *the appointed angel* — without name.

**6. The horn and the two earlier "blasts."**

[79:6]–[79:7]: "The day the quake quakes. Followed by the second blow." — *yawma tarjufu r-rājifah, tatba`uhā r-rādifah*. *The quaker* and *the follower*. Khalifa identifies these as the *two horn-blasts* of [39:68] — the first (*ar-Rājifah*) levels, the second (*ar-Rādifah*) resurrects.

**7. The horn and the "single shout."**

Some Quranic eschatology-passages use *aṣ-Ṣayḥah* (the shout / scream) instead of *aṣ-Ṣūr*. The two seem to refer to *the same audible event*:

[36:53]: "All it will take is one blow, whereupon they are summoned before us." — *in kānat illā ṣayḥatan wāḥidah*. *Just one cry / shout / single sound*.

[50:42]: "When they hear the inevitable cry; that is the day you come out." — *yawma yasma`ūna ṣ-ṣayḥata bi-l-ḥaqq*. *The cry of truth*.

These verses suggest that the *horn-blast* and *the shout* are *not* two different events but *the same auditory phenomenon* described by *different terms*.

**8. The horn and Quran-eschatology coherence.**

The horn is the *transition-event* between *this world* and *the resurrection*. It is named so consistently across the Meccan and Medinan corpus that it functions as a *fixed eschatological marker*:

(a) It *terminates* the present cosmic order ([39:68] first blast).

(b) It *initiates* the resurrection ([39:68] second blast, [36:51]).

(c) It is *audible to all* — those in heavens and earth ([39:68], [27:87]).

(d) Some are *spared* the unconsciousness — *those God wills* ([39:68], [27:87]).

(e) The interval between blasts is *not specified* by the Quran.

**Synthesis.**

The horn (*aṣ-Ṣūr*) is the *audible inauguration* of the Day of Resurrection in the Quran, appearing in ten places. The canonical structure: *one blast* causes universal unconsciousness; *a second* causes universal rising ([39:68]). All in heavens and earth fall except those God spares ([39:68]); all rise terrified except those God protects ([27:87]). Family ties dissolve ([23:101]); the disbelievers rise *blue* ([20:102]) and *throng-like* ([78:18]). The *blower* is unnamed in the Quran; the *equivalent term* *aṣ-ṣayḥah* (the shout) is sometimes used for the same event. The horn closes the present cosmic order and inaugurates the eschatological accounting.

question: What does the Quran say about the scales (mizan) of deeds?
answer:
*Al-Mīzān* — "the scale / balance" — in the Quran has two intertwined uses: a *physical scale* by which humans must conduct just commerce, and an *eschatological scale* on which deeds are weighed on the Day of Resurrection. The two uses are theologically connected: the *daily marketplace scale* is the *training ground* for the *cosmic scale*.

**1. The cosmic scale established with the heavens.**

[55:7]–[55:9]: "He constructed the sky and established the law. You shall not transgress the law. You shall establish justice; do not violate the law." — *wa-s-samā'a rafa`ahā wa-waḍa`a l-mīzān. Allā taṭghaw fī l-mīzān. Wa-aqīmū l-wazna bi-l-qisṭi wa-lā tukhsirū l-mīzān*.

*He raised the sky and set the balance. Do not transgress in the balance. Establish weight in equity and do not reduce the balance*. Three verses in sequence, three clauses of the *mīzān* — established simultaneously with the *sky's raising*, prohibited from *transgression*, requiring *just-establishment*.

The verse-cluster is one of the Quran's most poetically condensed statements of *cosmic order = moral order*. The same *mīzān* that holds the sky is the *mīzān* you operate at the grain-shop.

**2. The scale sent down with the messengers.**

[57:25]: "We sent our messengers supported by clear proofs, and we sent down to them the scripture and the law, that the people may uphold justice. And we sent down the iron, wherein there is strength, and many benefits for the people. All this in order for GOD to distinguish those who would support Him and His messengers, on faith. GOD is Powerful, Almighty." — *anzalnā ma`ahumu l-kitāba wa-l-Mīzān*. *We sent down with them the book and the balance*. The verse explicitly pairs *book* and *balance* — *messengers come with both scripture and the standard for weighing*.

[42:17]: "GOD has sent down the scripture, with the truth, as well as the balance (of justice). For all that you know, the Hour may be just around the corner." — *anzala l-kitāba bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-l-Mīzān*. Same pairing.

**3. The Day of Resurrection scale.**

[7:8]–[7:9]: "The scales will be set on that day, equitably. Those whose weights are heavy will be the winners. As for those whose weights are light, they will be the ones who lost their souls, because they disregarded our revelations, unjustly." — *al-waznu yawma'idhin al-ḥaqq*. *The weighing on that day is in truth*. The verse is the foundational statement of *eschatological weighing*.

[21:47]: "We will establish the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection. No soul will suffer the least injustice. Even the equivalent of a mustard seed will be accounted for. We are the most efficient reckoners." — *al-mawāzīn al-qisṭ*. *The balances of equity*. Even the *mustard seed* (*khardal*) — the smallest reckoning unit — is *accounted for*. The verse is *the* most-cited statement of *exact-measure recompense*.

**4. The two outcomes.**

[23:101]–[23:103]: "When the horn is blown, no relationships among them will exist on that day, nor will they care about one another. Those whose weights are heavy will be the winners. Those whose weights are light will be the ones who lost their souls; they abide in Hell forever." — *fa-man thaqulat mawāzīnuhū* (*heavy weights*) → *winners*. *Wa-man khaffat mawāzīnuhū* (*light weights*) → *Hell*.

[101:6]–[101:9]: surah 101 (Al-Qāri`ah — "The Shocker") closes with the *scale-verdict*: "As for him whose weights are heavy. He will lead a happy (eternal) life. As for him whose weights are light. His destiny is the abyss." — *fa-ammā man thaqulat mawāzīnuhū fa-huwa fī `īshatin rāḍiyah. wa-ammā man khaffat mawāzīnuhū fa-ummuhū hāwiyah*. *His mother is the abyss* — the abyss *embraces him*.

[101:10]–[101:11]: "And what an abyss it is! It is the blazing Hellfire." — *wa-mā adrāka mā hiyah. Nārun ḥāmiyah*.

**5. The "heavy weights" — what makes the scale tip?**

The Quran does not provide a complete enumeration of *what specifically weighs heavy*, but multiple verses suggest the contents:

(a) *Tawḥīd* itself ([10:3]).

(b) *Charity to the orphan, the deprived, the poor* ([76:8]–[76:9], [90:13]–[90:16]).

(c) *Salāt* and *Zakāt* ([2:177], [98:5]).

(d) *Patience in adversity* ([39:10]).

(e) *Honest speech and refraining from gossip* ([4:148]–[4:149], [49:11]–[49:12]).

(f) *Fasting* ([2:183]–[2:185]).

The general principle: *intentional acts of righteousness done for God's sake weigh*; acts done for show or with insincere intent do not.

**6. The proportionality.**

[6:160]: "Whoever does a good work receives the reward for ten, and the one who commits a sin is requited only for one. No one suffers the slightest injustice." — *the ten-to-one ratio* between good and bad — built into the scale's calibration.

**7. The non-equality of acts.**

[39:10]: "Say, 'O My servants who believed, you shall reverence your Lord. For those who worked righteousness in this world, a good reward. GOD's earth is spacious, and those who steadfastly persevere will receive their recompense generously, without limits.'" — *those-who-persevere* are paid *without account* (*bi-ghayri ḥisāb*) — a *category beyond the scale*. The scale weighs the routine; *patience* receives an open reward.

**8. The marketplace ethics connection.**

The same word — *mīzān* — appears in commercial-ethics contexts:

[83:1]–[83:3]: "Woe to the cheaters. Who demand full measure when receiving from the people. But when giving them the measures or weights, they cheat." — *the cheaters in the scale* (*muṭaffifīn*) — the marketplace short-changers. The Quranic ethic: *the merchant's scale is itself the audit-rehearsal for the eschatological scale*.

[55:8]: *do not transgress in the balance* — and at the same time:

[55:9]: *and do not reduce the balance*. Both the *over-asking* (when receiving) and the *under-giving* (when paying out) are *transgressions of the mīzān*.

**Synthesis.**

The *mīzān* in the Quran is *both physical and cosmic*: God *set the balance* alongside the sky's raising ([55:7]); *messengers come with the book and the balance* ([57:25], [42:17]); *the cheaters in the marketplace* are *woe-d* ([83:1]–[83:3]); on the Day, *the weighing is in truth* ([7:8]); *even a mustard seed is accounted for* ([21:47]); *heavy weights win* ([23:102], [101:6]–[101:7]); *light weights end in the abyss* ([23:103], [101:8]–[101:11]); the *good-to-bad ratio* is *ten-to-one* ([6:160]); and *the patient receive their reward without account* ([39:10]). The training ground for the *eschatological scale* is the *daily commercial scale* — *do not transgress, do not reduce*.

question: Does the Quran mention the bridge over Hell (sirat)?
answer:
This is one of the questions where the Quran's content and the *post-Quranic tradition* diverge significantly. The *bridge over Hell* (*aṣ-Ṣirāṭ* in the post-Quranic sense — *a narrow bridge stretched over Gehenna that the resurrected must cross, falling into the fire if their deeds are insufficient*) is a *Hadith-derived* doctrine and does *not* appear in the Quran in this specific form. The Quran does use the word *ṣirāṭ*, but for *the path*, not for *a bridge over hell*.

**1. *Ṣirāṭ* in the Quran — *the path*.**

[1:6]: "Guide us in the right path." — *ihdinā ṣ-ṣirāṭa l-mustaqīm*. The *straight path* — the petition of Al-Fātiḥah.

[1:7]: "The path of those whom You blessed; not of those who have deserved wrath, nor of the strayers." — *ṣirāṭa lladhīna an`amta `alayhim*. *The path of those whom You blessed*.

[6:153]: "And this is My path — a straight one. You shall follow it, and do not follow any other paths, lest they divert you from His path." — *hādhā ṣirāṭī mustaqīman*. *This is My straight path*.

The Quranic *ṣirāṭ* is consistently *the path one walks in this life and at the resurrection-day approach* — *not* a bridge that is *crossed* over hell.

**2. The *exposure* to Hell — the closest Quranic image.**

[19:71]–[19:72]: "Every single one of you must see it. This is an immutable decree of your Lord. Then we rescue the righteous, and leave the transgressors in it, humiliated." — *wa-in minkum illā wāriduhā*. *None of you but will pass over/to it*. *Then we save those who are reverent and leave the wrongdoers in it on their knees*.

The verse-pair is the *closest Quranic approximation* of a *passage-over-hell* image. Every soul *will be brought to it* (*wāriduhā* — "will arrive at it / pass by it"); then the *reverent* are *rescued out*; the *wrongdoers* are *left in*.

The verb *warada* (to come to, to arrive at, to approach water) is ambiguous between *passing over*, *passing through*, *being brought to the edge*. Khalifa renders *every single one of you must see it* — the *exposure*-reading rather than the *crossing*-reading.

**3. The judgment-stations.**

[37:23]–[37:24]: "Beside GOD, and guide them to the path of Hell. Stop them; they must be asked." — *wa-qifūhum innahum mas'ūlūn*. *Halt them — they must be questioned*. The verse establishes a *station-stop* at the judgment — *they are halted, they are interrogated* — before the *fire-or-paradise* allocation.

[37:62]–[37:65]: Description of the *Zaqqūm tree*, food of the dwellers of hell — *the head of devils*. The Quranic *hell-descent* is described *direct* — *thrown into it*, *led to it*, *burnt in it* — without a *bridge* intermediary.

**4. The *humbled-marched* image.**

[36:66]: "If we will, we can veil their eyes and, consequently, when they seek the path, they will not see." — *the path-not-seen* image: the wicked, on that day, *cannot find* the path.

**5. The summary — Quran vs. post-Quranic tradition.**

In Hadith literature (e.g., Bukhārī, Muslim), *aṣ-Ṣirāṭ* takes on a specific developed form: *a bridge thinner than a hair and sharper than a sword, stretched over Gehenna*, which the resurrected must cross. Those *with sufficient faith and deeds* cross *as quickly as lightning or wind*; those *without* fall into the fire below. The believers carry their *deeds-as-light* across.

This image is *not* in the Quran. It is *constructed from*:

(a) The Quranic *ṣirāṭ* (path).

(b) The Quranic *every soul will come to it / pass over it* ([19:71]).

(c) The Quranic *light-of-believers running ahead* ([57:12]: *yas`ā nūruhum bayna aydīhim* — *their light running in front of them and on their right*).

(d) Hadith-tradition *combining and dramatizing* the elements.

For Khalifa's framework, which holds *the Quran alone* as religious source, the *bridge-over-hell* tradition is *not authoritative* — it is a *post-Quranic* elaboration. What the *Quran* establishes is:

(a) *Every soul will come to hell / pass over to it* ([19:71]).

(b) The *believers are then rescued out*; the *wrongdoers left in* ([19:72]).

(c) There is *halting and interrogation* before the final allocation ([37:24]).

(d) The *light of the believers runs ahead of them* ([57:12]).

The *bridge-imagery* — *narrowness, sharpness, falling* — is *not* in the Quran.

**6. The Quranic *ṣirāṭ* metaphor.**

The Quranic emphasis is on *the path one walks in this life*. *Ihdinā ṣ-ṣirāṭa l-mustaqīm* is the prayer of every prayer-cycle: *guide us to the straight path*. *The path* is the *Quranic-given guidance*, the *Prophet's example as transmitted in the Quran*, and the *cumulative obedience to God's revealed instructions*. The Quranic *ṣirāṭ* is a *life-long walking path*, not a *moment-of-judgment crossing*.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran *does not mention a bridge over Hell* in the developed form of post-Quranic tradition. The Quran's *ṣirāṭ* is *the straight path one walks in this life* — Al-Fātiḥah's central petition ([1:6]–[1:7]) and the explicit instruction at [6:153]. The *closest Quranic image* of a *judgment-passage* is [19:71]–[19:72]: *every one of you will come to it, then we rescue the reverent and leave the wrongdoers in it*. The *bridge-imagery* is *Hadith-derived*; the Khalifa framework, which limits source to the Quran, treats it as *post-Quranic elaboration* rather than *Quranic doctrine*. What is Quranically established: *every soul approaches hell, the righteous are rescued, the wrongdoers are left*.

1:6.text: Guide us in the right path;

1:7.arabic: صرط الذين أنعمت عليهم غير المغضوب عليهم ولا الضالين

question: What does the Quran say about the angel of death?
answer:
The Quran's treatment of the *angel of death* is consistent and minimal: there is *a single named station* — *malak al-mawt* — *the angel of death* — assigned to *each soul*, paired with assistant angels who *extract the soul* differently depending on the soul's state.

**1. The named station.**

[32:11]: "Say, 'You will be put to death by the angel in whose charge you are placed, then to your Lord you will be returned.'" — *qul yatawaffākum malaku l-mawti lladhī wukkila bikum*. *The angel of death who is assigned to you*. The verse is the *only* Quranic verse using the *exact phrase* *malak al-mawt* — *the angel of death*.

The verse establishes three things:

(a) Death is *administered by an angel*, not a chaotic process.

(b) Each soul has *an angel assigned to it* (*wukkila bikum*).

(c) The destination is *return to your Lord* — death is *transition*, not termination.

**2. The plural-extractors.**

[79:1]–[79:2]: "The (angels who) snatch (the souls of the disbelievers) forcibly. And those who gently take (the souls of the believers) joyfully." — *wa-n-Nāzi`āti gharqā / wa-n-Nāshiṭāti nashṭā*. The first oath of surah 79 — the *snatching ones* (forcibly) and the *gentle ones* (kindly). Khalifa identifies these as the *two-mode* angels: *forceful extraction* for the disbelievers, *gentle drawing* for the believers.

**3. The forceful extraction — disbelievers.**

[6:93]: "Who is more evil than one who fabricates lies and attributes them to GOD, or says, 'I have received divine inspiration,' when no such inspiration was given to him, or says, 'I can write the same as GOD's revelations'? If only you could see the transgressors at the time of death! The angels extend their hands to them, saying, 'Let go of your souls. Today, you have incurred a shameful retribution for saying about GOD other than the truth, and for being too arrogant to accept His revelations.'" — *bāsiṭū aydīhim*. *The angels extending their hands* — the *cry-of-extraction*: *let go your souls*.

[8:50]: "If you could only see those who disbelieved when the angels put them to death! They will beat them on their faces and their rear ends: 'Taste the retribution of Hell.'" — the *physical-violence* of the death-process for the disbelievers.

[47:27]: "How will it be for them when the angels put them to death, beating their faces and their backs?"

**4. The gentle extraction — believers.**

[16:28]: "The angels put them to death in a state of wronging their souls. That is when they finally submit, saying, 'We did not do anything wrong!' Yes indeed. GOD is fully aware of everything you have done." — the *late submission* of the dying.

[16:32]: "The angels put them to death in a state of righteousness, saying, 'Peace be upon you. Enter Paradise (now), as a reward for your works.'" — *tatawaffāhumu l-malā'ikatu ṭayyibīn yaqūlūna salāmun `alaykum*. *The angels take their souls in a state of goodness, saying: peace upon you*. The verse is the *paradigmatic* believer-death-scene: *peace upon you, enter paradise*.

**5. The universal application.**

[3:185]: "Every soul will taste death, then you will receive your recompense on the Day of Resurrection." — *kullu nafsin dhā'iqatu l-mawt*. *Every soul will taste death*. The universality.

[21:35]: same formula.

[29:57]: "Every person will taste death, then to us you will be ultimately returned." — *thumma ilaynā turja`ūn*. *Then to Us you are returned*.

**6. The temporary-state in death.**

[6:60]: "He is the One who puts you to death during the night, and knows even the smallest of your actions during the day. He resurrects you every morning, until your life span is fulfilled, then to Him is your ultimate return. He will then inform you of everything you had done." — *yatawaffākum bi-l-layli*. *He takes you in (sleep) at night, then resurrects you in the day*. The verse treats *sleep* as a *temporary death* — a *small dress-rehearsal* of the eschatological death. (Cf. [39:42] on this same theme.)

**7. The dying-bed scene from the dying disbeliever's perspective.**

[23:99]–[23:100]: "When death comes to one of them, he says, 'My Lord, send me back. I will then work righteousness in everything I left.' Not true. This is a false claim that he makes. A barrier will separate his soul from this world until resurrection." — the *post-extraction request* — *send me back* — is *categorically refused*.

[63:10]: "You shall give from our provisions to you before death comes to you, then you say, 'My Lord, if only You could delay this for a short while! I would then be charitable and join the righteous!'" — the *deathbed-regret-of-the-stingy*.

[63:11]: "GOD never delays the appointed time of death for any soul." — *lan yu'akhkhira llāhu nafsan idhā jā'a ajaluhā*. *God will not delay any soul when its term has come*.

**8. The summary.**

The Quran's *angel of death* is *named singularly* but *operates as a station* served by multiple angels in different modes:

(a) *Malak al-mawt* — the chief assigned angel ([32:11]).

(b) *Forceful extractors* (*nāzi`āt*) — for disbelievers ([79:1]).

(c) *Gentle drawers* (*nāshiṭāt*) — for believers ([79:2]).

(d) *Angels with extended hands* commanding the disbeliever to *yield the soul* ([6:93]).

(e) *Angels of peace-greeting* welcoming the believer to paradise ([16:32]).

Classical Islam *names* the chief angel *`Izrā'īl* — but this name is *not* in the Quran (it is in the Hadith corpus). For Khalifa's Quran-alone framework, the angel is simply *the angel of death who is assigned to you*.

**9. The two-witness pairing.**

[50:21]: "Every soul comes with a herder and a witness." — *sā'iqun wa-shahīd*. *A driver and a witness*. Classical exegesis identifies *the herder* with *the angel of death* and *the witness* with *the recording angel*. The two angels assigned to each soul *meet again* at the resurrection-day standing.

**Synthesis.**

The angel of death (*malak al-mawt*) is *assigned to each soul* ([32:11]); the death-process is *administered by angels*, with *gentle extraction* for believers (*peace upon you, enter paradise* [16:32]) and *forceful extraction* for disbelievers (*let go your souls; you incurred shameful retribution* [6:93]); the *snatchers* and *gentle drawers* of [79:1]–[79:2] preserve this *two-mode* operation; *every soul will taste death* ([3:185]); the *post-extraction return-request* is *refused* ([23:100]); and the *driver and witness* of [50:21] are paired with each soul as it stands for judgment.

question: How is the Contact Prayer (Salat) structured in the Quran?
answer:
The Quran's instructions on *Ṣalāt* (Contact Prayer) cover *its number, timing, prerequisites, manner, and posture* — but with significantly less procedural detail than the developed post-Quranic ritual. The Quran establishes *five prayers, the times, the ablution, and the posture*; *the exact number of cycles per prayer* is *not* specified in the Quranic text alone.

**1. The number of prayers — five, derived from time-statements.**

The Quran does not state "*five prayers*" in those words; it specifies *five times* across three key verses:

(a) *Dawn prayer (Fajr)* — at [11:114] and [24:58]: *the dawn prayer / before sunrise*.

(b) *Noon prayer (Ẓuhr / al-Wusṭā)* — at [2:238]: "*the middle prayer*" — *aṣ-Ṣalātu l-Wusṭā*.

(c) *Afternoon prayer (`Aṣr)* — implicit in [2:238] as *one of the prayers* + at [11:114]'s *the day's ends*.

(d) *Sunset prayer (Maghrib)* — at [11:114] and [17:78]: *night fall*.

(e) *Night prayer (`Ishā')* — at [17:78]: *after night fall* + [24:58]: *after the `ishā'*.

The verse [17:78] gives the canonical time-frame: "You shall observe the contact prayer when the sun declines from its highest point at noon, as it moves towards sunset. You shall also observe the contact prayer at dawn. Reading the Quran at dawn is witnessed." — *aqimi ṣ-ṣalāta li-dulūki sh-shamsi ilā ghasaqi l-layli wa-qur'āna l-fajr*. *Establish the prayer from the sun's decline to night-fall, and the dawn-Quran*.

**2. The middle prayer instruction.**

[2:238]: "You shall consistently observe the contact prayers, especially the middle prayer, and devote yourselves totally to GOD." — *ḥāfiẓū `alā ṣ-ṣalawāti wa-ṣ-ṣalāti l-wusṭā*. *Maintain the prayers and the middle prayer*. The verse mentions the *prayers* in the plural and *singles out* the *middle one* for emphasis.

**3. The dawn prayer as witnessed.**

[17:78]: "...Reading the Quran at dawn is witnessed." — *qur'āna l-fajri kāna mashhūdā*. The verse names *the recitation at the dawn-prayer* as *witnessed by angels*.

**4. The ablution (Wudū').**

[5:6]: "O you who believe, when you observe the contact prayers (Salat), you shall: (1) wash your faces, (2) wash your arms to the elbows, (3) wipe your heads, and (4) wash your feet to the ankles. If you were unclean due to sexual orgasm, you shall bathe. If you are ill, or traveling, or had any digestive excretion (urinary, fecal, or gas), or had (sexual) contact with the women, and you cannot find water, you shall observe the dry ablution by touching clean dry soil, then rubbing your faces and hands." — the *four-step wudū'*: (a) wash face, (b) wash arms to elbows, (c) wipe the head, (d) wash feet to ankles. *Tayammum* — dry ablution with clean soil — is the substitute when water is unavailable.

The verse continues with the requirement for *ghusl* (full bath) after *seminal emission* or *sexual contact*.

[4:43] adds the instruction *not to pray drunk* or *in a state of janābah* (unwashed after sexual contact) *except in passing through* the prayer-place — until ablution is performed.

**5. The posture — bowing and prostration.**

[2:43]: "You shall observe the contact prayers (Salat) and give the obligatory charity (Zakat), and bow down with those who bow down." — *wa-rka`ū ma`a r-rāki`īn*. *Bow with those who bow*. The verse pairs *prayer* with *bowing*.

[22:77]: "O you who believe, you shall bow, prostrate, worship your Lord, and work righteousness, that you may succeed." — *yā ayyuhā lladhīna āmanū rka`ū wa-sjudū*. *Bow and prostrate*. The two postures of prayer named explicitly: *rukū`* (bowing) and *sujūd* (prostration).

**6. The volume control.**

[17:110]: "Say, 'Call upon GOD, or call upon the Most Gracious; whichever name you use, to Him belongs the best names.' You shall not utter your contact prayers too loudly, nor secretly; use a moderate tone." — *moderate voice* in prayer.

**7. The orientation — Qibla.**

[2:144]: "We have seen you turning your face about the sky. We now assign a Qiblah that is pleasing to you. Henceforth, you shall turn your face towards the Sacred Masjid. Wherever you may be, all of you shall turn your faces towards it." — the *Qibla* is *Masjid al-Ḥarām* in Mecca. The verse establishes the *physical orientation* of prayer.

**8. The *content* — recitation.**

[17:78] and [73:20] establish that *Quran-recitation* is *part of the prayer*. Surah 1 (Al-Fātiḥah) is, by Prophetic tradition, *recited in every cycle* — though this is *Hadith-tradition*, not Quranic text directly. The Quran establishes that *recitation is part of the prayer* without specifying *which surah*.

**9. The night-prayer (Tahajjud).**

[17:79]: "During the night, you shall meditate for extra credit, that your Lord may raise you to an honorable rank." — the *optional* night-prayer, distinct from the *five obligatory*.

[73:20]: extensive instruction on *qiyām al-layl* — the standing of the night, *recite from the Quran what is easy* — and the *concession* for those for whom *standing is difficult* (the sick, the travelers, those striving in God's cause).

**10. The Friday Salat.**

[62:9]: "O you who believe, when the Congregational Prayer (Salat Al-Jumu`ah) is announced on Friday, you shall hasten to the commemoration of GOD, and drop all business." — the *Friday Salat* is *named explicitly*. Business *stops* when the call sounds.

**11. The function.**

[29:45]: "You shall recite what is revealed to you of the scripture, and observe the contact prayers, for the contact prayers prohibit evil and vice. But the remembrance of GOD (through Salat) is the most important objective. GOD knows everything you do." — *inna ṣ-ṣalāta tanhā `ani l-faḥshā'i wa-l-munkar*. *The prayer prohibits indecency and evil*. The verse names the *moral-formation function* of the prayer.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran structures the Contact Prayer (*Ṣalāt*) through: *five timed prayers* derived from [11:114], [17:78], [2:238]; *the middle prayer* singled out at [2:238]; *the four-step ablution* at [5:6]; *the dry ablution alternative* at [5:6] and [4:43]; *the bow and prostration postures* at [22:77]; *the moderate volume* at [17:110]; *the Qibla orientation toward Mecca* at [2:144]; *recitation as part* at [17:78]; *the optional night-prayer (Tahajjud)* at [17:79] and [73:20]; *the Friday congregational prayer* at [62:9]; and *the moral-formation function* — *the prayer prohibits indecency and evil* — at [29:45]. The exact *number of cycles per prayer* (the *raka`āt* count) is *not* specified in the Quranic text and comes from the Prophet's *practice* preserved in early Muslim consensus.

2:43.text: You shall observe the Contact Prayers (Salat) and give the obligatory charity (Zakat), and bow down with those who bow down.
2:43.arabic: وأقيموا الصلوة وءاتوا الزكوة واركعوا مع الرـكعين

4:103.text: Once you complete your Contact Prayer (Salat), you shall remember GOD while standing, sitting, or lying down. Once the war is over, you shall observe the Contact Prayers (Salat); the Contact Prayers (Salat) are decreed for the believers at specific times.

4:43.text: O you who believe, do not observe the Contact Prayers (Salat) while intoxicated, so that you know what you are saying. Nor after sexual orgasm without bathing, unless you are on the road, traveling; if you are ill or traveling, or you had urinary or fecal-related excretion (such as gas), or contacted the women (sexually), and you cannot find water, you shall observe Tayammum (dry ablution) by touching clean dry soil, then wiping your faces and hands therewith. GOD is Pardoner, Forgiver.
4:43.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا لا تقربوا الصلوة وأنتم سكرى حتى تعلموا ما تقولون ولا جنبا إلا عابري سبيل حتى تغتسلوا وإن كنتم مرضى أو على سفر أو جاء أحد منكم من الغائط أو لمستم النساء فلم تجدوا ماء فتيمموا صعيدا طيبا فامسحوا بوجوهكم وأيديكم إن الله كان عفوا غفورا

5:6.text: O you who believe, when you observe the Contact Prayers (Salat), you shall: (1) wash your faces, (2) wash your arms to the elbows, (3) wipe your heads, and (4) wash your feet to the ankles. If you were unclean due to sexual orgasm, you shall bathe. If you are ill, or traveling, or had any digestive excretion (urinary, fecal, or gas), or had (sexual) contact with the women, and you cannot find water, you shall observe the dry ablution (Tayammum) by touching clean dry soil, then rubbing your faces and hands. GOD does not wish to make the religion difficult for you; He wishes to cleanse you and to perfect His blessing upon you, that you may be appreciative.
5:6.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا إذا قمتم إلى الصلوة فاغسلوا وجوهكم وأيديكم إلى المرافق وامسحوا برءوسكم وأرجلكم إلى الكعبين وإن كنتم جنبا فاطهروا وإن كنتم مرضى أو على سفر أو جاء أحد منكم من الغائط أو لمستم النساء فلم تجدوا ماء فتيمموا صعيدا طيبا فامسحوا بوجوهكم وأيديكم منه ما يريد الله ليجعل عليكم من حرج ولكن يريد ليطهركم وليتم نعمته عليكم لعلكم تشكرون

11:114.text: You shall observe the Contact Prayers (Salat) at both ends of the day, and during the night. The righteous works wipe out the evil works. This is a reminder for those who would take heed.
11:114.arabic: وأقم الصلوة طرفي النهار وزلفا من اليل إن الحسنت يذهبن السيـات ذلك ذكرى للذـكرين

17:78.text: You shall observe the Contact Prayer (Salat) when the sun declines from its highest point at noon, as it moves towards sunset. You shall also observe (the recitation of) the Quran at dawn. (Reciting) the Quran at dawn is witnessed.
17:78.arabic: أقم الصلوة لدلوك الشمس إلى غسق اليل وقرءان الفجر إن قرءان الفجر كان مشهودا

17:79.text: During the night, you shall meditate for extra credit, that your Lord may raise you to an honorable rank.
17:79.arabic: ومن اليل فتهجد به نافلة لك عسى أن يبعثك ربك مقاما محمودا

17:110.text: Say, "Call Him GOD, or call Him the Most Gracious; whichever name you use, to Him belongs the best names." You shall not utter your Contact Prayers (Salat) too loudly, nor secretly; use a moderate tone.
17:110.arabic: قل ادعوا الله أو ادعوا الرحمن أيا ما تدعوا فله الأسماء الحسنى ولا تجهر بصلاتك ولا تخافت بها وابتغ بين ذلك سبيلا

20:14.text: "I am GOD; there is no other god beside Me. You shall worship Me alone, and observe the Contact Prayers (Salat) to remember Me. End of the World is Not Hidden
20:14.arabic: إنني أنا الله لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدني وأقم الصلوة لذكري

20:130.text: Therefore, be patient in the face of their utterances, and praise and glorify your Lord before sunrise and before sunset. And during the night glorify Him, as well as at both ends of the day, that you may be happy.
20:130.arabic: فاصبر على ما يقولون وسبح بحمد ربك قبل طلوع الشمس وقبل غروبها ومن ءاناي اليل فسبح وأطراف النهار لعلك ترضى

22:77.text: O you who believe, you shall bow, prostrate, worship your Lord, and work righteousness, that you may succeed. Abraham: Original Messenger of Islam
22:77.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا اركعوا واسجدوا واعبدوا ربكم وافعلوا الخير لعلكم تفلحون۩

29:45.text: You shall recite what is revealed to you of the scripture, and observe the Contact Prayers (Salat), for the Contact Prayers prohibit evil and vice. But the remembrance of GOD (through Salat) is the most important objective. GOD knows everything you do.
29:45.arabic: اتل ما أوحي إليك من الكتب وأقم الصلوة إن الصلوة تنهى عن الفحشاء والمنكر ولذكر الله أكبر والله يعلم ما تصنعون

62:9.text: O you who believe, when the Congregational Prayer (Salat Al- Jumu`ah) is announced on Friday, you shall hasten to the commemoration of GOD, and drop all business. This is better for you, if you only knew.
62:9.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا إذا نودي للصلوة من يوم الجمعة فاسعوا إلى ذكر الله وذروا البيع ذلكم خير لكم إن كنتم تعلمون

73:20.text: Your Lord knows that you meditate during two-thirds of the night, or half of it, or one-third of it, and so do some of those who believed with you. GOD has designed the night and the day, and He knows that you cannot always do this. He has pardoned you. Instead, you shall read what you can of the Quran. He knows that some of you may be ill, others may be traveling in pursuit of GOD's provisions, and others may be striving in the cause of GOD. You shall read what you can of it, and observe the contact prayers (Salat), give the obligatory charity (Zakat), and lend GOD a loan of righteousness. Whatever good you send ahead on behalf of your souls, you will find it at GOD far better and generously rewarded. And implore GOD for forgiveness. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful.
73:20.arabic: ۞إن ربك يعلم أنك تقوم أدنى من ثلثي اليل ونصفه وثلثه وطائفة من الذين معك والله يقدر اليل والنهار علم أن لن تحصوه فتاب عليكم فاقرءوا ما تيسر من القرءان علم أن سيكون منكم مرضى وءاخرون يضربون في الأرض يبتغون من فضل الله وءاخرون يقتلون في سبيل الله فاقرءوا ما تيسر منه وأقيموا الصلوة وءاتوا الزكوة وأقرضوا الله قرضا حسنا وما تقدموا لأنفسكم من خير تجدوه عند الله هو خيرا وأعظم أجرا واستغفروا الله إن الله غفور رحيم

98:5.text: All that was asked of them was to worship GOD, devoting the religion absolutely to Him alone, observe the contact prayers (Salat), and give the obligatory charity (Zakat). Such is the perfect religion.
98:5.arabic: وما أمروا إلا ليعبدوا الله مخلصين له الدين حنفاء ويقيموا الصلوة ويؤتوا الزكوة وذلك دين القيمة

question: What does the Quran specify about zakat — who pays, who receives?
answer:
*Zakāt* in the Quran — usually rendered *the obligatory charity* — is named *32 times*, almost always paired with *Ṣalāt*. The Quran identifies *who must give*, *who receives*, *what is given*, and *the spiritual function* — though the *exact rate* (the famous 2.5% of accumulated wealth) is *not* in the Quranic text directly; it comes from the Prophet's *practice* as transmitted by early consensus.

**1. The pairing with Salat.**

The phrase *aqāmū ṣ-ṣalāta wa-ātaw z-zakāh* — *they establish the prayer and give the obligatory charity* — recurs throughout the Quran ([2:43], [2:177], [2:277], [9:18], [9:71], [22:41], [22:78], [24:56], [27:3], [31:4], [58:13], [98:5]). The pairing is so consistent that the two duties function as a *single double-act* of *vertical worship + horizontal economy*.

**2. The eight categories of recipients.**

[9:60] is the most detailed Quranic statement of *who receives*: "Charities shall go to the poor, the needy, the workers who collect them, the new converts, to free the slaves, to those burdened by sudden expenses, in the cause of GOD, and to the traveling alien. Such is GOD's commandment. GOD is Omniscient, Most Wise." — *innamā ṣ-ṣadaqātu li-l-fuqarā'i wa-l-masākīni wa-l-`āmilīna `alayhā wa-l-mu'allafati qulūbuhum wa-fī r-riqāb wa-l-ghārimīna wa-fī sabīli llāhi wa-bni s-sabīl*.

The *eight named categories*:

(a) *Al-Fuqarā'* — the poor (those without means).

(b) *Al-Masākīn* — the needy / destitute (different shade — *miskīn* implies *one whose poverty is hidden*).

(c) *Al-`Āmilīna `alayhā* — those *who collect* the zakāt (administration).

(d) *Al-Mu'allafati qulūbuhum* — those whose *hearts are to be reconciled* (new converts; people moving toward Islam).

(e) *Fī r-riqāb* — *in the necks* (slave-emancipation).

(f) *Al-Ghārimīn* — *those burdened with debt* (especially unforeseen debts from disaster, illness, etc.).

(g) *Fī sabīli llāh* — *in God's cause* (broadly understood: public works, defense, education, evangelism).

(h) *Ibn as-Sabīl* — *the son of the road* (the traveler / wayfarer stranded without resources).

This is the *Quranic-fixed* list. No category may be added; none may be subtracted.

**3. The voluntary additional recipients.**

[2:215]: "They ask you about giving: say, 'The charity you give shall go to the parents, the relatives, the orphans, the poor, and the traveling alien.' Any good you do, GOD is fully aware thereof."

[2:177] expands the *normative recipients* in the *righteousness-definition*: "Righteousness is... to give the money, cheerfully, to the relatives, the orphans, the needy, the traveling alien, the beggars, and to free the slaves..."

These verses describe *charity in general* — both the *obligatory zakāt* and the *voluntary ṣadaqah*. The categories overlap with [9:60]'s zakāt-list but extend to *parents*, *relatives*, *orphans*, *beggars*.

**4. What is given.**

[2:267]: "O you who believe, you shall give to charity from the good things you earn, and from what we have produced for you from the earth. Do not pick out the bad therein to give away, when you yourselves do not accept it unless your eyes are closed. You should know that GOD is Rich, Praiseworthy." — *give from the good things*; *do not give what you yourselves wouldn't accept*. The *quality-control* clause: *you cannot palm off your worst on the poor*.

[30:39]: "The usury that is practiced to increase some people's wealth, does not gain anything at GOD. But if you give to charity, seeking GOD's pleasure, these are the ones who receive their reward many fold." — contrasts *ribā* (usury) with *zakāt*: *usury does not grow with God; charity multiplies*.

**5. The *known portion* in wealth.**

[70:24]–[70:25]: "Part of their money is set aside. For the poor and the needy." — *fī amwālihim ḥaqqun ma`lūm li-s-sā'ili wa-l-maḥrūm*. *A known portion in their wealth is for the asker and the deprived*. The phrase *ḥaqq ma`lūm* — *a known right / known portion* — is one of the Quran's strongest framings of zakāt: *not a favor, but a right*. The *asker* (*sā'il*) is *one who asks*; the *deprived* (*maḥrūm*) is *one who needs but does not ask*.

The parallel verse [51:19] uses the same phrase: *and in their money was a right for the asker and the deprived*.

**6. The function — purification.**

[9:103]: "Take from their money a charity to purify them and sanctify them. And encourage them, for your encouragement reassures them. GOD is Hearer, Omniscient." — *khudh min amwālihim ṣadaqatan tuṭahhiruhum wa-tuzakkīhim bihā*. *Take charity from their money to purify and sanctify them with it*. The verb *zakkā* — same root as *zakāt* — means *to purify and grow*. The verse establishes the *etymological-theological* fact: *zakāt* is *the purification of wealth*.

**7. The recipients' dignity.**

[2:273]: "Charity shall go to the poor who are suffering in the cause of GOD, and cannot emigrate. The unaware would think that they are rich, due to their dignity. But you can recognize them by certain signs; they never beg from the people persistently. Whatever charity you give, GOD is fully aware thereof." — the *dignity-preservation* clause: some poor *do not beg*; their need must be recognized *despite their dignity*.

**8. The privacy clause.**

[2:271]: "If you declare your charities, they are still good. But if you keep them secret, and give them to the poor, it is better for you, and remits more of your sins. GOD is fully Cognizant of everything you do." — *if you reveal it, fine; if you conceal it and give to the poor, better*. The verse establishes *concealed* giving as the *higher form*.

**9. The neighbor as recipient.**

[2:215] and [4:36] extend the implicit network: *the neighbor*, *the companion-at-side*, *the stranger* — all named as *recipients of kindness* (though *kindness* in [4:36] is broader than *zakāt*).

**10. Who pays?**

The Quran is *silent on a specific income / wealth threshold*. The *2.5% / nisāb (minimum)* rule is from *post-Quranic ḥadīth and consensus*. The Quranic position is *anyone with means above subsistence* gives, with the *known portion in wealth* ([70:24]) framing.

The post-Quranic developed framework (which Khalifa's frame neither fully endorses nor fully rejects, since the Quran's silence here is *under-specification*, not *contradiction*): 2.5% of accumulated wealth held a full year, with a *nisāb* of approximately the value of 85 grams of gold; agricultural produce at 5-10% depending on irrigation method; livestock with specific thresholds.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran specifies that zakāt: *is paired with Ṣalāt* throughout the scripture; *goes to eight named categories* ([9:60]) — poor, needy, administrators, new converts, slave-emancipation, debt-relief, God's-cause, the wayfarer; *is taken from the good things earned and from agricultural produce* ([2:267]); *is a known right in wealth, not a favor* ([70:24]–[70:25], [51:19]); *purifies and grows the wealth* ([9:103]); *should be given in concealment when possible* ([2:271]); and *its recipients' dignity is to be preserved* ([2:273]). The *specific rate* (the 2.5%) is from post-Quranic consensus on the Prophet's practice; the Quran establishes *the structure, the recipients, the source, and the function*.

2:43.text: You shall observe the Contact Prayers (Salat) and give the obligatory charity (Zakat), and bow down with those who bow down.
2:43.arabic: وأقيموا الصلوة وءاتوا الزكوة واركعوا مع الرـكعين

2:177.text: Righteousness is not turning your faces towards the east or the west. Righteous are those who believe in GOD, the Last Day, the angels, the scripture, and the prophets; and they give the money, cheerfully, to the relatives, the orphans, the needy, the traveling alien, the beggars, and to free the slaves; and they observe the Contact Prayers (Salat) and give the obligatory charity (Zakat); and they keep their word whenever they make a promise; and they steadfastly persevere in the face of persecution, hardship, and war. These are the truthful; these are the righteous.
2:177.arabic: ۞ليس البر أن تولوا وجوهكم قبل المشرق والمغرب ولكن البر من ءامن بالله واليوم الأخر والملـئكة والكتب والنبيـن وءاتى المال على حبه ذوي القربى واليتمى والمسكين وابن السبيل والسائلين وفي الرقاب وأقام الصلوة وءاتى الزكوة والموفون بعهدهم إذا عهدوا والصـبرين في البأساء والضراء وحين البأس أولـئك الذين صدقوا وأولـئك هم المتقون

2:215.text: They ask you about giving: say, "The charity you give shall go to the parents, the relatives, the orphans, the poor, and the traveling alien." Any good you do, GOD is fully aware thereof. Believers: The Ultimate Victors
2:215.arabic: يسـلونك ماذا ينفقون قل ما أنفقتم من خير فللولدين والأقربين واليتمى والمسكين وابن السبيل وما تفعلوا من خير فإن الله به عليم

2:267.text: O you who believe, you shall give to charity from the good things you earn, and from what we have produced for you from the earth. Do not pick out the bad therein to give away, when you yourselves do not accept it unless your eyes are closed. You should know that GOD is Rich, Praiseworthy.
2:267.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا أنفقوا من طيبت ما كسبتم ومما أخرجنا لكم من الأرض ولا تيمموا الخبيث منه تنفقون ولستم بـاخذيه إلا أن تغمضوا فيه واعلموا أن الله غني حميد

2:271.text: If you declare your charities, they are still good. But if you keep them anonymous, and give them to the poor, it is better for you, and remits more of your sins. GOD is fully Cognizant of everything you do. God is the Only One Who Guides
2:271.arabic: إن تبدوا الصدقت فنعما هي وإن تخفوها وتؤتوها الفقراء فهو خير لكم ويكفر عنكم من سيـاتكم والله بما تعملون خبير

2:273.text: Charity shall go to the poor who are suffering in the cause of GOD, and cannot emigrate. The unaware may think that they are rich, due to their dignity. But you can recognize them by certain signs; they never beg from the people persistently. Whatever charity you give, GOD is fully aware thereof.
2:273.arabic: للفقراء الذين أحصروا في سبيل الله لا يستطيعون ضربا في الأرض يحسبهم الجاهل أغنياء من التعفف تعرفهم بسيمهم لا يسـلون الناس إلحافا وما تنفقوا من خير فإن الله به عليم

9:60.text: Charities shall go to the poor, the needy, the workers who collect them, the new converts, to free the slaves, to those burdened by sudden expenses, in the cause of GOD, and to the traveling alien. Such is GOD's commandment. GOD is Omniscient, Most Wise.
9:60.arabic: ۞إنما الصدقت للفقراء والمسكين والعملين عليها والمؤلفة قلوبهم وفي الرقاب والغرمين وفي سبيل الله وابن السبيل فريضة من الله والله عليم حكيم

9:103.text: Take from their money a charity to purify them and sanctify them. And encourage them, for your encouragement reassures them. GOD is Hearer, Omniscient.
9:103.arabic: خذ من أمولهم صدقة تطهرهم وتزكيهم بها وصل عليهم إن صلوتك سكن لهم والله سميع عليم

23:4.text: And they give their obligatory charity (Zakat).

30:39.text: The usury that is practiced to increase some people's wealth, does not gain anything at GOD. But if you give to charity, seeking GOD's pleasure, these are the ones who receive their reward manifold. Who Is Worthy of Worship?
30:39.arabic: وما ءاتيتم من ربا ليربوا في أمول الناس فلا يربوا عند الله وما ءاتيتم من زكوة تريدون وجه الله فأولـئك هم المضعفون

41:7.text: "Who do not give the obligatory charity (Zakat), and with regard to the Hereafter, they are disbelievers."
41:7.arabic: الذين لا يؤتون الزكوة وهم بالأخرة هم كفرون

70:24.text: Part of their money is set aside.

70:25.text: For the poor and the needy.

98:5.arabic: وما أمروا إلا ليعبدوا الله مخلصين له الدين حنفاء ويقيموا الصلوة ويؤتوا الزكوة وذلك دين القيمة

question: How does the Quran describe the practice of fasting?
answer:
The Quran's instructions on fasting (*ṣawm*) cover *Ramadan-fasting* (the obligatory annual fast) and *non-Ramadan fasting* (atonement for specific sins, pilgrimage substitutes, vow-fulfillment). The Ramadan section in surah 2 is the foundational text.

**1. The institution and its purpose.**

[2:183]: "O you who believe, fasting is decreed for you, as it was decreed for those before you, that you may attain salvation." — *kutiba `alaykumu ṣ-ṣiyāmu kamā kutiba `alā lladhīna min qablikum la`allakum tattaqūn*. *Fasting is decreed for you as it was decreed for those before you, that you may attain taqwā*. The purpose: *taqwā* — reverent awareness — is the *operational goal*.

The verse establishes (a) the *continuity* with prior monotheistic communities (Jewish and Christian fasting traditions), (b) *taqwā as the purpose*, not bodily denial as an end in itself.

**2. The duration — Ramadan, the month of the Quran.**

[2:185]: "Ramadan is the month during which the Quran was revealed, providing guidance for the people, clear teachings, and the statute book. Those of you who witness this month shall fast therein. Those who are ill or traveling may substitute the same number of other days. GOD wishes for you convenience, not hardship, that you may fulfill your obligations, and to glorify GOD for guiding you, and to express your appreciation."

The verse establishes:

(a) *Ramadan is the month of the Quran's revelation* — *shahru Ramaḍāna lladhī unzila fīhi l-Qur'ān*.

(b) *Those who witness the month fast it* — *fa-man shahida minkumu sh-shahra fa-l-yaṣumhu*.

(c) *The sick and the traveler* may substitute the same number of days later.

(d) *God wants ease for you, not hardship* — *yurīdu llāhu bikumu l-yusra wa-lā yurīdu bikumu l-`usr*. The classical *yusr-of-the-religion* principle.

**3. The original optional form.**

[2:184]: "Specific days (are designated for fasting); if one is ill or traveling, an equal number of other days may be substituted. Those who can fast, but with great difficulty, may substitute feeding one poor person for each day of breaking the fast. If one volunteers (more righteous works), it is better. But fasting is the best for you, if you only knew." — *fidyatun ṭa`āmu miskīn*. The *fidyah* — *feeding a poor person per day-broken* — as the *substitute* for those who *can fast but with difficulty*. [2:184] and [2:185] together imply a *progression*: the original allowance for *fidyah-substitution* was *broadened* by [2:185]'s *fast it if you witness it*.

Classical reading: [2:184] was *abrogated* by [2:185]. Khalifa-aligned reading: [2:184] *remains* as an *optional* fall-back for those who *can fast only with extreme difficulty* (e.g., the chronically ill, the elderly, the pregnant or nursing).

**4. The diurnal limits.**

[2:187]: "Permitted for you is sexual intercourse with your wives during the nights of fasting. They are the keepers of your secrets, and you are the keepers of their secrets. GOD knew that you used to betray your souls, and He has redeemed you, and has pardoned you. Henceforth, you may have intercourse with them, seeking what GOD has permitted for you. You may eat and drink until the white thread of light becomes distinguishable from the dark thread of night at dawn. Then, you shall fast until sunset." — *kulū wa-shrabū ḥattā yatabayyana lakumu l-khayṭu l-abyaḍu mina l-khayṭi l-aswadi mina l-fajr, thumma atimmū ṣ-ṣiyāma ilā l-layl*.

The verse establishes:

(a) *Nights of fasting* — sexual intercourse, eating, drinking — *permitted at night*.

(b) *White thread / dark thread* — the *dawn marker* (literally, when you can distinguish white thread from black thread in dim morning light).

(c) *Until night* — the *sunset terminator*.

The fast is *diurnal* — from *dawn (true dawn)* to *sunset*.

**5. The retreat clause.**

[2:187] continues: "You shall not have sexual intercourse with them during your retreats (i`tikāf) in the masjids." — the *i`tikāf* (mosque-retreat) practice is *implicitly* recognized; the only constraint named is *sexual abstention* during it.

**6. Atonement fasts.**

[4:92]: For unintended killing of a believer — *freeing a believing slave + bloodwit to the family*; if these are not available, *two months' fasting*.

[5:89]: For breaking an oath — *feeding ten poor people + clothing them*, or *freeing a slave*; if not feasible, *three days' fasting*.

[5:95]: For violating the *iḥrām* (pilgrim sanctity) by hunting — atonement specified, with *fasting* as one option.

[58:4]: For *ẓihār* (the pre-Islamic estrangement-formula) reconciliation — *freeing a slave*; if not available, *two consecutive months' fasting*; if neither, *feeding sixty poor people*.

These verses establish *fasting as a category of penance / atonement* — distinct from the *obligatory annual fast* but using the same *bodily discipline*.

**7. Pilgrimage-substitution fasts.**

[2:196]: For one who cannot perform the full Hajj rites and must release before completion — *three days' fasting + seven days after return*, a *total of ten*.

**8. Mary's silence-fast.**

[19:26]: "Therefore, eat and drink, and be happy. When you see anyone, say, 'I have made a vow of silence (Sawm) to the Most Gracious; I am not talking today to any human being.'" — *innī nadhartu li-r-Raḥmāni ṣawmā*. *I have vowed to the Most Gracious a fast*. Mary's *fast* is *a fast from speech* — *silence-fast* — *not* a food-fast. The verse establishes that *ṣawm* can encompass *speech-abstinence* in addition to food-abstinence, a Quranic precedent for *broader fasting* practices.

**9. The fasting category — for both men and women.**

[33:35]: "Surely, the submitting men and the submitting women, the believing men and the believing women, the obedient men and the obedient women, the truthful men and the truthful women, the steadfast men and the steadfast women, the reverent men and the reverent women, the charitable men and the charitable women, the fasting men and the fasting women..." — the verse's parallel-gender enumeration of categories of righteousness includes *aṣ-ṣā'imīna wa-ṣ-ṣā'imāt* — *the fasting men and the fasting women*. Fasting is *one of the recognized excellences*.

**10. The function in spiritual discipline.**

[2:183]'s *taqwā* and [2:185]'s *praise God for guiding you* establish that the fast's *function* is *reverence-formation* and *gratitude*. The fast is *not* magical; it is *training* — *physical limitation to develop the soul's discipline*.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran describes fasting through: *Ramadan as the month of revelation, to be fasted by those who witness it* ([2:185]); *purpose is taqwā* ([2:183]); *diurnal — dawn to sunset, with permitted nights* ([2:187]); *sick and traveler may substitute* ([2:184]–[2:185]); *fidyah-substitution for those who fast only with great difficulty* ([2:184]); *atonement-fasts for various breaches — killing, oath-breaking, ẓihār, hunting in iḥrām* ([4:92], [5:89], [5:95], [58:4]); *pilgrimage-substitute fasts* ([2:196]); *Mary's silence-fast* ([19:26]) as Quranic precedent for non-food fasting; and *fasting as one of the recognized excellences of both men and women* ([33:35]). The fast's *function* is *reverence-formation and gratitude*.

2:183.text: O you who believe, fasting is decreed for you, as it was decreed for those before you, that you may attain salvation.
2:183.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا كتب عليكم الصيام كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم لعلكم تتقون

2:184.text: Specific days (are designated for fasting); if one is ill or traveling, an equal number of other days may be substituted. Those who can fast, but with great difficulty, may substitute feeding one poor person for each day of breaking the fast. If one volunteers (more righteous works), it is better. But fasting is the best for you, if you only knew.
2:184.arabic: أياما معدودت فمن كان منكم مريضا أو على سفر فعدة من أيام أخر وعلى الذين يطيقونه فدية طعام مسكين فمن تطوع خيرا فهو خير له وأن تصوموا خير لكم إن كنتم تعلمون

2:185.text: Ramadan is the month during which the Quran was revealed, providing guidance for the people, clear teachings, and the statute book. Those of you who witness this month shall fast therein. Those who are ill or traveling may substitute the same number of other days. GOD wishes for you convenience, not hardship, that you may fulfill your obligations, and to glorify GOD for guiding you, and to express your appreciation. God Answers the Prayers of "His Servants"
2:185.arabic: شهر رمضان الذي أنزل فيه القرءان هدى للناس وبينت من الهدى والفرقان فمن شهد منكم الشهر فليصمه ومن كان مريضا أو على سفر فعدة من أيام أخر يريد الله بكم اليسر ولا يريد بكم العسر ولتكملوا العدة ولتكبروا الله على ما هدىكم ولعلكم تشكرون

2:187.text: Permitted for you is sexual intercourse with your wives during the nights of fasting. They are the keepers of your secrets, and you are the keepers of their secrets. GOD knew that you used to betray your souls, and He has redeemed you, and has pardoned you. Henceforth, you may have intercourse with them, seeking what GOD has permitted for you. You may eat and drink until the white thread of light becomes distinguishable from the dark thread of night at dawn. Then, you shall fast until sunset. Sexual intercourse is prohibited if you decide to retreat to the masjid (during the last ten days of Ramadan). These are GOD's laws; you shall not transgress them. GOD thus clarifies His revelations for the people, that they may attain salvation. Bribery, Corruption Condemned
2:187.arabic: أحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إلى نسائكم هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن علم الله أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم فالـن بشروهن وابتغوا ما كتب الله لكم وكلوا واشربوا حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأبيض من الخيط الأسود من الفجر ثم أتموا الصيام إلى اليل ولا تبشروهن وأنتم عكفون في المسجد تلك حدود الله فلا تقربوها كذلك يبين الله ءايته للناس لعلهم يتقون

2:196.text: You shall observe the complete rites of Hajj and`Umrah for GOD. If you are prevented, you shall send an offering, and do not resume cutting your hair until your offering has reached its destination. If you are ill, or suffering a head injury (and you must cut your hair), you shall expiate by fasting, or giving to charity, or some other form of worship. During the normal Hajj, if you break the state of Ihraam (sanctity) between `Umrah and Hajj, you shall expiate by offering an animal sacrifice. If you cannot afford it, you shall fast three days during Hajj and seven when you return home - this completes ten - provided you do not live at the Sacred Masjid. You shall observe GOD, and know that GOD is strict in enforcing retribution.
2:196.arabic: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله فإن أحصرتم فما استيسر من الهدي ولا تحلقوا رءوسكم حتى يبلغ الهدي محله فمن كان منكم مريضا أو به أذى من رأسه ففدية من صيام أو صدقة أو نسك فإذا أمنتم فمن تمتع بالعمرة إلى الحج فما استيسر من الهدي فمن لم يجد فصيام ثلثة أيام في الحج وسبعة إذا رجعتم تلك عشرة كاملة ذلك لمن لم يكن أهله حاضري المسجد الحرام واتقوا الله واعلموا أن الله شديد العقاب

4:92.text: No believer shall kill another believer, unless it is an accident. If one kills a believer by accident, he shall atone by freeing a believing slave, and paying a compensation to the victim's family, unless they forfeit such a compensation as a charity. If the victim belonged to people who are at war with you, though he was a believer, you shall atone by freeing a believing slave. If he belonged to people with whom you have signed a peace treaty, you shall pay the compensation in addition to freeing a believing slave. If you cannot find a slave to free, you shall atone by fasting two consecutive months, in order to be redeemed by GOD. GOD is Knower, Most Wise.
4:92.arabic: وما كان لمؤمن أن يقتل مؤمنا إلا خطـا ومن قتل مؤمنا خطـا فتحرير رقبة مؤمنة ودية مسلمة إلى أهله إلا أن يصدقوا فإن كان من قوم عدو لكم وهو مؤمن فتحرير رقبة مؤمنة وإن كان من قوم بينكم وبينهم ميثق فدية مسلمة إلى أهله وتحرير رقبة مؤمنة فمن لم يجد فصيام شهرين متتابعين توبة من الله وكان الله عليما حكيما

5:89.text: GOD does not hold you responsible for the mere utterance of oaths; He holds you responsible for your actual intentions. If you violate an oath, you shall atone by feeding ten poor people from the same food you offer to your own family, or clothing them, or by freeing a slave. If you cannot afford this, then you shall fast three days. This is the atonement for violating the oaths that you swore to keep. You shall fulfill your oaths. GOD thus explains His revelations to you, that you may be appreciative.
5:89.arabic: لا يؤاخذكم الله باللغو في أيمنكم ولكن يؤاخذكم بما عقدتم الأيمن فكفـرته إطعام عشرة مسكين من أوسط ما تطعمون أهليكم أو كسوتهم أو تحرير رقبة فمن لم يجد فصيام ثلثة أيام ذلك كفـرة أيمنكم إذا حلفتم واحفظوا أيمنكم كذلك يبين الله لكم ءايته لعلكم تشكرون

5:95.text: O you who believe, do not kill any game during pilgrimage. Anyone who kills any game on purpose, his fine shall be a number of livestock animals that is equivalent to the game animals he killed. The judgment shall be set by two equitable people among you. They shall make sure that the offerings reach the Ka`bah. Otherwise, he may expiate by feeding poor people, or by an equivalent fast to atone for his offense. GOD has pardoned past offenses. But if anyone returns to such an offense, GOD will avenge it. GOD is Almighty, Avenger.
5:95.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا لا تقتلوا الصيد وأنتم حرم ومن قتله منكم متعمدا فجزاء مثل ما قتل من النعم يحكم به ذوا عدل منكم هديا بلغ الكعبة أو كفـرة طعام مسكين أو عدل ذلك صياما ليذوق وبال أمره عفا الله عما سلف ومن عاد فينتقم الله منه والله عزيز ذو انتقام

19:26.text: "Eat and drink, and be happy. When you see anyone, say, `I have made a vow of silence; I am not talking today to anyone.' "
19:26.arabic: فكلي واشربي وقري عينا فإما ترين من البشر أحدا فقولي إني نذرت للرحمن صوما فلن أكلم اليوم إنسيا

33:35.text: The submitting men, the submitting women, the believing men, the believing women, the obedient men, the obedient women, the truthful men, the truthful women, the steadfast men, the steadfast women, the reverent men, the reverent women, the charitable men, the charitable women, the fasting men, the fasting women, the chaste men, the chaste women, and the men who commemorate GOD frequently, and the commemorating women; GOD has prepared for them forgiveness and a great recompense. Major Error Committed by Muhammad Muhammad the Man Disobeys Muhammad the Messenger
33:35.arabic: إن المسلمين والمسلمت والمؤمنين والمؤمنت والقنتين والقنتت والصـدقين والصـدقت والصـبرين والصـبرت والخشعين والخشعت والمتصدقين والمتصدقت والصـئمين والصـئمت والحفظين فروجهم والحفظت والذـكرين الله كثيرا والذـكرت أعد الله لهم مغفرة وأجرا عظيما

58:4.text: If you cannot find a slave to free, you shall fast two consecutive months before resuming sexual relations. If you cannot fast, then you shall feed sixty poor people. You shall believe in GOD and His messenger. These are GOD's laws. The disbelievers have incurred a painful retribution.
58:4.arabic: فمن لم يجد فصيام شهرين متتابعين من قبل أن يتماسا فمن لم يستطع فإطعام ستين مسكينا ذلك لتؤمنوا بالله ورسوله وتلك حدود الله وللكفرين عذاب أليم

question: What does the Quran say about the rites of hajj?
answer:
The Quran's instructions on *Ḥajj* — the major pilgrimage to Mecca — cover its *origin*, *obligations*, *timing*, *prohibitions during iḥrām*, *the specific rites*, and *the spiritual function*. The two key texts are surah 2 (Al-Baqarah, verses 189-203, on the pilgrimage rites) and surah 22 (Al-Ḥajj, named for the pilgrimage).

**1. The Abrahamic origin.**

[2:127]: "As Abraham raised the foundations of the shrine, together with Ismail (they prayed): 'Our Lord, accept this from us. You are the Hearer, the Omniscient.'" — the *Kaaba* is *built by Abraham and Ismail*.

[22:26]: "We appointed Abraham to establish the Shrine: 'You shall not idolize any other god beside Me, and purify My shrine for those who visit it, those who live near it, and those who bow and prostrate.'"

[22:27]: "And proclaim that the people shall observe Hajj pilgrimage. They will come to you walking or riding on various exhausted (means of transportation). They will come from the deepest valleys." — Abraham *proclaims the pilgrimage*.

[3:96]–[3:97]: "The most important shrine established for the people is the one in Becca; a blessed beacon for all the people. In it are clear signs: the station of Abraham. Anyone who enters it shall be granted safe passage. The people owe it to GOD that they shall observe Hajj to this shrine, when they can afford it. As for those who disbelieve, GOD does not need anyone." — *li-llāhi `alā n-nāsi ḥijju l-bayti mani staṭā`a ilayhi sabīlā*. *It is the right of God upon people to pilgrimage to the house — whoever is able to find a way to it*. The verse establishes:

(a) The *obligation* is on *every able-bodied and able-funded* Muslim *once in a lifetime*.

(b) The *condition* is *ability* — *mani staṭā`a ilayhi sabīlā* — *whoever can find a way*.

(c) Inability is *not punished*; the obligation lapses for the incapable.

**2. The months.**

[2:197]: "Hajj shall be observed in the specified months. Whoever sets out to observe Hajj shall refrain from sexual intercourse, misconduct, and arguments throughout Hajj. Whatever good you do, GOD is fully aware thereof. As you prepare your provisions for the journey, the best provision is righteousness. You shall observe Me, O you who possess intelligence." — *al-Ḥajju ashhurun ma`lūmāt*. *The Hajj is known months*. The classical identification: *Shawwāl, Dhū al-Qa`dah, the first ten of Dhū al-Ḥijjah*.

**3. The iḥrām and its prohibitions.**

[2:197]: *no sexual intercourse, no misconduct, no arguments* during Hajj.

[5:1]–[5:2]: "O you who believe, you shall fulfill your covenants. Permitted for you to eat is the livestock, except those specifically prohibited herein. You shall not permit hunting throughout Hajj pilgrimage."

[5:95]: "O you who believe, do not kill any game during Hajj pilgrimage. Anyone who kills any game on purpose, his fine shall be a number of livestock animals that is equivalent to the game animals he killed."

The *iḥrām* prohibitions are: *sexual relations, hunting, arguments*. The verse also implies the *iḥrām-clothing* requirement (two unstitched white cloths) — though this specific dress code is *Hadith-derived* rather than Quranic-explicit.

**4. The rites — the specific sequence.**

[2:158]: "The knolls of Safa and Marwah are among the rites decreed by GOD. Anyone who observes Hajj or `Umrah commits no error by traversing the distance between them. If one volunteers more righteous works, then GOD is Appreciative, Omniscient." — *aṣ-Ṣafā wa-l-Marwa min sha`ā'iri llāh*. The *Safā-Marwah walk* (*sa`y*) — between the two small hills near the Kaaba — is *part of the rites*.

[22:29]: "They shall complete their obligations, fulfill their vows, and visit the ancient shrine." — *al-bayt al-`atīq*. The *ancient shrine* — the Kaaba.

[22:33]: "The (livestock) provide you with many benefits for a period, before being donated to the ancient shrine." — the *animal-offering* (*hady*) for sacrifice.

[22:36]–[22:37]: "The animal offerings are among the rites decreed by GOD for your own good. You shall mention GOD's name on them while they are standing in line. Once they are offered for sacrifice, you shall eat therefrom and feed the poor and the needy. This is why we subdued them for you, that you may show your appreciation." — *hady* — the *sacrificial animals*. The flesh is *eaten + given to the poor*.

**5. The day-of-`Arafah.**

[2:198]: "You commit no error by seeking provisions from your Lord (through commerce). When you file from Arafat, you shall commemorate GOD at the Sacred Location (of Muzdalifah). You shall commemorate Him for guiding you; before this, you had gone astray." — *fa-idhā afaḍtum min `Arafāt fa-dhkurū llāha `inda l-Mash`ari l-Ḥarām*. The *standing at `Arafah* (*wuqūf*) is *the central rite*; the *filing toward Muzdalifah* (*Mash`ar al-Ḥarām*) follows.

[2:199]: "You shall file together, with the rest of the people who file, and ask GOD for forgiveness. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful."

**6. The stoning, the three-day stay.**

[2:203]: "You shall commemorate GOD for a number of days (in Mina); whoever hastens to do this in two days commits no sin, and whoever stays longer commits no sin, so long as righteousness is maintained. You shall observe GOD, and know that before Him you will be gathered." — the *Mina stay* — *the three days* (or *two if hastened*).

[22:28]: "They may seek commercial benefits, and they shall commemorate GOD's name during the specified days for providing them with livestock. 'Eat therefrom and feed the despondent and the poor.'" — the *food-distribution* clause.

**7. The substitution for those who cannot complete.**

[2:196]: "You shall observe the complete rites of Hajj and `Umrah for GOD. If you are prevented, you shall send an offering, and do not resume cutting your hair until your offering has reached its destination. If you are ill, or suffering a head injury (and you must cut your hair), you shall expiate by fasting, or giving to charity, or some other form of worship. During the normal Hajj, if you break the state of Ihram (sanctity) between `Umrah and Hajj, you shall expiate by offering an animal sacrifice. If you cannot afford it, you shall fast three days during Hajj and seven when you return home — this completes ten — provided you do not live at the Sacred Masjid. You shall observe GOD, and know that GOD is strict in enforcing retribution." — the *substitution-fasts and offerings* for those who *cannot complete* in the standard way.

**8. The function — to witness.**

[22:28] continues: "They may witness benefits for themselves, and commemorate GOD's name on the specified days." — *li-yashhadū manāfi`a lahum*. *That they may witness benefits for themselves*. The *purpose* of Hajj — to *witness benefits*, both *spiritual and communal*.

**9. The non-monetary character.**

[22:32]: "Indeed, those who reverence the rites decreed by GOD demonstrate the righteousness of their hearts." — *dhālika wa-man yu`aẓẓim sha`ā'ira llāhi fa-innahā min taqwā l-qulūb*. *Whoever magnifies the rites of God — that is from the reverence of the hearts*. The *reverence of the rites* is *the heart-quality* — not the *ritual mechanics* alone.

**10. The crowd-as-community.**

[2:200]: "Once you complete your rites, you shall continue to commemorate GOD as you commemorate your own parents, or even better. Some people would say, 'Our Lord, give us of this world,' while having no share in the Hereafter."

**Synthesis.**

The Quran describes Hajj through: *Abrahamic origin* — the Kaaba built by Abraham and Ismail, the pilgrimage proclaimed by Abraham ([22:27]); *obligation on every able Muslim once* ([3:97]); *specified months* ([2:197]); *iḥrām prohibitions on intercourse, hunting, arguments* ([2:197], [5:95]); *Ṣafā-Marwah walk* ([2:158]); *animal sacrifice* with sharing to the poor ([22:36]); *standing at `Arafah and proceeding to Muzdalifah* ([2:198]); *commemoration days at Mina, two or three days* ([2:203]); *substitution fasts and offerings* for those prevented ([2:196]); and *the function is to witness benefits and magnify the rites from heart-reverence* ([22:28], [22:32]). The *specific procedural details* (exact circumambulation count, prayer positions, dress code particulars) are *Hadith-derived* but consistent with the Quranic framework.

2:125.text: We have rendered the shrine (the Ka`aba) a focal point for the people, and a safe sanctuary. You may use Abraham's shrine as a prayer house. We commissioned Abraham and Ismail: "You shall purify My house for those who visit, those who live there, and those who bow and prostrate."
2:125.arabic: وإذ جعلنا البيت مثابة للناس وأمنا واتخذوا من مقام إبرهـم مصلى وعهدنا إلى إبرهـم وإسمعيل أن طهرا بيتي للطائفين والعكفين والركع السجود

2:127.text: As Abraham raised the foundations of the shrine, together with Ismail (they prayed): "Our Lord, accept this from us. You are the Hearer, the Omniscient.
2:127.arabic: وإذ يرفع إبرهـم القواعد من البيت وإسمعيل ربنا تقبل منا إنك أنت السميع العليم

2:158.text: The knolls of Safa and Marwah are among the rites decreed by GOD. Anyone who observes Hajj or `Umrah commits no error by traversing the distance between them. If one volunteers more righteous works, then GOD is Appreciative, Omniscient.
2:158.arabic: ۞إن الصفا والمروة من شعائر الله فمن حج البيت أو اعتمر فلا جناح عليه أن يطوف بهما ومن تطوع خيرا فإن الله شاكر عليم

2:189.text: They ask you about the phases of the moon! Say, "They provide a timing device for the people, and determine the time of Hajj." It is not righteous to beat around the bush; righteousness is attained by upholding the commandments and by being straightforward. You shall observe GOD, that you may succeed.
2:189.arabic: ۞يسـلونك عن الأهلة قل هي موقيت للناس والحج وليس البر بأن تأتوا البيوت من ظهورها ولكن البر من اتقى وأتوا البيوت من أبوبها واتقوا الله لعلكم تفلحون

2:196.text: You shall observe the complete rites of Hajj and`Umrah for GOD. If you are prevented, you shall send an offering, and do not resume cutting your hair until your offering has reached its destination. If you are ill, or suffering a head injury (and you must cut your hair), you shall expiate by fasting, or giving to charity, or some other form of worship. During the normal Hajj, if you break the state of Ihraam (sanctity) between `Umrah and Hajj, you shall expiate by offering an animal sacrifice. If you cannot afford it, you shall fast three days during Hajj and seven when you return home - this completes ten - provided you do not live at the Sacred Masjid. You shall observe GOD, and know that GOD is strict in enforcing retribution.
2:196.arabic: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله فإن أحصرتم فما استيسر من الهدي ولا تحلقوا رءوسكم حتى يبلغ الهدي محله فمن كان منكم مريضا أو به أذى من رأسه ففدية من صيام أو صدقة أو نسك فإذا أمنتم فمن تمتع بالعمرة إلى الحج فما استيسر من الهدي فمن لم يجد فصيام ثلثة أيام في الحج وسبعة إذا رجعتم تلك عشرة كاملة ذلك لمن لم يكن أهله حاضري المسجد الحرام واتقوا الله واعلموا أن الله شديد العقاب

2:197.text: Hajj shall be observed in the specified months. Whoever sets out to observe Hajj shall refrain from sexual intercourse, misconduct, and arguments throughout Hajj. Whatever good you do, GOD is fully aware thereof. As you prepare your provisions for the journey, the best provision is righteousness. You shall observe Me, O you who possess intelligence.
2:197.arabic: الحج أشهر معلومت فمن فرض فيهن الحج فلا رفث ولا فسوق ولا جدال في الحج وما تفعلوا من خير يعلمه الله وتزودوا فإن خير الزاد التقوى واتقون يـأولي الألبب

2:198.text: You commit no error by seeking provisions from your Lord (through commerce). When you file from `Arafaat, you shall commemorate GOD at the Sacred Location (of Muzdalifah). You shall commemorate Him for guiding you; before this, you had gone astray.
2:198.arabic: ليس عليكم جناح أن تبتغوا فضلا من ربكم فإذا أفضتم من عرفت فاذكروا الله عند المشعر الحرام واذكروه كما هدىكم وإن كنتم من قبله لمن الضالين

2:199.text: You shall file together, with the rest of the people who file, and ask GOD for forgiveness. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful.
2:199.arabic: ثم أفيضوا من حيث أفاض الناس واستغفروا الله إن الله غفور رحيم

2:200.text: Once you complete your rites, you shall continue to commemorate GOD as you commemorate your own parents, or even better. Some people would say, "Our Lord, give us of this world," while having no share in the Hereafter.
2:200.arabic: فإذا قضيتم منسككم فاذكروا الله كذكركم ءاباءكم أو أشد ذكرا فمن الناس من يقول ربنا ءاتنا في الدنيا وما له في الأخرة من خلق

2:203.text: You shall commemorate GOD for a number of days (in Mena); whoever hastens to do this in two days commits no sin, and whoever stays longer commits no sin, so long as righteousness is maintained. You shall observe GOD, and know that before Him you will be gathered.
2:203.arabic: ۞واذكروا الله في أيام معدودت فمن تعجل في يومين فلا إثم عليه ومن تأخر فلا إثم عليه لمن اتقى واتقوا الله واعلموا أنكم إليه تحشرون

3:96.text: The most important shrine established for the people is the one in Becca; a blessed beacon for all the people.
3:96.arabic: إن أول بيت وضع للناس للذي ببكة مباركا وهدى للعلمين

3:97.text: In it are clear signs: the station of Abraham. Anyone who enters it shall be granted safe passage. The people owe it to GOD that they shall observe Hajj to this shrine, when they can afford it. As for those who disbelieve, GOD does not need anyone.
3:97.arabic: فيه ءايت بينت مقام إبرهيم ومن دخله كان ءامنا ولله على الناس حج البيت من استطاع إليه سبيلا ومن كفر فإن الله غني عن العلمين

5:1.text: O you who believe, you shall fulfill your covenants. Permitted for you to eat are the livestock, except those specifically prohibited herein. You shall not permit hunting throughout Hajj pilgrimage. GOD decrees whatever He wills.
5:1.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا أوفوا بالعقود أحلت لكم بهيمة الأنعم إلا ما يتلى عليكم غير محلي الصيد وأنتم حرم إن الله يحكم ما يريد

5:2.text: O you who believe, do not violate the rites instituted by GOD, nor the Sacred Months, nor the animals to be offered, nor the garlands marking them, nor the people who head for the Sacred Shrine (Ka`bah) seeking blessings from their Lord and approval. Once you complete the pilgrimage, you may hunt. Do not be provoked into aggression by your hatred of people who once prevented you from going to the Sacred Masjid. You shall cooperate in matters of righteousness and piety; do not cooperate in matters that are sinful and evil. You shall observe GOD. GOD is strict in enforcing retribution.
