They thus realized that if they really knew the unseen, they would have stopped working so hard as soon as he died.
34:14.arabic: فلما قضينا عليه الموت ما دلهم على موته إلا دابة الأرض تأكل منسأته فلما خر تبينت الجن أن لو كانوا يعلمون الغيب ما لبثوا في العذاب المهين

question: How does the Quran describe the angels and their roles?
answer:
The Quran's *angelology* is systematic, detailed, and *theologically functional*. Angels (*malā'ikah*, sing. *malak*) appear in *every section* of the Quran with *specific named roles*: *Gabriel, Michael, the angel of death, the recording angels, the throne-bearers, the rank-organizers, the punishment-administrators, the gate-keepers*. The Quranic *angelology* is *anti-anthropomorphic* (angels are *not little flying babies*) and *strictly functional* — each angelic class has *a specific divine-mission*.

**1. The general descriptions.**

[35:1]: "Praise be to GOD, Initiator of the heavens and the earth, and appointer of the angels to be messengers with wings - two, three, and four (wings). He increases the creation as He wills. GOD is Omnipotent." — *jā`ili l-malā'ikati rusulan ulī ajniḥatin mathnā wa-thulātha wa-rubā`*.

The verse establishes: (a) *angels are messengers* (*rusul*); (b) *angels have wings* — *two, three, four* (and presumably more for higher-ranked angels); (c) *the angelic-population is variable* — *God increases the creation as He wills*.

[2:30]: "Recall that your Lord said to the angels, 'I am placing a representative (a temporary god) on Earth.' They said, 'Will You place therein one who will spread evil therein and shed blood, while we sing Your praises, glorify You, and uphold Your absolute authority?' He said, 'I know what you do not know.'"

The verse establishes: (a) *angels are addressed by God* and *can respond*; (b) *angels glorify and praise God* as their *constant activity*; (c) *angels have knowledge-limits* — *God knows what they do not know*.

**2. The named angels.**

The Quran names *very few angels* by individual name:

(a) *Gabriel (Jibrīl)* — [2:97]: *the messenger of revelation* — *brought down the Quran to your heart*.

(b) *Michael (Mīkāl)* — [2:98]: *paired with Gabriel as a named angel* whose *opposition is equivalent to opposition to God*.

(c) *Hārūt and Mārūt* — [2:102]: *the two angels in Babylon* who *taught magic* (under specific conditions).

The Quran does *not* name *the angel of death* (*malak al-mawt*) — referring to him by *function* only at [32:11].

The Quran does *not* name *Israfil* (the *trumpet-blower*) — the *name* is from *the hadith-tradition*; the *Quran* refers to *the trumpet-blower* by *function* at [39:68], [78:18].

The Quran does *not* name *Munkar* and *Nakīr* (the *grave-interrogators*) — these are from *the hadith-tradition*.

The Quranic *angelology* is *strictly minimalist* in naming — preferring *functional descriptions* over *proper names*.

**3. The angelic roles.**

(a) *Revelation-bearers* — [16:2]: *He sends down the angels with the revelations, carrying His commands, to whomever He chooses from among His servants*. *Angels deliver the divine-revelation to prophets*.

(b) *Recording-angels* — [82:10]: *Knowing full well that there are (angels) watching over you*. [82:11]: *They are honorable recorders*. [82:12]: *They know everything you do*. *Two recording angels — kirāman kātibīn — record human deeds*.

(c) *Heart-watching angels* — [50:17]: *Note that two recording (angels), at right and left, are constantly recording*. [50:18]: *Not an utterance does he utter without an alert witness*.

(d) *Throne-bearers* — [40:7]: *Those who serve the throne and all those around it glorify and praise their Lord*. [69:17]: *The angels will be all around, and the dominion of your Lord will then encompass eight (universes)*.

(e) *Death-angel* — [32:11]: *Say, 'You will be put to death by the angel in whose charge you are placed, then to your Lord you will be returned.'*

(f) *Hell-administrators* — [74:31]: *We appointed angels to be guardians of Hell*.

(g) *Paradise-greeters* — [13:23]–[13:24]: *They will enter the Gardens of Eden, together with the righteous from among their parents, their spouses, and their children. The angels will enter unto them from every gate*. *Greetings to you for your steadfastness*.

(h) *Rank-organizers* — [37:1]–[37:3]: *the saff-aligners, the zajr-rebukers, the dhikr-reciters*. *Three classes of angels* organized by *function*.

(i) *Soul-extractors* — [79:1]–[79:5]: *the soul-extractors (nāzi`āt), the soul-drawers (nāshiṭāt), the swimmers (sābiḥāt), the racers (sābiqāt), the affair-managers (mudabbirāt)*. *Five classes of angels* with *eschatological-functional roles*.

(j) *Punishment-administrators* — implicit in multiple verses describing *the angels of the fire* and *the angels at the Day of Judgment*.

(k) *Battle-helpers* — [3:124]–[3:125]: *Is it not enough that your Lord supports you with three thousand angels, sent down? Indeed, if you steadfastly persevere and maintain righteousness, then they attack you suddenly, your Lord will support you with five thousand angels, well trained*.

(l) *Mercy-petitioners* — [40:7]: *And they ask forgiveness for those who believe*.

**4. The submission and obedience.**

[16:50]: "They reverence their Lord, high above them, and they do whatever they are commanded to do." — *yakhāfūna rabbahum min fawqihim wa-yaf`alūna mā yu'marūn*.

[66:6]: "...They never disobey GOD; they do whatever they are commanded to do." — *lā ya`ṣūna llāha mā amarahum wa-yaf`alūna mā yu'marūn*.

The Quranic angelology *categorically* establishes *angelic obedience*: *angels do not have free-will in the human-jinn sense*; *they are programmed-to-obey*; *they do not disobey*.

This *distinguishes* angels from jinn (who *have free-will and can disobey* — Iblis being the paradigm case). The *Iblis-was-an-angel* reading (common in Christian-Jewish traditions) is *Quranically refuted* — Iblis is *categorically a jinn* ([18:50]), *not a fallen angel*.

**5. The descent on Laylat al-Qadr.**

[97:4]: "The angels and the Spirit descend therein, by their Lord's leave, to carry out every command." — *tanazzalu l-malā'ikatu wa-r-rūḥu fīhā bi-idhni rabbihim min kulli amr*.

The verse describes *the Night of Power* — *the angels and the Spirit descend en masse* on this night, *carrying out every command*. The *Spirit (ar-Rūḥ)* is *sometimes identified* with *Gabriel* and *sometimes* as *a distinct angelic-being* (the Quran does *not specify*).

**6. The Abrahamic-visit episode.**

[11:69]: "When our messengers went to Abraham with good news, they said, 'Peace.' He said, 'Peace,' and soon brought a roasted calf."

The verse describes *the angelic-visit to Abraham* — *the same angels* who *destroyed the people of Lot*. The *narrative* establishes: (a) *angels can take human form* (Abraham *served them a meal* — they *appeared as guests*); (b) *angels can refuse to eat* — [11:70]: *When he saw that their hands did not reach for it, he became suspicious and afraid of them*; (c) *the angels announce* the *birth of Isaac* and the *destruction of the people of Lot*.

The *human-form-capacity* of angels is *specific to mission-contexts*; the *general angelology* does *not* describe angels as *routinely visible to humans*.

**7. The 'changing the condition of a people' clause.**

[13:11]: "...GOD does not change the condition of any people unless they themselves change..." — the verse begins: *lahū mu`aqqibātun min bayni yadayhi wa-min khalfihī yaḥfaẓūnahū min amri llāh* — *for him are alternating (angels) in front and behind, guarding him by God's command*.

The verse identifies *protecting-angels* — *they guard the human from divine-decree* (until the *decree-time arrives*). The Khalifa-translation reads this as *angels in front and behind, by God's command*.

**8. The 'not children' clause.**

[16:57]: "They even assign daughters to GOD, be He glorified, while they themselves prefer what they like."

[37:149]–[37:150]: "Ask them: 'Does your Lord have daughters, while they have sons?' Did we create the angels to be females? Did they witness that?"

The Quran *categorically refutes* the *pre-Islamic Arabian belief* that *the angels are God's daughters*. *The angels are not God's children*; *the angels are not gendered*; *the angels do not have human-family-structures*.

**9. The Khalifa framework.**

For Khalifa's framework, the *angelology* is *strictly functional* — *each angelic class has a specific divine-mission*. The *anti-anthropomorphism* is *categorical* — *angels are not little babies, not gendered, not God's children*. The *Khalifa-translation* consistently uses *angels* (rather than *cherubim, seraphim, etc.*) — preferring the *generic functional designation* over *Christian-derived hierarchies*. The *practical implication* for the believer: *the angels are watching*; *the recording is precise*; *the day will come when each soul is presented with its complete record*.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's *angelology* is *systematic and functional*: *angels have wings* ([35:1]); *they glorify and praise God constantly* ([2:30]); they perform *specific functional roles* — *revelation-bearers* ([16:2]), *recording-angels* ([82:10]–[82:12], [50:17]–[50:18]), *throne-bearers* ([40:7]), *death-angel* ([32:11]), *hell-administrators* ([74:31]), *paradise-greeters* ([13:23]–[13:24]), *rank-organizers* ([37:1]–[37:3]), *soul-extractors* ([79:1]–[79:5]), *battle-helpers* ([3:124]–[3:125]), *mercy-petitioners* ([40:7]), and *protectors* ([13:11]). *Angels are categorically obedient* ([16:50], [66:6]) — *they do not disobey*; *Iblis is a jinn, not a fallen angel* ([18:50]). The Quran *categorically refutes* the *pre-Islamic Arabian belief* that *the angels are God's daughters* ([37:149]–[37:150]). The *Khalifa framework* emphasizes the *strict functional minimalism* of *Quranic angelology* — *each angelic class has a specific divine-mission*; *no anthropomorphism*, *no gendering*, *no family-structures*.

question: What does the Quran mean by belief in the Unseen (al-ghayb)?
answer:
The Quran's concept of *al-ghayb* — *the unseen* — is *foundational* to its *epistemology of belief*. The very first verses of Surah Al-Baqarah ([2:3]) identify *belief in the unseen* as *the primary characteristic of the righteous*. *Al-ghayb* is *not* primarily *the supernatural* in a *Western-occult sense*; it is *the domain of reality that is not accessible to human direct-perception* — *God Himself, the angels, the Day of Judgment, the resurrection, the soul's interior, the future, the divine decree*.

**1. The foundational verse.**

[2:3]: "who believe in the unseen, observe the Contact Prayers (Salat), and from our provisions to them, they give to charity." — *alladhīna yu'minūna bi-l-ghaybi wa-yuqīmūna ṣ-ṣalāta wa-mimmā razaqnāhum yunfiqūn*.

The verse — the *third verse* of the *second surah*, after the *opening Fatiha* — identifies *belief in the unseen* as *the first listed characteristic* of *the righteous* (*al-muttaqīn* from [2:2]). The *theological priority* is *categorical*: *the unseen-believer is the prototype-righteous-person*.

**2. The 'God alone knows the unseen' verses.**

[6:59]: "With Him are the keys to all secrets; none knows them except He. He knows everything on land and in the sea. Not a leaf falls without His knowledge. Nor is there a grain in the depths of the soil. Nor is there anything wet or dry, that is not recorded in a profound record." — *wa-`indahū mafātīḥu l-ghaybi lā ya`lamuhā illā huwa*.

[27:65]: "Say, 'No one in the heavens and the earth knows the future except GOD. They do not even perceive how or when they will be resurrected.'" — *qul lā ya`lamu man fī s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi l-ghayba illā llāh*.

[72:26]: "He is the Knower of the future; He does not reveal the future to anyone." — *`ālimu l-ghaybi fa-lā yuẓhiru `alā ghaybihī aḥadā*.

The three verses establish *the categorical claim*: *only God has full knowledge of the unseen*; *no creature — human, jinn, angel — has unseen-access* except as God grants it through *specific revelation*.

**3. The 'except through chosen messengers' qualifier.**

[72:27]: "Only through the messengers that He chooses, does He reveal past and future events. He sends guards before them and behind them." — *illā mani rtaḍā min rasūl*.

The verse *qualifies* [72:26]: *God does not reveal the unseen to anyone, except through chosen messengers*. The *prophets* receive *partial unseen-revelations* as part of their *prophetic mission*; *no other category of creature* has *unseen-access*.

The *exclusion* is *categorical*: *fortune-tellers, soothsayers, jinn-channelers, palm-readers, astrologers* — *none have unseen-access*. The *Quranic epistemology of the unseen* is *strictly anti-occultist*.

**4. The five-keys-of-ghayb.**

[31:34]: "With GOD is the knowledge about the Hour (end of the world). He is the One who sends down the rain, and He knows the contents of the womb. No soul knows what will happen to it tomorrow, and no one knows in which land he or she will die. GOD is Omniscient, Cognizant."

The verse identifies *five domains of unseen-knowledge* that *only God possesses*:

(a) *The Hour* — *the timing of the end-of-the-world*.

(b) *The rain* — *when, where, how much*.

(c) *The contents of the womb* — *the embryo's identity, properties, destiny*.

(d) *What will happen tomorrow* — *the next-day's events*.

(e) *Where one will die* — *the location of one's death*.

These *five keys* are *the classical list* of *the unseen-domains*. (The hadith-tradition expanded this list, but the Quranic-list is just these five.)

**5. The 'I do not know the unseen' prophetic-confessions.**

[6:50]: "Say, 'I do not say to you that I possess the treasures of GOD. Nor do I know the future. Nor do I say to you that I am an angel. I simply follow what is revealed to me.' Say, 'Is the blind the same as the seer? Do you not reflect?'" — *qul lā aqūlu lakum `indī khazā'inu llāhi wa-lā a`lamu l-ghayba wa-lā aqūlu lakum innī malak, in attabi`u illā mā yūḥā ilayy*.

The verse — *Muhammad's own self-confession* — categorically *denies prophetic-unseen-access* beyond *what is revealed*: *I do not say I possess God's treasures; I do not know the unseen; I do not claim to be an angel; I follow only what is revealed to me*.

[11:31] (Noah): "'I do not say to you that I possess the treasures of GOD. Nor do I know the future. Nor do I say that I am an angel. Nor do I say to those despised by your eyes that GOD will not bestow any blessings upon them. GOD knows best what is in their innermost thoughts. (If I did this,) I would be a transgressor.'"

The same prophetic-confession-formula is repeated by *Noah*. The *prophetic-self-denial of unseen-access* (beyond what is revealed) is a *standard prophetic-confession formula* — applied to *Noah* and *Muhammad* both.

**6. The 'I do not know the unseen' applied to Noah's son.**

[11:49]: "This is news from the past that we reveal to you. You had no knowledge about them - neither you, nor your people - before this. Therefore, be patient. The ultimate victory belongs to the righteous." — *tilka min anbā'i l-ghaybi nūḥīhā ilayk*.

The verse identifies *the Noah-narrative itself* as *anbā' al-ghayb* — *unseen-news* — *that you and your people had no knowledge of before this revelation*. *The past* — when *no longer accessible to direct-witness* — is *one form of unseen* that *only revelation can disclose*.

**7. The 'judging by appearances vs. ghayb' clause.**

[49:18]: "GOD knows all the secrets of the heavens and the earth; GOD is Seer of everything you do." — *innallāha ya`lamu ghayba s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-llāhu Baṣīrun bi-mā ta`malūn*.

The verse pairs *God's unseen-knowledge* with *God's visible-knowledge*: *He knows both ghayb and shahādah*. The *theological implication*: *the human moral-life is fully transparent to God* — *both the visible-actions and the hidden-intentions, secret-thoughts, private-deeds*.

The *practical implication* — *do not act for human-witnesses alone*; *the divine-witness is constant and complete*.

**8. The categories of ghayb-content.**

The Quranic *al-ghayb* covers *multiple domains*:

(a) *God Himself* — His *essence*, *attributes*, *will*.

(b) *The angels* — their *existence, roles, identities*.

(c) *The jinn* — their *existence, roles* (though *humans cannot directly perceive them*).

(d) *The Day of Judgment* — *the eschatological events*.

(e) *Paradise and hell* — *the eschatological reward-punishment domains*.

(f) *The resurrection* — *the post-death existence*.

(g) *The soul's interior* — *thoughts, intentions, hidden-states*.

(h) *The future* — *what has not yet happened*.

(i) *The past not directly witnessed* — *the prophetic-narratives, the prior-creations*.

(j) *The divine-decree (qadar)* — *the unfolding of God's plan*.

Each category is *accessible* only through *divine-revelation* — *not* through *empirical observation*, *not* through *philosophical deduction*, *not* through *occult practice*.

**9. The 'belief in the unseen' as epistemological posture.**

The Quranic *belief in the unseen* (*al-īmān bi-l-ghayb*) is *not* primarily *belief in propositions about the supernatural*; it is *the epistemological posture of trust in God's testimony about what humans cannot directly verify*. The believer *accepts* what God has *revealed* about *the unseen* on the basis of *trust in the divine-source* — not on the basis of *empirical-verification* (which is *categorically impossible* for unseen-content) and not on the basis of *philosophical-proof* (which is *insufficient* to establish *specific unseen-content*).

This is *theologically critical*: the *Quranic epistemology* is *neither pure-empiricism* nor *pure-rationalism* — it is *revelation-grounded trust* for *unseen-content* and *empirical-rational verification* for *visible-content*. The two epistemological modes operate *complementarily*.

**10. The Khalifa framework.**

For Khalifa's framework, *belief in the unseen* is *foundational* to *Submission*: the Submitter *trusts God's testimony* about *the unseen* on the *basis of God's reliability* (established by *the visible-signs* and *the mathematical-19 code*). The *unseen-trust* is *grounded* in *visible-evidence* of *God's reliability* — *not* in *blind-faith*. The Khalifa-translation consistently renders *al-ghayb* as *the unseen* or *the future* (depending on context) — preserving the *epistemological-domain* meaning.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's *al-ghayb* (the unseen) is *the domain of reality not accessible to human direct-perception*: *God Himself, the angels, the Day of Judgment, paradise, hell, the resurrection, the soul's interior, the future, the un-witnessed past, the divine-decree*. *Only God has full knowledge of the unseen* ([6:59], [27:65], [72:26]) — *except as He reveals to chosen messengers* ([72:27]). The *five-keys-of-ghayb* at [31:34] — *the Hour, rain, womb-contents, tomorrow's events, place-of-death* — are *the classical list* of unseen-domains. *Prophets themselves do not claim full unseen-knowledge* — Muhammad ([6:50]) and Noah ([11:31]) categorically confess: *I do not know the unseen; I follow only what is revealed to me*. *Belief in the unseen* is the *first listed characteristic* of *the righteous* ([2:3]) — the *epistemological posture* of *revelation-grounded trust* for *unseen-content*. The *anti-occult implications* are *categorical*: *fortune-tellers, soothsayers, astrologers* are *Quranically excluded* from *legitimate unseen-access*.

2:3.text: who believe in the unseen, observe the Contact Prayers (Salat), and from our provisions to them, they give to charity.
2:3.arabic: الذين يؤمنون بالغيب ويقيمون الصلوة ومما رزقنهم ينفقون

6:50.text: Say, "I do not say to you that I possess the treasures of GOD. Nor do I know the future. Nor do I say to you that I am an angel. I simply follow what is revealed to me." Say, "Is the blind the same as the seer? Do you not reflect?"

6:59.arabic: ۞وعنده مفاتح الغيب لا يعلمها إلا هو ويعلم ما في البر والبحر وما تسقط من ورقة إلا يعلمها ولا حبة في ظلمت الأرض ولا رطب ولا يابس إلا في كتب مبين

Nor do I say to those despised by your eyes that GOD will not bestow any blessings upon them. GOD knows best what is in their innermost thoughts. (If I did this,) I would be a transgressor."
11:31.arabic: ولا أقول لكم عندي خزائن الله ولا أعلم الغيب ولا أقول إني ملك ولا أقول للذين تزدري أعينكم لن يؤتيهم الله خيرا الله أعلم بما في أنفسهم إني إذا لمن الظـلمين

49:18.arabic: إن الله يعلم غيب السموت والأرض والله بصير بما تعملون

And we sent down the iron, wherein there is strength, and many benefits for the people. All this in order for GOD to distinguish those who would support Him and His messengers, on faith. GOD is Powerful, Almighty.
