question: What does the Quran say about forgiving debt or giving time to a debtor?
answer:
The Quran's treatment of *debt* is *systematically merciful* — *prioritizing the debtor's dignity* over *the creditor's strict legal-right*. The foundational verse is [2:280]: *if the debtor is in difficulty, grant him time until he can pay; and if you forgive the debt as charity, it is better for you, if you only knew*. The Quran *establishes* a *layered-set of preferences*: *enforce-payment is the legal-right*; *grant-extension is the preferred-conduct*; *forgive-as-charity is the highest-virtue*.

**1. The foundational debt-mercy verse.**

[2:280]: "If the debtor is unable to pay, wait for a better time. If you give up the loan as a charity, it would be better for you, if you only knew." — *wa-in kāna dhū `usratin fa-naẓiratun ilā maysarah, wa-an taṣaddaqū khayrun lakum in kuntum ta`lamūn*.

The verse establishes *two layered-preferences*:

(a) *Naẓiratun ilā maysarah* — *extension-until-ease*. *If the debtor is in difficulty, grant time until the situation improves*. This is the *baseline-mercy expected of the creditor*.

(b) *Wa-an taṣaddaqū khayrun lakum* — *and if you forgive the debt as charity, it is better for you*. *Debt-forgiveness as charity* is *the higher-virtue* — *the Quran explicitly identifies it as "better"*.

The *closing-clause* — *if you only knew* — *implies* that *the spiritual-and-eschatological benefit* of *debt-forgiveness* *exceeds* the *material-loss* — *but humans typically do not perceive this*.

**2. The debt-documentation verse.**

[2:282]: "O you who believe, when you transact a loan for any period, you shall write it down. An impartial scribe shall do the writing. No scribe shall refuse to perform this service, according to GOD's teachings. He shall write, while the debtor dictates the terms..." (continues at length, the longest verse in the Quran).

The verse — *the longest single-verse in the Quran* — establishes *the elaborate-debt-documentation protocol*: *written contract, impartial scribe, debtor dictates the terms, two male witnesses (or one male and two female), no scribe-refusal, no witness-refusal, document each transaction*. The *theological-implication*: *debt is a serious matter requiring careful documentation* — *not a casual handshake*. *The documentation protects both parties* — *the creditor against denial, the debtor against false-additions*.

**3. The 'debt-forgiveness as zakat-recipient' category.**

[9:60]: "Charities shall go to the poor, the needy, the workers who collect them, the new converts, to free the slaves, to those burdened by sudden expenses, in the cause of GOD, and to the traveling alien. Such is GOD's commandment. GOD is Omniscient, Most Wise."

The verse identifies *eight categories* of *zakat-recipients*. *Al-ghārimīn* — *those burdened by sudden-expenses / debt* — is *one of the eight categories*. *The believer-community is mandated to use zakat-funds to relieve the indebted*. *Debt-relief* is *systematically built into* *the zakat-distribution structure*.

The implication: *the believer-community-treasury* — *the zakat-pool* — *includes debt-relief among its mandated-uses*. *The Submitter-community is obligated* to *systematically relieve debts of the burdened*.

**4. The 'good loan to God' verses.**

[2:245]: "Who would like to loan GOD a loan of righteousness, to have it multiplied for him manifold? GOD is the One who withholds and the One who gives, and to Him you will be returned."

[57:11]: "Who would like to loan GOD a loan of righteousness, to have it multiplied for him manifold, and end up with a generous recompense?" — *man dhā lladhī yuqriḍu llāha qarḍan ḥasanan fa-yuḍā`ifahū lahū wa-lahū ajrun karīm*.

[57:18]: "Surely, the charitable men and women, have loaned GOD a loan of righteousness. They will receive their reward multiplied manifold; they have deserved a generous recompense."

[64:17]: "If you lend GOD a loan of righteousness, He will multiply the reward for you manifold, and forgive you. GOD is Appreciative, Clement."

[73:20]: "...and lend GOD a loan of righteousness..."

The recurring *qarḍ ḥasan* — *the good loan* — vocabulary establishes *a profound theological-frame*: *every charitable-gift, every debt-forgiveness, every interest-free-loan to one in need* is *literally a loan to God*. *God will repay it manifold*.

The *theological-implication*: *the creditor who extends interest-free-loans or forgives debts* is *not losing anything*; *the creditor is making a divine-investment with multiplied-return*. *The eschatological-economy* operates by *the qarḍ-ḥasan principle*: *what you give to others in need, God repays multiplied*.

**5. The 'milk-and-honey' principle.**

The Quranic-architecture combines:

(a) *Strict-prohibition of usury (ribā)* — *no interest on loans* ([2:275]–[2:280]).

(b) *Encouragement of qarḍ-ḥasan* — *interest-free loans to those in need* ([2:245], [57:11], [57:18], [64:17], [73:20]).

(c) *Mandatory-extension to difficulty* — *if the debtor is in difficulty, grant time until ease* ([2:280]).

(d) *Preferred-forgiveness as charity* — *forgive the debt as charity, it is better* ([2:280]).

(e) *Zakat-mandate to relieve debts* — *al-ghārimīn as one of the eight zakat-categories* ([9:60]).

The *theological-architecture* is *coherent*: *loans should be interest-free*; *the lender should be flexible in repayment*; *the lender should forgive when possible*; *the community should fund debt-relief through zakat*.

**6. The 'fulfill your contracts' command.**

[5:1]: "O you who believe, you shall fulfill your covenants..." — *yā ayyuhā lladhīna āmanū awfū bi-l-`uqūd*.

The verse establishes *the foundational-contract-fulfillment command*. The verse does *not exempt* the *debtor from the obligation to repay if able* — *the debtor's duty to repay* is *categorical when capacity exists*. The *mercy-toward-the-debtor* is *the creditor's-side response* when *the debtor lacks capacity*.

**7. The pre-Islamic context.**

The *pre-Islamic Arabian debt-context*:

(a) *Heavy compound-interest (ribā)* — *the debt could rapidly multiply beyond the original principal*.

(b) *Debt-slavery* — *the unable-to-pay debtor* could be *enslaved by the creditor* or *have family-members enslaved*.

(c) *No systematic-community-relief for debt*.

(d) *Aggressive-collection practices* including *physical-detention of the debtor*.

The *Quranic-debt-ethics* — *no interest, mandatory extension, preferred forgiveness, systematic zakat-relief* — *categorically transformed* the *Arabian debt-system*. *Debt-slavery was eliminated*; *debt-relief was institutionalized*.

**8. The Khalifa framework.**

For Khalifa's framework, *debt-mercy* is *one of the operational-manifestations of iḥsān*. The Submitter-creditor's *standard-behavior* should be: (a) *lend interest-free when able*; (b) *grant extension when the debtor faces difficulty*; (c) *forgive the debt as charity when possible*; (d) *contribute to zakat-funds for debt-relief*.

The *Khalifa-translation* of [2:280] — *if you give up the loan as a charity, it would be better for you, if you only knew* — preserves the *layered-preference architecture*: *extension is the baseline; forgiveness is the higher-virtue*.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's debt-ethics is *systematically merciful*: *if the debtor is in difficulty, grant extension; if you forgive as charity, it is better* ([2:280]). *The longest verse in the Quran* ([2:282]) establishes *the elaborate debt-documentation protocol* — *protecting both creditor and debtor*. *Debt-relief is one of the eight zakat-categories* ([9:60]: *al-ghārimīn*) — *systematically built into the believer-community-treasury*. The *qarḍ-ḥasan principle* ([2:245], [57:11], [57:18], [64:17], [73:20]) — *every interest-free loan or debt-forgiveness is a loan to God, repaid manifold*. The *theological-architecture combines*: *prohibition of ribā, encouragement of qarḍ-ḥasan, mandatory extension to difficulty, preferred forgiveness as charity, zakat-mandate to relieve debts*. The *pre-Islamic Arabian debt-system* — *heavy interest, debt-slavery, no community-relief* — was *categorically transformed* by *the Quranic debt-ethics*. The *Khalifa framework* identifies *debt-mercy* as *one of the operational-manifestations of iḥsān* — *the Submitter-creditor extends, forgives, and contributes to debt-relief*.

2:280.text: If the debtor is unable to pay, wait for a better time. If you give up the loan as a charity, it would be better for you, if you only knew.
2:280.arabic: وإن كان ذو عسرة فنظرة إلى ميسرة وأن تصدقوا خير لكم إن كنتم تعلمون

2:282.text: O you who believe, when you transact a loan for any period, you shall write it down. An impartial scribe shall do the writing. No scribe shall refuse to perform this service, according to GOD's teachings. He shall write, while the debtor dictates the terms. He shall observe GOD his Lord and never cheat. If the debtor is mentally incapable, or helpless, or cannot dictate, his guardian shall dictate equitably. Two men shall serve as witnesses; if not two men, then a man and two women whose testimony is acceptable to all. Thus, if one woman becomes biased, the other will remind her. It is the obligation of the witnesses to testify when called upon to do so. Do not tire of writing the details, no matter how long, including the time of repayment. This is equitable in the sight of GOD, assures better witnessing, and eliminates any doubts you may have. Business transactions that you execute on the spot need not be recorded, but have them witnessed. No scribe or witness shall be harmed on account of his services. If you harm them, it would be wickedness on your part. You shall observe GOD, and GOD will teach you. GOD is Omniscient.
2:282.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا إذا تداينتم بدين إلى أجل مسمى فاكتبوه وليكتب بينكم كاتب بالعدل ولا يأب كاتب أن يكتب كما علمه الله فليكتب وليملل الذي عليه الحق وليتق الله ربه ولا يبخس منه شيـا فإن كان الذي عليه الحق سفيها أو ضعيفا أو لا يستطيع أن يمل هو فليملل وليه بالعدل واستشهدوا شهيدين من رجالكم فإن لم يكونا رجلين فرجل وامرأتان ممن ترضون من الشهداء أن تضل إحدىهما فتذكر إحدىهما الأخرى ولا يأب الشهداء إذا ما دعوا ولا تسـموا أن تكتبوه صغيرا أو كبيرا إلى أجله ذلكم أقسط عند الله وأقوم للشهدة وأدنى ألا ترتابوا إلا أن تكون تجرة حاضرة تديرونها بينكم فليس عليكم جناح ألا تكتبوها وأشهدوا إذا تبايعتم ولا يضار كاتب ولا شهيد وإن تفعلوا فإنه فسوق بكم واتقوا الله ويعلمكم الله والله بكل شيء عليم

5:1.text: O you who believe, you shall fulfill your covenants. Permitted for you to eat are the livestock, except those specifically prohibited herein. You shall not permit hunting throughout Hajj pilgrimage. GOD decrees whatever He wills.
5:1.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا أوفوا بالعقود أحلت لكم بهيمة الأنعم إلا ما يتلى عليكم غير محلي الصيد وأنتم حرم إن الله يحكم ما يريد

9:60.text: Charities shall go to the poor, the needy, the workers who collect them, the new converts, to free the slaves, to those burdened by sudden expenses, in the cause of GOD, and to the traveling alien. Such is GOD's commandment. GOD is Omniscient, Most Wise.
9:60.arabic: ۞إنما الصدقت للفقراء والمسكين والعملين عليها والمؤلفة قلوبهم وفي الرقاب والغرمين وفي سبيل الله وابن السبيل فريضة من الله والله عليم حكيم

57:11.text: Who would like to loan GOD a loan of righteousness, to have it multiplied for him manifold, and end up with a generous recompense?
57:11.arabic: من ذا الذي يقرض الله قرضا حسنا فيضعفه له وله أجر كريم

57:18.text: Surely, the charitable men and women, have loaned GOD a loan of goodness. They will receive their reward multiplied manifold; they have deserved a generous recompense.
57:18.arabic: إن المصدقين والمصدقت وأقرضوا الله قرضا حسنا يضعف لهم ولهم أجر كريم

64:17.text: If you lend GOD a loan of righteousness, He will multiply the reward for you manifold, and forgive you. GOD is Appreciative, Clement.
64:17.arabic: إن تقرضوا الله قرضا حسنا يضعفه لكم ويغفر لكم والله شكور حليم

73:20.text: Your Lord knows that you meditate during two-thirds of the night, or half of it, or one-third of it, and so do some of those who believed with you. GOD has designed the night and the day, and He knows that you cannot always do this. He has pardoned you. Instead, you shall read what you can of the Quran. He knows that some of you may be ill, others may be traveling in pursuit of GOD's provisions, and others may be striving in the cause of GOD. You shall read what you can of it, and observe the contact prayers (Salat), give the obligatory charity (Zakat), and lend GOD a loan of righteousness. Whatever good you send ahead on behalf of your souls, you will find it at GOD far better and generously rewarded. And implore GOD for forgiveness. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful.
73:20.arabic: ۞إن ربك يعلم أنك تقوم أدنى من ثلثي اليل ونصفه وثلثه وطائفة من الذين معك والله يقدر اليل والنهار علم أن لن تحصوه فتاب عليكم فاقرءوا ما تيسر من القرءان علم أن سيكون منكم مرضى وءاخرون يضربون في الأرض يبتغون من فضل الله وءاخرون يقتلون في سبيل الله فاقرءوا ما تيسر منه وأقيموا الصلوة وءاتوا الزكوة وأقرضوا الله قرضا حسنا وما تقدموا لأنفسكم من خير تجدوه عند الله هو خيرا وأعظم أجرا واستغفروا الله إن الله غفور رحيم

question: How does the Quran address anger and its restraint?
answer:
The Quran's *anger-management* is *one of the most-developed character-virtue teachings* in the scripture. The *suppression-of-anger* (*kazm al-ghayẓ*) is *explicitly named* among *the defining-characteristics of paradise-bound believers* ([3:134]). The Quran *combines* (a) *the prohibition of acting-on-anger* with (b) *the prescription of constructive-responses* — *pardoning, exhorting-to-good, returning-evil-with-better*. The Quranic-approach is *psychologically-realistic*: *anger is acknowledged as a natural-human emotion*; *the discipline* is *not in not-feeling-anger* but *in not-acting-destructively-on-it*.

**1. The 'suppressors of anger' verse.**

[3:134]: "who give to charity during the good times, as well as the bad times. They are suppressors of anger, and pardoners of the people. GOD loves the charitable." — *wa-l-kāẓimīna l-ghayẓa wa-l-`āfīna `ani n-nās*.

The verse — *defining the paradise-bound muttaqīn* — names *three characteristics*:

(a) *Spending in good and bad times* (the *charity-consistency virtue*).

(b) *Kāẓimīna l-ghayẓ* — *suppressors-of-anger*.

(c) *`Āfīna `ani n-nās* — *pardoners-of-the-people*.

The *theological-architecture*: *anger-suppression* and *pardoning* are *paired* as *the two-pole-virtues of anger-management*. *Suppression* is *the negative-pole* (*do not act-on-anger*); *pardoning* is *the positive-pole* (*actively-pardon the offender*).

The Arabic *kāẓim* (from root ك-ظ-م) means *to swallow, hold-back, contain*. The image: *swallowing one's rage*. The verse does *not say* the muttaqīn *do not feel anger*; it says they *swallow / contain / hold-back the anger*. *The anger is felt; the response is restrained*.

**2. The 'reciprocate evil with better' command.**

[41:34]: "Not equal is the good response and the bad response. You shall resort to the nicest possible response. Thus, the one who used to be your enemy, may become your best friend." — *idfa` bi-llatī hiya aḥsanu fa-idhā lladhī baynaka wa-baynahū `adāwatun ka-annahū waliyyun ḥamīm*.

[41:35]: "None can attain this except those who steadfastly persevere. None can attain this except those who are extremely fortunate." — *wa-mā yulaqqāhā illā lladhīna ṣabarū, wa-mā yulaqqāhā illā dhū ḥaẓẓin `aẓīm*.

The verse-pair establishes *the highest-anger-response standard*: *respond to evil with what is best*; *the enemy may become an intimate-friend*. The verse acknowledges *the difficulty*: *only the steadfastly-patient attain this*; *those who do are extremely fortunate*.

The *theological-implication*: *the constructive-anger-response* is *not natural*; it requires *discipline*. *The natural-response* is *retaliation*; *the higher-response* is *better-than-retaliation*. *The transformation of an enemy into a friend* requires *the higher-response*.

**3. The 'self-defense permitted, restraint preferred' verses.**

[42:39]–[42:43]: "When gross injustice befalls them, they stand up for their rights. Although the just requital for an injustice is an equivalent retribution, those who pardon and maintain righteousness are rewarded by GOD. He does not love the unjust. Certainly, those who stand up for their rights, when injustice befalls them, are not committing any error. The wrong ones are those who treat the people unjustly, and resort to aggression without provocation. These have incurred a painful retribution. Resorting to patience and forgiveness reflects a true strength of character."

The cluster establishes *the Quranic-anger-architecture*:

(a) *Self-defense and retaliation-up-to-equivalence is permitted* — *no sin in standing up for rights*.

(b) *Pardoning and reconciliation is rewarded by God* — *the higher-path*.

(c) *Aggression-without-provocation is forbidden* — *the lower-path*.

(d) *Patience and forgiveness reflect strength of character* — *the highest-path*.

The Quran *does not demand pacifism*; it *permits proportionate-defense*. But *it identifies the higher-virtue* as *patience-and-forgiveness*.

**4. The 'Moses-and-the-anger' narratives.**

[7:150]: "When Moses returned to his people, angry and disappointed, he said, 'What a terrible thing you have done in my absence! Could you not wait for the commandments of your Lord?' He threw down the tablets, and took hold of his brother's head, pulling him towards himself. (Aaron) said, 'Son of my mother, the people took advantage of my weakness, and almost killed me. Let not my enemies rejoice, and do not count me with the transgressing people.'"

[7:154]: "When Moses' anger subsided, he picked up the tablets, containing guidance and mercy for those who reverence their Lord."

The Moses-narrative *acknowledges* the *Quranic-realism* about *anger*: *even Moses, the prophet*, *felt and acted on anger* — *throwing down the tablets, pulling Aaron's head*. *Aaron's plea* (*the people took advantage of my weakness*) — *recognizes Moses's anger as understandable but excessive*. *When the anger subsided*, *Moses picked up the tablets* — *resuming his prophetic-role*.

The narrative-pattern: *anger is human and even prophetic-humans feel it*; *the anger eventually subsides*; *the post-anger restoration* is *the recovery of guidance*.

**5. The 'Jonah-and-the-anger' narrative.**

[21:87]: "And Zan-Noon (Jonah, 'the one with the 'N'), abandoned his mission in protest, thinking that we could not control him. He ended up imploring from the darkness (of the big fish's belly): 'There is no god other than You. Be You glorified. I have committed a gross sin.'"

The Jonah-narrative *acknowledges* the *prophetic-anger-and-its-consequence*: *Jonah was angry*, *abandoned his mission*, *was swallowed by the fish* — *recognizing his sin from the fish's belly*. *Repentance* — *no god other than You; I have committed a gross sin* — is *the recovery-pathway*.

The narrative-pattern: *anger-driven action has consequences*; *the consequences invite repentance*; *the repentance restores the relationship with God*.

**6. The 'be patient with the deniers' command.**

[16:126]: "If you punish, you shall inflict an equivalent punishment. But if you resort to patience (instead of revenge), it would be better for the patient ones." — *wa-in `āqabtum fa-`āqibū bi-mithli mā `ūqibtum bihī, wa-la-in ṣabartum la-huwa khayrun li-ṣ-ṣābirīn*.

[16:127]: "You shall resort to patience - and your patience is attainable only with GOD's help. Do not grieve over them, and do not be annoyed by their schemes." — *wa-ṣbir wa-mā ṣabruka illā bi-llāhi wa-lā taḥzan `alayhim wa-lā taku fī ḍayqin mim-mā yamkurūn*.

The verses *explicitly to the Prophet*: *if you punish, equivalent-only*; *if you are patient, it is better*; *your patience is only with God's help*. The *practical-prophetic-discipline*: *patience as the default-response to provocation*; *equivalence as the maximum-response when necessary*.

**7. The 'forgive when angered' verse.**

[42:37]: "They avoid gross sins and vice, and when angered they forgive." — *wa-lladhīna yajtanibūna kabā'ira l-ithmi wa-l-fawāḥisha wa-idhā mā ghaḍibū hum yaghfirūn*.

The verse — *one of the most-economical anger-statements* in the Quran — pairs *avoidance-of-major-sins* with *forgiveness-when-angered*. *The two virtues* are *coupled*: *the believer avoids both committing-major-sins and responding-vengefully-to-others'-sins*.

The verb *ghaḍiba* (to be angered) is *temporal*: *when angered* (not *if angered*) — *acknowledging the anger as a recurring-experience*. The *believer-response* is *to forgive in the moment of anger* — *not letting the anger drive the action*.

**8. The 'do not let anger sway your justice' command.**

[42:43]: "Resorting to patience and forgiveness reflects a true strength of character." — *wa-la-man ṣabara wa-ghafara inna dhālika la-min `azmi l-umūr*.

The verse identifies *patience and forgiveness* as *`azm al-umūr* — *the resolute-determinations of high-affairs*. *The strong character* is *the one who restrains anger* and *forgives* — *not* the *one who unleashes-rage*.

**9. The Moses-by-the-rock anger model.**

[20:86] (Moses to his people after his return): "He returned to his people, angry and disappointed, saying, 'O my people, did your Lord not promise you a good promise? Could you not wait? Did you want to incur wrath from your Lord? Is this why you broke your agreement with me?'"

The verse *narrates* Moses's *anger* in his *speech* — *not in destructive-action*. *Anger expressed in verbal-confrontation* is *acceptable*; *anger expressed in physical-destruction* (the *thrown tablets, pulled-head* of [7:150]) *is not the ideal*. The Quranic-realism preserves *the distinction* between *expressed-anger-in-rebuke* (acceptable) and *acted-anger-in-violence* (excessive).

**10. The cultivation of anger-restraint.**

The Quran *identifies* the *cultivation-methods* for *anger-restraint*:

(a) *Remembrance of paradise* — *the muttaqīn-list at [3:133]–[3:134]*; *anger-suppression is one of the paradise-entry virtues*.

(b) *Pardoning practice* — *active-pardoning is paired with anger-suppression* ([3:134]).

(c) *Return-evil-with-better* — *the highest standard* ([41:34]).

(d) *Patience-with-God's-help* — *not self-generated discipline* ([16:127]).

(e) *Equivalence-not-exceeded* — *if you must retaliate, equivalent-only* ([16:126], [42:40]).

(f) *Recognition of patience-as-strength* — *patience and forgiveness reflect true strength of character* ([42:43]).

(g) *Prayer in the moment of anger* — *implicit in the Quran's general prayer-prescription*.

(h) *Imitation of prophetic-models* — *Moses's anger-subsidence and tablet-recovery; Jonah's repentance from the fish's belly*.

**11. The Khalifa framework.**

For Khalifa's framework, *anger-restraint* is *one of the practical-character-virtues* that *distinguishes* the *Submitter*. The Khalifa-translation consistently renders *kāẓim al-ghayẓ* as *suppressors of anger* — preserving the *active-discipline character* of the virtue. The *Submitter-discipline*: *acknowledge the anger; do not let it drive the action; respond with patience, forgiveness, or at-most-equivalence*.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's *anger-management* is *psychologically-realistic* and *practically-detailed*. *Suppressors of anger* (*kāẓimīna l-ghayẓ*) and *pardoners of the people* are *defining-characteristics of the paradise-bound muttaqīn* ([3:134]). *Respond to evil with what is best — the enemy may become an intimate-friend* ([41:34]); *only the steadfastly-patient attain this* ([41:35]). *Self-defense and retaliation-up-to-equivalence is permitted; pardoning and reconciliation is rewarded* ([42:39]–[42:43]). *Moses's anger* at the Golden Calf ([7:150]); *the anger subsided and Moses picked up the tablets* ([7:154]). *Jonah's anger-driven mission-abandonment and his repentance from the fish's belly* ([21:87]). *If you punish, equivalent-only; if you are patient, it is better; your patience is only with God's help* ([16:126]–[16:127]). *When angered they forgive* ([42:37]). *Patience and forgiveness reflect true strength of character* ([42:43]). The *cultivation-methods*: *paradise-remembrance, pardoning-practice, return-evil-with-better, patience-with-God's-help, equivalence-not-exceeded, recognition-of-patience-as-strength, prayer-in-the-moment, imitation-of-prophetic-models*. The *Khalifa framework* identifies *anger-restraint* as *a practical-character-virtue* that *distinguishes the Submitter* — *acknowledge the anger; do not let it drive the action; respond with patience, forgiveness, or at-most-equivalence*.

3:134.text: who give to charity during the good times, as well as the bad times. They are suppressors of anger, and pardoners of the people. GOD loves the charitable.
3:134.arabic: الذين ينفقون في السراء والضراء والكظمين الغيظ والعافين عن الناس والله يحب المحسنين

7:150.text: When Moses returned to his people, angry and disappointed, he said, "What a terrible thing you have done in my absence! Could you not wait for the commandments of your Lord?" He threw down the tablets, and took hold of his brother's head, pulling him towards himself. (Aaron) said, "Son of my mother, the people took advantage of my weakness, and almost killed me. Let not my enemies rejoice, and do not count me with the transgressing people."
7:150.arabic: ولما رجع موسى إلى قومه غضبن أسفا قال بئسما خلفتموني من بعدي أعجلتم أمر ربكم وألقى الألواح وأخذ برأس أخيه يجره إليه قال ابن أم إن القوم استضعفوني وكادوا يقتلونني فلا تشمت بي الأعداء ولا تجعلني مع القوم الظـلمين

7:154.text: When Moses' anger subsided, he picked up the tablets, containing guidance and mercy for those who reverence their Lord.
7:154.arabic: ولما سكت عن موسى الغضب أخذ الألواح وفي نسختها هدى ورحمة للذين هم لربهم يرهبون

20:86.text: Moses returned to his people, angry and disappointed, saying, "O my people, did your Lord not promise you a good promise? Could you not wait? Did you want to incur wrath from your Lord? Is this why you broke your agreement with me?"
20:86.arabic: فرجع موسى إلى قومه غضبن أسفا قال يقوم ألم يعدكم ربكم وعدا حسنا أفطال عليكم العهد أم أردتم أن يحل عليكم غضب من ربكم فأخلفتم موعدي

21:87.text: And Zan-Noon (Jonah, "the one with an `N' in his name"), abandoned his mission in protest, thinking that we could not control him. He ended up imploring from the darkness (of the big fish's belly): "There is no god other than You. Be You glorified. I have committed a gross sin."
21:87.arabic: وذا النون إذ ذهب مغضبا فظن أن لن نقدر عليه فنادى في الظلمت أن لا إله إلا أنت سبحنك إني كنت من الظـلمين

42:37.text: They avoid gross sins and vice, and when angered they forgive.
42:37.arabic: والذين يجتنبون كبـئر الإثم والفوحش وإذا ما غضبوا هم يغفرون

42:43.text: Resorting to patience and forgiveness reflects a true strength of character.
42:43.arabic: ولمن صبر وغفر إن ذلك لمن عزم الأمور

16:126.text: And if you punish, you shall inflict an equivalent punishment. But if you resort to patience (instead of revenge), it would be better for the patient ones.
16:126.arabic: وإن عاقبتم فعاقبوا بمثل ما عوقبتم به ولئن صبرتم لهو خير للصـبرين

16:127.text: You shall resort to patience - and your patience is attainable only with GOD's help. Do not grieve over them, and do not be annoyed by their schemes.
16:127.arabic: واصبر وما صبرك إلا بالله ولا تحزن عليهم ولا تك في ضيق مما يمكرون

41:34.text: Not equal is the good response and the bad response. You shall resort to the nicest possible response. Thus, the one who used to be your enemy, may become your best friend.
41:34.arabic: ولا تستوي الحسنة ولا السيئة ادفع بالتي هي أحسن فإذا الذي بينك وبينه عدوة كأنه ولي حميم

41:35.text: None can attain this except those who steadfastly persevere. None can attain this except those who are extremely fortunate.
41:35.arabic: وما يلقىها إلا الذين صبروا وما يلقىها إلا ذو حظ عظيم

question: What does the Quran say about backbiting (ghibah)?
answer:
The Quran's *prohibition of ghībah* (*backbiting, slander, malicious gossip*) is *unusually-vivid* — *comparing the act to eating the flesh of one's dead brother*. The foundational verse is [49:12]: *would any of you like to eat the flesh of his dead brother? You would loathe it*. The Quran *categorically prohibits* (a) *backbiting* (truth-based malicious-talk about an absent person), (b) *slander* (false malicious-talk), (c) *suspicion* (un-evidenced negative-judgment), and (d) *spying* (private-information-acquisition for malicious-purposes).

**1. The foundational backbiting verse.**

[49:12]: "O you who believe, you shall avoid any suspicion, for even a little bit of suspicion is sinful. You shall not spy on one another, nor shall you backbite one another; this is as abominable as eating the flesh of your dead brother. You certainly abhor this. You shall observe GOD. GOD is Redeemer, Most Merciful." — *wa-lā yaghtab ba`ḍukum ba`ḍā, a-yuḥibbu aḥadukum an ya'kula laḥma akhīhi maytan fa-karihtumūh*.

The verse establishes *four prohibitions*:

(a) *Avoid suspicion* (*ijtanibū kathīran mina ẓ-ẓann*) — *unevidenced negative-judgment*. *Even a little bit of suspicion is sinful*.

(b) *Do not spy* (*wa-lā tajassasū*) — *private-information acquisition for malicious-purposes*.

(c) *Do not backbite* (*wa-lā yaghtab ba`ḍukum ba`ḍā*) — *truth-based malicious-talk about an absent person*.

(d) *The vivid-analogy* — *backbiting is like eating the flesh of your dead brother*. *You loathe this; you should loathe backbiting equally*.

The *theological-imagery* at [49:12] is *among the most-vivid* in the Quran for *describing a sin*. *Cannibalism of a dead brother* is *the analogical-equivalent* of *backbiting*. The *intent of the imagery*: *to make the act morally-repugnant to the believer-imagination*.

**2. The 'do not mock one another' verse.**

[49:11]: "O you who believe, no people shall ridicule other people, for they may be better than they. Nor shall any women ridicule other women, for they may be better than they. Nor shall you mock one another, or make fun of your names. Evil indeed is the reversion to wickedness after attaining faith. Anyone who does not repent after this, these are the transgressors."

The verse — *immediately preceding the backbiting verse* — extends the *prohibition* to *mockery, ridicule, name-calling*. The *theological-frame*: *the mocker may be morally-inferior to the mocked*; *the mocker does not know the divine-evaluation of the other*. *Mockery presumes superiority that one does not have*.

**3. The 'humza-and-lumza' surah.**

[104:1]: "Woe to every backbiter, slanderer."

[104:2]: "He hoards money and counts it."

[104:3]: "As if his money will make him immortal."

Surah *Al-Humazah* — *one of the shortest surahs* — *dedicates* *the entire-surah* to *condemnation of the backbiter-slanderer*. The opening *waylun li-kulli humazatin lumazah* — *woe to every backbiter, slanderer* — *categorically condemns* the *type-of-person* who *engages in this behavior*.

The surah's *theological-architecture*: *the backbiter-slanderer* is *characterized by money-hoarding* — *the materialistic-orientation* and *the malicious-tongue* are *paired-vices*. *The materialistic-orientation* — *thinking money brings immortality* — *produces* *the social-malice* that *manifests in backbiting*.

The surah's *consequence* — *the backbiter-slanderer is thrown into Hutamah, the crushing fire* ([104:4]–[104:9]) — establishes *the eschatological-severity* of *backbiting*.

**4. The 'wicked-tongue-slanderer' verses.**

[68:10]: "Do not obey every lowly swearer."

[68:11]: "A slanderer, a backbiter."

[68:12]: "Forbidder of charity, a transgressor, a sinner."

[68:13]: "Unappreciative, and greedy."

The cluster — *describing the negative-character-type* — identifies *the slanderer-backbiter* as *one of the defining-vices* of *the unrighteous-personality*. The *associated-vices*: *lowly-swearing, forbidding-charity, transgression, sinfulness, unappreciativeness, greed*. The *Quranic-personality-profile* of *the backbiter* — *not* an *isolated-vice* but *a constellation of related-vices*.

**5. The 'do not pursue what you do not know' verse.**

[17:36]: "You shall not accept any information, unless you verify it for yourself. I have given you the hearing, the eyesight, and the brain, and you are responsible for using them." — *wa-lā taqfu mā laysa laka bihī `ilm*.

The verse — *the verification-mandate* — *categorically prohibits* *acting-on or repeating un-verified information*. *Backbiting* often *involves repeating un-verified information*; *the verification-mandate* applies *directly*. *Even if* the *information is true*, *the un-verified-repetition* is *prohibited*.

The *theological-frame*: *God has given hearing, sight, and brain*; *the believer is responsible for using them* — *not for acting-on second-hand un-verified information*.

**6. The 'one-who-defames-believers' verse.**

[33:58]: "Those who persecute the believing men and the believing women, who did not do anything wrong, have committed not only a falsehood, but also a gross sin." — *wa-lladhīna yu'dhūna l-mu'minīna wa-l-mu'mināti bi-ghayri mā ktasabū fa-qadi ḥtamalū buhtānan wa-ithman mubīnā*.

The verse condemns *false-defamation* (*buhtān*) of *innocent believers* as *not just a falsehood but a gross sin*. The Arabic *buhtān* — *a stunning lie* — is *more severe* than *ordinary ghībah*; *it is slander against the innocent*.

**7. The categorization of malicious-speech-sins.**

The classical-Islamic tradition distinguishes *several categories* of *malicious-speech*:

(a) *Ghībah* — *speaking the truth about an absent person in a way they would dislike*. *Prohibited even when true* ([49:12]).

(b) *Buhtān* — *false-attribution about an absent person*. *Doubly-prohibited — both the falsehood and the malice* ([33:58]).

(c) *Namīmah* — *carrying tales between people to incite discord*. *Prohibited under the general "do not spy" injunction*.

(d) *Tahmis / Lamz* — *mockery, name-calling, gesture-mocking* ([49:11], [104:1]).

(e) *Iftirā'* — *fabrication, especially of religious-doctrine*. *Prohibited under the broader "do not lie against God" injunctions*.

The Quranic-vocabulary *distinguishes* these categories; the *prohibitions* are *layered* — *the more severe the malice, the more severe the consequence*.

**8. The 'exceptions' question.**

The classical-Islamic tradition recognizes *limited exceptions* to *the ghībah-prohibition*:

(a) *Warning of harm* — *if someone is about to be defrauded by a known-fraudster, warning them is permitted*.

(b) *Seeking judicial-redress* — *describing a wrong-done-to-oneself before a judge*.

(c) *Consulting in legitimate matters* — *e.g., describing a potential-marriage-partner's character to a marriage-arranger*.

(d) *Open-sinners' open-sins* — *describing the publicly-known sins of one who openly-sins*.

(e) *Correcting public-doctrine* — *naming a teacher of heresy in order to refute the doctrine*.

The Quranic-text *itself* does *not enumerate* these exceptions; they are *derived from the principle* that *the prohibition's purpose* is *preventing-malice*; *legitimate-purposes* that *coincide with the speech* are *not the prohibited-category*.

**9. The cultivation of speech-discipline.**

The Quran *identifies* the *cultivation-methods* for *speech-discipline*:

(a) *Verification before speaking* ([17:36]).

(b) *Imagining the cannibalism-analogy* ([49:12]) — *making the act morally-repugnant to oneself*.

(c) *Recognizing the speaker-may-be-inferior-to-the-spoken-about* ([49:11]).

(d) *Awareness of the recording-angels* ([50:18]: *not an utterance does he utter without an alert witness*).

(e) *Speaking-only-good or remaining-silent* (the *general-discipline of qawl ḥasan*).

(f) *Avoiding-the-six-vice constellation* — *lowly-swearing, slander, backbiting, charity-forbidding, transgression, greed* ([68:10]–[68:13]).

**10. The Khalifa framework.**

For Khalifa's framework, *speech-discipline* is *one of the practical-tests of authentic-submission*. The Submitter who *prays, fasts, and gives zakat* while *backbiting* is *failing the comprehensive-test*. The Khalifa-translation of [49:12] preserves the *vivid-cannibalism-imagery* — *this is as abominable as eating the flesh of your dead brother* — *foregrounding the moral-repugnance* the verse intends to evoke.

**Synthesis.**

The Quran's *prohibition of ghībah* (*backbiting, slander, malicious gossip*) is *unusually-vivid*: *would any of you like to eat the flesh of his dead brother? You would loathe it* ([49:12]). *Four prohibitions* at [49:12]: *suspicion, spying, backbiting, and the cannibalism-analogy*. *Mockery, ridicule, name-calling* are *prohibited* at [49:11] — *the mocker may be morally-inferior to the mocked*. *Surah Al-Humazah* ([104:1]–[104:3]) — *the entire-surah condemns the backbiter-slanderer* — paired with *money-hoarding* as *coupled-vices*. *Slanderer-backbiter* in *the negative-character-profile* at [68:10]–[68:13]. *Verification-before-acting-on-information* at [17:36]. *False-defamation (buhtān) of innocent-believers* is *not just a falsehood but a gross sin* ([33:58]). *Five categories of malicious-speech*: *ghībah, buhtān, namīmah, tahmis/lamz, iftirā'*. *Limited exceptions* from *classical-tradition*: *warning-of-harm, judicial-redress, consultation-in-legitimate-matters, open-sinners'-open-sins, public-doctrine-correction*. The *cultivation-methods*: *verification-before-speaking, cannibalism-analogy-imagery, recognition-of-speaker-inferiority, awareness-of-recording-angels, speak-good-or-be-silent, avoiding-the-six-vice-constellation*. The *Khalifa framework* identifies *speech-discipline* as *operational-test of authentic-submission* — *no Submitter can pray and backbite*.

49:11.text: O you who believe, no people shall ridicule other people, for they may be better than they. Nor shall any women ridicule other women, for they may be better than they. Nor shall you mock one another, or make fun of your names. Evil indeed is the reversion to wickedness after attaining faith. Anyone who does not repent after this, these are the transgressors.

GOD is Redeemer, Most Merciful.
